name: FILLET
description: The FILLET function has 2 calculation functions: The first determines the dimensions of an arc that fillets or blends two 3-D vectors together The second determines the dimensions of two 2D arcs that blends 2 points with directions
params: Location of the input data in TABLE memory.
Location of the output data in TABLE memory.

Used to calculate the arc between 2 straight lines in
  3D.

Calculates a pair of arcs between 2 points with
  directions.

Calculate the “dot product” and mid vector from 2 x 3D
  vector.
Fillet 2D line and 2D arc with 1 arc.
examples:
DEFPOS(150, 0, 0)
TRIGGER TABLE(100, -150, 0, 0)
TABLE(103, 50, 200, 100, 70)
FILLET(100, 200, 0)
xin = TABLE(200): yin = TABLE(201): zin = TABLE(202)
MOVE(xin, yin, zin)
xend = TABLE(203): yend = TABLE(204): zend = TABLE(205)
xmid = TABLE(206): ymid = TABLE(207): zmid = TABLE(208)
MSPHERICAL(xend, yend, zend, xmid, ymid, zmid, 0)
xout = TABLE(214): yout = TABLE(215): zout = TABLE(216)
MOVE(xout, yout, zout)
fillet_ang = TABLE(217): fillet_len = TABLE(218)
PRINT fillet_ang, fillet_len

name: FLAG
description: The FLAG command is used to set and read a bank of 24 flag bits. The FLAG command is provided to aid compatibility with earlier controllers and is not recommended for new programs.
params: With one parameter it returns the state of the flag
The flag number is a value from 0..31.

The state to set the given flag to. 
ON

  or 
OFF
.
examples:
IF FLAG(21) and VR(100)=123 THEN
FLAG(21,OFF)
ELSE IF NOT FLAG(21) and VR(100)<>123 THEN


  FLAG(21,ON)
ENDIF

name: FLAGS
description: Read or Set the 32bit FLAGS as a block. The FLAGS command is provided to aid compatibility with earlier controllers and is not recommended for new programs.
params: no parameters = returns the status of all flag bits

The decimal equivalent of the bit pattern to set the
  flags to
examples:
FLAGS(146)’ 2 + 16 + 128
IF (FLAGS and 8) <>0 then GOSUB somewhere
IF (FLAGS and 8) <>0 then GOSUB somewhere

name: FLASHTABLE
description: Function to copy TABLE data in RAM to and from the permanent FLASH memory. The flash memory is split into 32 pages numbered from 0, and each page can store 16000 table elements. The table below shows the first few table pages and how they map to table elements: Table page 0 1 2 3 Table index range 0 to 15999 16000 to 31999 32000 to 47999 48000 to 63999 Table pages are stored in flash pages, of which there are 32 (indexed 0 to 31), so flash pages can be used to store different table pages for easy restoration, such as to define multiple behaviours. If FLASHTABLE is being used, then you cannot use FLASHVR (-1)
params: FLASH page has been used (when function set to 3).
FLASH page hasn’t been used (when function set to 3).

Write a page of 
TABLE
 data
  into flash memory, always returning TRUE.

Read a page of flash memory into 
TABLE

  data, always returning TRUE.

Return TRUE if the flash page is in use (there is TABLE
  data flashed already), otherwise FALSE.
Erase flash page, always returning TRUE.

The index number (0 ... 31) of a 16000 values page of
  Flash EPROM where the table data is to be stored to or retrieved from.

The index number (0 ... 
INT
(
TSIZE
 / 16000)) of the
  page in table memory where the data is to be copied from or restored to.
  (Only used with functions 1 and 2).

name: FLASHVR
description: Copies the specified VR index to permanent Flash memory, a negative index signifying a special action (see table below). If FLASHVR(-1) is being used then you cannot use FLASHTABLE This command should only be used on MC2XX and MC3XX, which do not store data in flash memory automatically. Other controllers automatically store VR in flash memory, and FLASHTABLE should be used to backup TABLE data.
params:
The index of the VR array element to write to flash
  memory.

Stores the entire 
TABLE
 to the
  Flash EPROM and use it to replace the RAM table data on power-up.
Stop using the EPROM copy of table during power-up.

Force all changed 
VR
’s to be
  committed to Flash EPROM (non-battery backed controllers only)
examples: FLASHVR(-1)

name: FLASH_DATA
description: FLASH_DATA controls whether VR or TABLE data is automatically backed up to flash memory. The default setting (0) will use VR memory as the source for backup. However, by changing this parameter to 1 within MC_CONFIG will cause TABLE data as the source for backup. Please note that regardless of which data source is selected, only the first 4096 elements will be available for automatic backup. MC664 would use the first 196608 TABLE and all the VR points as a backup (flash memory) by default. When FLASH_DATA=1 all the TABLE and VR points are backed up in flash. MC508, Flex-6 Nano and MC6N would only backup VR points by default. When FLASHTABLE=1, all VR and the first 64000 TABLE points would be backed up in flash.
examples:
FLASH_DATA = 0 'Select VR memory for backup
FLASH_DATA = 1 'Select TABLE memory for backup
FLASH_DATA = 0 'Select VR memory for backup
FLASH_DATA = 1 'Select TABLE memory for backup

name: FLASH_LOG
description: Records up to 8192 of the most recent controller system events. Most controllers support 4096 messages but some record 2048 or 8192 events. Each event is recorded with a timestamp of the current RTC value and the time since power-up of the controller. Each event is also tagged with a checksum for verification. When executing the command, either in the terminal or a Trio BASIC program, it will print the recorded event messages to the terminal. Note : Before displaying a log entry the checksum for the log is validated, if it is invalid then the message '<INVALID LOG ENTRY>' will be displayed instead.
params:
Displays all flash log entries starting from line
  number 'start'.

Special case, erases log from flash memory (second
  parameter is not needed).

Special case, will display the most recent ‘n’ entries
  where ‘n’ is the second parameter.

Displays all flash log entries starting from line
  number 'start' until line number 'finish'.

name: FLASH_STATUS
description: This read-only parameter returns the status of the flash memory. The Flash memory can be busy when programs have been edited or new programs downloaded. Flash re-programming is done as a background process and large files take some time to store. On Flex-7, flash read/write time is fixed regardless of the number of VRs written, unlike other controllers where it increases with data size.
params:
1 = flash busy (data may be lost if the controller is
  powered off)


  0 = flash idle (data saved, so safe to power off the controller)

name: FLASH_TABLE_FIRST
description: FLASH_VR_FIRST, FLASH_VR_LAST, FLASH_TABLE_FIRST and FLASH_TABLE_LAST are system parameters that allow user configuration of the range of VR/TABLE locations that are preserved in flash memory. VR and TABLE locations outside the defined range will not be preserved when the power is removed. Requires firmware V2.0313 and later.
examples:
FLASH_TABLE_FIRST = 10000


FLASH_TABLE_LAST = 39999

name: FLASH_TABLE_LAST
description: FLASH_VR_FIRST, FLASH_VR_LAST, FLASH_TABLE_FIRST and FLASH_TABLE_LAST are system parameters that allow user configuration of the range of VR/TABLE locations that are preserved in flash memory. VR and TABLE locations outside the defined range will not be preserved when the power is removed. Requires firmware V2.0313 and later.
examples:
FLASH_TABLE_FIRST = 0


FLASH_TABLE_LAST = 31999

name: FLASH_VR_FIRST
description: FLASH_VR_FIRST, FLASH_VR_LAST, FLASH_TABLE_FIRST and FLASH_TABLE_LAST are system parameters that allow user configuration of the range of VR/TABLE locations that are preserved in flash memory. VR and TABLE locations outside the defined range will not be preserved when the power is removed. Requires firmware V2.0313 and later.
examples:
FLASH_VR_FIRST = 2000


FLASH_VR_LAST = 4095

name: FLASH_VR_LAST
description: FLASH_VR_FIRST, FLASH_VR_LAST, FLASH_TABLE_FIRST and FLASH_TABLE_LAST are system parameters that allow user configuration of the range of VR/TABLE locations that are preserved in flash memory. VR and TABLE locations outside the defined range will not be preserved when the power is removed. Requires firmware V2.0313 and later.
examples:
FLASH_VR_FIRST = 0


FLASH_VR_LAST = 499

name: FMOD
description: Returns the floating point modulus of an expression. The FMOD operation finds the remainder after division of one number by another (called the modulus of the operation). Given two numbers, a and b, a FMOD b is the remainder of the Euclidean division of a by b.
params:
Any valid TrioBASIC expression used as the value to apply
  the modulus to.
Any valid TrioBASIC expression used as the modulus

name: FOR..TO.. STEP..NEXT
syntax:
FOR variable = start TO end [STEP increment]


  commands


NEXT variable
description: A FOR..NEXT loop is used to execute a block of code a specific number of times. On entering this loop, the control variable is initialised to the value of ‘start’ and the block of commands is then executed. Upon reaching the NEXT command the control variable is incremented by the specified ‘increment’, and if its value has not gone beyond the ‘end’ parameter, then the block of commands is executed again, otherwise execution drops out of the FOR..NEXT loop. FOR..NEXT loops can be nested up to 8 deep in each program.
params: Trio BASIC statements that you wish to execute

A valid Trio BASIC variable. Either a global 
VR
 variable, or a local variable may be used.
The initial value for the variable
The final value for the variable

The value that the variable is incremented by, which may
  be positive or negative
examples:
FOR op_num = 10 TO 18
OP(op_num, ON)
NEXT op_num
FOR dist = 5 TO -5 STEP -0.25
MOVEABS(dist)
WAIT IDLE
GOSUB pick_up
NEXT dist
FOR x = 1 TO 8
FOR y = 1 TO 6
MOVEABS(x * 100, y * 100)
WAIT IDLE
GOSUB operation
IF MOTIONERROR THEN
EXIT_LOOP
ENDIF
NEXT y
NEXT x

FOR dist = 5 TO -5 STEP -0.25
MOVEABS(dist)
WAIT IDLE
GOSUB pick_up
NEXT dist

FOR x = 1 TO 8
FOR y = 1 TO 6
MOVEABS(x * 100, y * 100)
WAIT IDLE
GOSUB operation
IF MOTIONERROR THEN
EXIT_LOOP
ENDIF
NEXT y
NEXT x

name: FORCE_ACCEL
description: This parameter sets the acceleration rate for a motion command that supports the advanced speed control. The VP_SPEED will accelerate at the rate given by FORCE_ACCEL. With VP_MODE = 5 and VP_MODE = 6 with VP_OPTIONS.1 = 1, the forced demands (those loaded with the move) are bound to the axis demands such that the axis demands are primed with the forced demands of a move when it becomes active. This binding allows the axis demands to be adjusted, and the changes made influence the current move’s profiling. FORCE_ACCEL is also used to provide the acceleration demand to MOVEADD.
examples:
FORWARD
WA(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
FORCE_ACCEL = 2000
FORCE_DECEL = 2000
FORCE_JERK = 50000
MOVEADD(40)

FORWARD
WA(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
FORCE_ACCEL = 2000
FORCE_DECEL = 2000
FORCE_JERK = 50000
MOVEADD(40)

name: FORCE_DECEL
description: This parameter sets the deceleration rate for a motion command that supports the advanced speed control. The VP_SPEED will decelerate at the rate given by FORCE_DECEL. With VP_MODE = 5 and VP_MODE = 6 with VP_OPTIONS.1 = 1, the forced demands (those loaded with the move) are bound to the axis demands such that the axis demands are primed with the forced demands of a move when it becomes active. This binding allows the axis demands to be adjusted, and the changes made influence the current move’s profiling. FORCE_DECEL is also used to provide the deceleration demand to MOVEADD.
examples:
FORWARD
WA(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
FORCE_ACCEL = 2000
FORCE_DECEL = 2000
FORCE_JERK = 50000
MOVEADD(40)

FORWARD
WA(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
FORCE_ACCEL = 2000
FORCE_DECEL = 2000
FORCE_JERK = 50000
MOVEADD(40)

name: FORCE_DWELL
description: FORCE_DWELL can be applied to SP type moves so that the programmed dwell time will occur ahead of the next move in the move buffer. This axis parameter sets a dwell time in milliseconds to take place at the end of the next SP move. After setting a dwell for a move, it is necessary to set FORCE_DWELL to zero before loading subsequent moves that do not require a dwell.
examples:
SPEED = 100


FORCE_SPEED = 100


ENDMOVE_SPEED = 0
MOVESP(distance1)
MOVESP(distance2)
FORCE_DWELL = 25
MOVESP(distance3)
FORCE_DWELL = 0
MOVESP(distance4)

name: FORCE_JERK
description: This parameter sets the JERK for a motion command that supports the advanced speed control. The VP_SPEED will have a rate of change of acceleration/deceleration that is controlled by the FORCE_JERK value. With VP_MODE = 5 and VP_MODE = 6 with VP_OPTIONS.1 = 1, the forced demands (those loaded with the move) are bound to the axis demands such that the axis demands are primed with the forced demands of a move when it becomes active. This binding allows the axis demands to be adjusted, and the changes made influence the current move’s profiling. FORCE_JERK is also used to provide the jerk demand to MOVEADD.
examples:
FORWARD
WA(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
FORCE_ACCEL = 2000
FORCE_DECEL = 2000
FORCE_JERK = 50000
MOVEADD(40)

FORWARD
WA(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
FORCE_ACCEL = 2000
FORCE_DECEL = 2000
FORCE_JERK = 50000
MOVEADD(40)

name: FORCE_RAMP
description: FORCE_RAMP programs a change to the FORCE_SPEED over the length of the move and can be applied to advanced speed control moves (those suffixed with “SP”, like MOVESP, MOVEABSSP, MOVECIRCSP etc.). The ramp is defined by the acceleration required to change from one speed to another over the length of the move with an increasing speed having a positive value and a decreasing speed a negative value. This is calculated by determining the duration of the move (from the move’s distance divided by the average speed (the sum of the initial and final speeds divided by 2)), and the acceleration required is determined from the speed change over the move divided by the duration just calculated. After setting a ramp for a move, it is necessary to set FORCE_RAMP to zero before loading subsequent moves that do not require a ramp. Since the way that this works is by constantly adjusting the move’s FORCE_SPEED value, this will result in many small changes in speed, and if using a VP_MODE that uses JERK, this will result in repeated application of jerk all along the length of the move. If this is a problem, then the same value that was assigned to FORCE_RAMP can be assigned to FORCE_ACCEL if the ramp is increasing in speed or FORCE_DECEL if the ramp is decreasing in speed, and the ENDMOVE_SPEED setting to the speed required, and this will result in a single application of jerk to start the ramp and a single application of jerk to end the ramp.
examples:
start_sp = 100
end_sp = 75
dist = 1
t = dist * 2 / (start_sp + end_sp)
ramp = (end_sp - start_sp) / t
FORCE_SPEED = start_sp
ENDMOVE_SPEED = end_sp
FORCE_RAMP = ramp
MOVESP(dist)

name: FORCE_SPEED
description: This parameter sets the main speed for a motion command that supports the advanced speed control (commands ending in SP). The VP_SPEED will accelerate or decelerate so that the profile is run at FORCE_SPEED FORCE_SPEED is loaded into the buffer at the same time as the move so you can set different speeds for subsequent moves. With VP_MODE = 5 and VP_MODE = 6 with VP_OPTIONS.1 = 1, the forced demands (those loaded with the move) are bound to the axis demands such that the axis demands are primed with the forced demands of a move when it becomes active. This binding allows the axis demands to be adjusted, and the changes made influence the current move’s profiling. FORCE_SPEED is also used to provide the speed demand to MOVEADD.
examples:
SPEED = 15
MOVE(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 30
ENDMOVE_SPEED = -1 'Disable ENDMOVE_SPEED
MOVESP(20)
MOVE(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 100
ENDMOVE_SPEED = 50
MOVESP(100, 0)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
MOVECIRCSP(100, 100, 100, 0, 1)
FORCE_SPEED = 100
ENDMOVE_SPEED = 100
MOVESP(0, 100)

SPEED = 15
MOVE(100)
FORCE_SPEED = 30
ENDMOVE_SPEED = -1 'Disable ENDMOVE_SPEED
MOVESP(20)
MOVE(100)

FORCE_SPEED = 100
ENDMOVE_SPEED = 50
MOVESP(100, 0)
FORCE_SPEED = 50
MOVECIRCSP(100, 100, 100, 0, 1)
FORCE_SPEED = 100
ENDMOVE_SPEED = 100
MOVESP(0, 100)

name: FORWARD
description: Sets continuous forward movement. The axis accelerates at the programmed ACCEL rate and continues moving at the SPEED value until either a CANCEL or RAPIDSTOP command are encountered. It then decelerates to a stop at the programmed DECEL rate. If the axis reaches either the forward limit switch or forward soft limit, the FORWARD will be cancelled and the axis will decelerate to a stop.
examples:
FORWARD
'wait for stop signal
WAIT UNTIL IN(12)=ON
CANCEL
WAIT IDLE
BASE(3)
FWD_IN=7   'limit switch connected to input 7
FORWARD
WAIT IDLE  'wait for motion to stop on the switch
MOVE(-25.0)
WAIT IDLE
BASE(4)
ATYPE=0       'Set axis 4 to virtual axis
REP_OPTION=1
SERVO=ON
FORWARD       'starts line shaft
BASE(0)
CONNECT(-1,4) 'Connects base 0 to virtual axis in reverse
WHILE IN(2)=ON
BASE(1)
'Links axis 1 to the shaft in reverse direction
MOVELINK(-4000,2000,0,0,4,2,1000)
WAIT IDLE
WEND
RAPIDSTOP

FORWARD
'wait for stop signal
WAIT UNTIL IN(12)=ON
CANCEL
WAIT IDLE

BASE(3)
FWD_IN=7   'limit switch connected to input 7
FORWARD
WAIT IDLE  'wait for motion to stop on the switch
MOVE(-25.0)
WAIT IDLE

BASE(4)
ATYPE=0       'Set axis 4 to virtual axis
REP_OPTION=1
SERVO=ON
FORWARD       'starts line shaft
BASE(0)
CONNECT(-1,4) 'Connects base 0 to virtual axis in reverse
WHILE IN(2)=ON
BASE(1)
'Links axis 1 to the shaft in reverse direction
MOVELINK(-4000,2000,0,0,4,2,1000)
WAIT IDLE
WEND
RAPIDSTOP

name: FPGA_PROGRAM
description: This function allows you to select between the different FPGA programs that are available on controllers that support FPGA re-programming. Rather than using this command we recommend using the tool in Motion Perfect to select the FPGA variant.
params:
Displays FPGA images stored in local controller flash
  memory

The program number to load, see table below or check FPGA_PROGRAM(-1)
  to see available options.
HW_PSWITCH only available on first 2 axes
examples:
>>FPGA_PROGRAM(-1)


0 : (00C) Servo,Stepper,PSwitch,SSI,Tamagawa                


1 : (00C)
Servo,Stepper,PSwitch,SSI,EnDAT                   


>>FPGA_PROGRAM(1)


>>

name: FPGA_VERSION
description: Using the SLOT modifier on the MC464 enables checking of the FPGA version number in the main controller and any of the expansion modules. On controllers that support FPGA re-programming, the version number is split to display the main version number and program loaded.
examples:
>>PRINT HEX(FPGA_VERSION)


10C
>>

name: FPU_EXCEPTIONS
description: Any process that raises an FPU (Floating Point Unit) exception will have a bit set here, if process 23 raises an exception then bit 23 will be set This parameter is used by software development engineers when debugging the Trio firmware. For example, an FPU exception can be caused by an illegal request for a floating point operation.

name: FRAC
description: Returns the fractional part of the floating-point value.
params: The fractional part of the expression

name: FRAME
description: A FRAME is a transformation which enables the user to program in one coordinate system when the machine or robot does not have a direct or one-to-one mechanical connection to this coordinate system. The FRAME command selects which transformation to use on axes in a FRAME_GROUP . Applying a FRAME to an axis in a FRAME_GROUP will apply that frame to all the axes in the group. To make this compatible with older firmware, if no FRAME_GROUP s have been configured then a default group is generated using the lowest axes, regardless of what axis the FRAME parameter was issued on. Most transformations require configuration data to specify the lengths of mechanical links or operating modes. This is stored in the table with offsets detailed below in the parameters list. These table positions are offset by the ‘table_offset’ parameter in FRAME_GROUP . For a default FRAME_GROUP table_offset is 0. Do not change the FRAME TABLE parameters with the FRAME enabled. This can result in unpredictable movement which could cause damage or harm. The kinematic runtime feature enable code is required to run FRAME 14 and higher. System with FRAME=0 System with FRAME<>0 Axis scaling When a FRAME is enabled UNITS applies the scaling to the world coordinate system and AXIS_UNITS applies scaling to the axis coordinate system. When FRAME is enabled MPOS is scaled by AXIS_UNITS , when frame is disabled MPOS is scaled by UNITS . Position and following errors When a FRAME is active MPOS is the motor position and DPOS is in the world coordinate system. AXIS_DPOS can be read to find the demand position in the motor coordinate system. The following error is calculated between MPOS and AXIS_DPOS and so is the following error of the motor. When using multiple frames or if you wish to group your axis you can use DISABLE_GROUP so that a MOTION_ERROR on one axis does not affect all. Hardware and Software limits As FS_LIMIT and RS_LIMIT use DPOS they are both active in the world coordinate system. VOLUME_LIMIT also uses DPOS so is also in the world coordinate system. FWD_IN and REV_IN , AXIS_FS_LIMIT and AXIS_RS_LIMIT use AXIS_DPOS as so act on the forward and reverse limit of the motor. When moving off FWD_IN and AXIS_FS_LIMIT the motor must move in a reverse direction. Due to the FRAME transformation this may not be a reverse movement in the world coordinate system. When moving off a REV_IN and AXIS_RS_LIMIT the motor must move in a forward direction. Due to the FRAME transformation this may not be a forward movement in the world coordinate system. Offsetting positions When a FRAME is enabled OFFPOS and DEFPOS must not be used as they cause a jump in both DPOS and MPOS . As the transformation separates DPOS and MPOS using these commands will cause an undesirable jump in motor position. REP_DIST also causes a jump in DPOS and MPOS so when using a FRAME the position must never reach REP_DIST . REP_OPTION must be set to 0 and REP_DIST must be at least twice the size of the biggest possible move on the system. When DATUM is complete it also causes a jump in DPOS and MPOS , so DATUM must never be used when FRAME <> 0 You can use USER_FRAME to define a different origin to program from. Power on sequence and Homing Some FRAME transformations require the machine to be homed and/ or moved to a position before the FRAME is enabled. This can be done using the DATUM function. If you home position is not the zero position of the FRAME then you can use DEFPOS / OFFPOS to set the correct offset before enabling the FRAME. When a FRAME is enabled DPOS is adjusted to the world coordinates which are calculated from the current AXIS_DPOS . You should not perform a DATUM homing routine when the FRAME is enabled as this will change the DPOS which may result in undesirable motion. If you need to perform homing when the FRAME is enabled, you can move to a registration position and then use USER_FRAME to apply the offset.
examples:
ATYPE = 0 'disable built in axes for MC464
FRAME = 0
'Define a start position
DEFPOS(150, 50)
FRAME = 2

name: FRAME_REP_DIST
description: Orientation axes on a FRAME or USER_FRAME must be programmed between ± half a revolution ( UNITS can be used to set radians, degrees etc). This cannot be done using REP_DIST and has to be done using FRAME_REP_DIST and REP_OPTION bit 3. When this is configured the DPOS will wrap to ± half a revolution and AXIS_DPOS will not be wrapped so that the absolute axis position is maintained. Wrapping will only occur when FRAME <> 0 or USER_FRAME <> 0. While both are set to zero the wrapping will be inhibited so that the absolute axis position is maintained. With REP_OPTION bit 3 set and DPOS exceeding FRAME_REP_DIST it will wrap to –FRAME_REP_DIST. The same applies in reverse so when DPOS exceeds –FRAME_REP_DIST it will wrap to FRAME_REP_DIST.
examples:
BASE(axis_w)


  UNITS = (FRAME_SCALE*2*PI) / 360 'degrees


  FRAME_REP_DIST = 180


  REP_OPTION = 8
BASE(axis_w)


  UNITS = (FRAME_SCALE*2*PI) / 360 'degrees


  FRAME_REP_DIST = 180


  REP_OPTION = 9

BASE(axis_w)


  UNITS = (FRAME_SCALE*2*PI) / 360 'degrees


  FRAME_REP_DIST = 180


  REP_OPTION = 8

BASE(axis_w)


  UNITS = (FRAME_SCALE*2*PI) / 360 'degrees


  FRAME_REP_DIST = 180


  REP_OPTION = 9

name: FRAME_SCALE
description: FRAME_ SCALE is used to adjust the resolution of the high accuracy FRAMEs (100+). The default value is very large and so the accuracy is sufficient for most applications.
examples:
FOR ax = 0 TO 5
BASE(ax)
UNITS = FRAME_SCALE
NEXT ax

name: FRAME_TRANS
description: This function enables you to perform both the forward and inverse transformation calculations of a FRAME. One particular use is to check following errors in user units or to calculate positions outside of the FRAME working area. FRAME_TRANS requires the kinematic runtime FEC to use a FRAME 14 and higher. The FRAME calculations are performed on raw position data. When using a FRAME typically the raw position data for DPOS is micrometres and the raw position data for MPOS is encoder counts but this can vary depending on which FRAME you select.
params: The start position in the TABLE of the input positions

The start position in the TABLE of the generated
  positions

The first position in the table where the frame
  configuration is found (default 0)
examples:
'Load positions into the table


FOR x=0 TO 3
BASE(x)
TABLE(1000+x,MPOS AXIS(x)*UNITS AXIS(x))
NEXT x
'Calculate forward transform to see MPOS is Cartesian
coordinates
FRAME_TRANS(15, 1000,2000,1,100)
TABLE(3000, TABLE(2000)/ UNITS AXIS(0))
TABLE(3001, TABLE(2001)/ UNITS AXIS(1))
TABLE(3002, TABLE(2002)/ UNITS AXIS(2))
PRINT "DPOS IN ENCODER COUNTS",TABLE(2000),TABLE(2001),TABLE(2002)
PRINT "DPOS IN
MM",TABLE(3000),TABLE(3001),TABLE(3002)
PRINT "FE in world x = ", TABLE(3000) – DPOS AXIS(0)
PRINT "FE in world y = ", TABLE(3001) – DPOS AXIS(1)
PRINT "FE in world z = ", TABLE(3002) – DPOS AXIS(2)
'Load positions into the table
TABLE(5000,100*UNITS AXIS(0),200*UNITS AXIS(1),400*UNITS
AXIS(2))
'Calculate reverse transform to see
FRAME_TRANS(14, 5000,6000,0)
'Divide the result by the AXIS_UNITS to get
'the MPOS in degrees
TABLE(7000, TABLE(6000)/ AXIS_UNITS)
TABLE(7001, TABLE(6001)/ AXIS_UNITS)
TABLE(7002, TABLE(6002)/ AXIS_UNITS)
PRINT "MPOS RAW ENCODER COUNTS",
TABLE(6000),TABLE(6001),TABLE(6002)
PRINT "MPOS degrees",
TABLE(7000),TABLE(7001),TABLE(7002)

'Load positions into the table


FOR x=0 TO 3
BASE(x)
TABLE(1000+x,MPOS AXIS(x)*UNITS AXIS(x))
NEXT x
'Calculate forward transform to see MPOS is Cartesian
coordinates

TABLE(3000, TABLE(2000)/ UNITS AXIS(0))
TABLE(3001, TABLE(2001)/ UNITS AXIS(1))
TABLE(3002, TABLE(2002)/ UNITS AXIS(2))
PRINT "DPOS IN ENCODER COUNTS",TABLE(2000),TABLE(2001),TABLE(2002)
PRINT "DPOS IN
MM",TABLE(3000),TABLE(3001),TABLE(3002)

PRINT "FE in world y = ", TABLE(3001) – DPOS AXIS(1)
PRINT "FE in world z = ", TABLE(3002) – DPOS AXIS(2)

'Load positions into the table

name: FREE
description: Returns the amount of program memory available for user programs. Each line takes a minimum of 4 characters (bytes) in memory. This is for the length of this line, the length of the previous line, number of spaces at the beginning of the line and a single command token. Additional commands need one byte per token, most other data is held as ASCII. The Motion Coordinator compiles programs before they are run, this means that a little under twice the memory is required to be able to run a program.

name: FS_LIMIT
description: An end of travel limit may be set up in software thus allowing the program control of the working envelope of the machine. This parameter holds the absolute position of the forward travel limit in user units. Bit 9 of the AXISSTATUS register is set when the axis position is greater than the FS_LIMIT. When DPOS is greater than FS_LIMIT the controller will cancel the move, so the axis will decelerate at FASTDEC if specified, otherwise at DECEL . FS_LIMIT is disabled when it has a value that is much greater than REP_DIST . The value must take account of the position lag on MPOS.
examples:
BASE(1)
DATUM(3)
WAIT IDLE
FS_LIMIT = 200
FASTDEC = 1000
FS_LIMIT = REPDIST + 100

BASE(1)
DATUM(3)
WAIT IDLE
FS_LIMIT = 200
FASTDEC = 1000
FS_LIMIT = REPDIST + 100

name: FULL_SP_RADIUS
description: This parameter is used with CORNER_MODE , it defines the minimum radius that will be executed at full speed. When a radius to be profiled is smaller than FULL_SP_RADIUS the speed will be proportionally reduced so that VP_SPEED = FORCE_SPEED * radius / FULL_SP_RADIUS.
examples:
CORNER_MODE = 8
MERGE = ON
SPEED = 10
FULL_SP_RADIUS = 6
DEFPOS(0, 0)
MOVESP(10, 10)
MOVESP(10, 5)
MOVESP(5, 5)
MOVECIRCSP(8, 8, 0, 8, 1)
MOVECIRCSP(3, 3, 0, 3, 1)
MOVESP(5, 5)
MOVESP(10, 5)

name: FUNCTION
syntax:
FUNCTION name([param1 AS type[, param2 AS type[, param3 AS
type …. ]]])


….


RETURN x


ENDFUNC
description: User defined function residing in a function library for use by any BASIC program in the Motion Coordinator multi-tasking system. A function must as a minimum have a name and finish with the ENDFUNC keyword. The contents of the function can take any BASIC commands so long as they work in the context of the required function operation. Up to 1024 FUNCTIONs can be defined and used in firmware 2.0301 and later. Older versions of firmware support up to 256 FUNCTIONs. FUNCTION requires a matching ENDFUNC keyword to complete a function definition. Parameters are optional (maximum of 16) and should be specified within parentheses; parameters of the same data type can be separated by commas. A return parameter is also optional and should be specified at the end of the line. FUNCTION may be used only within a Function Library. BASIC Library files support a smaller subset of commands compared with standard program files. For example, GOSUB and GOTO are not supported within a BASIC Library file. Library functions can have a nested hierarchy of calls to other library functions to a maximum depth of 16 levels, the maximum number of parameters that can traverse through this hierarchy is 200.
params: First optional parameter to be passed into the function
Up to 16 parameters may be passed
examples:
FUNCTION

set_rs232(baudrate 
AS
 INTEGER
)


    
SETCOM
(baudrate, 
8
,

1
, 
0
, 
1
, 
0
)



ENDFUNC

get_decel_distance(f_decel

2

2

'============================================================
' Function to return multiple values in an array.
' Call: get_multi_vr(index, number_of_values,
array_pointer)
' Example to get 100 VRs
'   DIM my_array AS FLOAT(100)
'   get_multi_vr(200, 100, my_array)
'   PRINT my_array(54)
'============================================================
FUNCTION get_multi_vr(index, size AS INTEGER, return_array
AS FLOAT())
DIM a_index, v_index AS INTEGER
a_index = 0
FOR v_index = index TO index + size - 1
return_array(a_index) = VR(v_index)
a_index = a_index + 1
NEXT v_index
ENDFUNC

name: FWD_IN
description: This parameter holds the input number to be used as a forward limit input. When the forward limit input is active, CANCEL is issued on that axis. When FWD_IN is active AXISSTATUS bit 4 is set. The input used for FWD_IN is active low. When the forward limit input is active the controller will cancel the move, so the axis will decelerate at FASTDEC if specified, otherwise at DECEL .
examples: FWD_IN AXIS(9) = 19

name: FWD_JOG
description: This parameter holds the input number to be used as a jog forward input. When the FWD_JOG input is active the axis moves forward at JOGSPEED . The input used for FWD_JOG is active low. It is advisable to use INVERT_IN on the input for FWD_JOG so that 0V at the input disables the jog. FWD_JOG overrides REV_JOG if both are assigned to the same input and activated. If they are assigned to different inputs and activated one after the other, the first command takes precedence, the second command is ignored.
examples:
INVERT_IN(7,ON)
FWD_JOG=7

name: FWD_START
description: Force an immediate forward jog motion. When FWD_JOG is -1 and FWD_START is set to 1, the axis will run the MTYPE 14 forward_jog in the axis. If FWD_JOG is set to an input number, (not equal to -1) then FWD_START has no function.
examples:
BASE(2)
FWD_JOG = -1
REV_JOG = -1
IF IDLE THEN
REPEAT
SELECT_CASE VR(10)
CASE 0
FWD_START = 0
REV_START = 0
CASE 1
FWD_START = 1
REV_START = 0
CASE 2


      FWD_START = 0
REV_START = 1
END_CASE
UNTIL VR(10) = 99


ENDIF

name: GET
description: Waits for the arrival of a single character on the serial. The ASCII value of the character is assigned to the variable specified. The user program will wait until a character is available. Poll KEY to check to if a character has been received before performing a GET.
params: See # for the full channel list (default 0 if omitted)

The variable to store the received character, this may
  be local variable, 
VR
 or 
TABLE
examples:
start:
PRINT #5, "Press 'y' for YES or 'n' for NO"
GET #5, char
IF char = 121 THEN
PRINT #5, "YES selected"
ELSEIF char = 110 THEN
PRINT #5, "NO selected"
ELSE
PRINT #5, "BAD selection"
GOTO start
ENDIF
WHILE KEY #2
GET #2, dump
WEND
PRINT #2, "ENTER NAME"
WAIT UNTIL KEY #2
count = 0
WHILE char <> $D 'Carriage return
GET #2, char
VR(count) = char
count = count + 1
WEND

start:
PRINT #5, "Press 'y' for YES or 'n' for NO"
GET #5, char
IF char = 121 THEN
PRINT #5, "YES selected"
ELSEIF char = 110 THEN
PRINT #5, "NO selected"
ELSE
PRINT #5, "BAD selection"
GOTO start
ENDIF

WHILE KEY #2
GET #2, dump
WEND
PRINT #2, "ENTER NAME"
WAIT UNTIL KEY #2
count = 0
WHILE char <> $D 'Carriage return
GET #2, char
VR(count) = char
count = count + 1
WEND

  tags.

Optional parameter to indicate where in the VRs the tag
  information will be written. If -1 (default), then write to the standard
  output stream.


  

See 
Note

x_pos: Memory: 1 (Vr), Address: 100, Datatype: 0xC3

name: GLOBAL
description: GLOBALs can be declared in the controller which are available to all programs. GLOBAL declares the name as a reference to one of the global VR variables. The name can then be used both within the program containing the GLOBAL definition and all other programs in the Motion Coordinator project. They must be declared on startup. The program declaring GLOBALs must be the ONLY process running at power-up. Once a GLOBAL has been assigned it cannot be changed, even if you change the program that assigns it. Model number Max number of GLOBALs MC464 1024 MC403(Z), MC405, Euro404, Euro408, MC4N-ECAT, MC4N-RTEX 4096 MC664(X), MC508, Flex-6 Nano, Flex-6X Nano, MC6N-ECAT 8192 While developing you may wish to clear or change a GLOBAL. You can clear a single GLOBAL by using the first parameter alone. All GLOBALs can be cleared by issuing GLOBAL. You can view all GLOBALS using LIST_GLOBAL . Whenever a program is RUN, the GLOBAL and CONSTANT tables are searched for the names of already defined variables. If a large number of GLOBAL/CONSTANT names are pre-defined, this may take several seconds. When using the GLOBAL and CONSTANT system is it not advised to depend on temporarily running and then stopping programs. It is best to run all the required programs when the machine starts and leave them in that running state.
params:
Any user-defined name containing lower case alpha,
  numerical or underscore (_) characters.

The number of the 
VR
 to be
  associated with name.
examples:
GLOBAL "screw_pitch",12
GLOBAL "ratio1",534
ratio1 = 3.56
screw_pitch = 23.0
PRINT screw_pitch, ratio1
>>GLOBAL "pitch",12


>>GLOBAL "roll",534


>>GLOBAL "yaw",922


>>LIST_GLOBAL ‘ Display GLOBALs


Global                           VR


----------------                 ----


pitch                            12


roll                             534


yaw                              922


Constant                         Value


----------------                 -------
>>GLOBAL "roll" ‘ Delete roll


>>LIST_GLOBAL ‘ Display GLOBALs


Global                           VR


----------------                 ----


pitch                            12


yaw                              922


Constant                         Value


----------------                 -------
>>GLOBAL ‘ Delete all GLOBALs


>>LIST_GLOBAL ‘ Display GLOBALs


Global                           VR


----------------                 ----


Constant                         Value


----------------                 -------
>>
' machine settings
GLOBAL "initial_speed", 10
GLOBAL "run_speed", 11
' vision data via Modbus
GLOBAL "vision_x", 51
GLOBAL "vision_y", 51
GLOBAL "vision_r", 52
GLOBAL "vision_status", 53
CONSTANT "x_axis", 0
CONSTANT "y_axis", 1

RUN "MAIN", 2 ' run the main program on process
#2

GLOBAL "screw_pitch",12

ratio1 = 3.56
screw_pitch = 23.0
PRINT screw_pitch, ratio1

>>GLOBAL "pitch",12


>>GLOBAL "roll",534


>>GLOBAL "yaw",922


>>LIST_GLOBAL ‘ Display GLOBALs

----------------                 ----


pitch                            12


roll                             534


yaw                              922


Constant                         Value


----------------                 -------
>>GLOBAL "roll" ‘ Delete roll


>>LIST_GLOBAL ‘ Display GLOBALs


Global                           VR


----------------                 ----


pitch                            12


yaw                              922


Constant                         Value


----------------                 -------
>>GLOBAL ‘ Delete all GLOBALs


>>LIST_GLOBAL ‘ Display GLOBALs


Global                           VR


----------------                 ----


Constant                         Value


----------------                 -------
>>

name: GOSUB..RETURN
description: Stores the position of the line after the GOSUB command and then branches to the label specified. Upon reaching the RETURN statement, control is returned to the stored line. GOSUB..RETURN loops can be nested up to 8 deep in each program.
params: TrioBASIC statements that you wish to execute
A valid label that occurs in the program.
examples:
WHILE machine_active
GOSUB routine1
GOSUB routine2
WEND
STOP 'prevents running into subroutines when machine
stopped.
routine1:
PRINT "Measured Position="; MPOS; CHR(13);
RETURN
routine2:
PRINT "Demand Position="; DPOS; CHR(13);
RETURN
y = 1
z = 4
GOSUB calc
PRINT "New value = "; x
STOP
calc:
x = y + z / 2
RETURN

WHILE machine_active
GOSUB routine1
GOSUB routine2
WEND
STOP 'prevents running into subroutines when machine
stopped.
routine1:
PRINT "Measured Position="; MPOS; CHR(13);
RETURN
routine2:
PRINT "Demand Position="; DPOS; CHR(13);
RETURN

y = 1
z = 4
GOSUB calc
PRINT "New value = "; x
STOP
calc:
x = y + z / 2
RETURN

name: GOTO
description: Identifies the next line of the program to be executed.
params: A valid label that exists in the program.
examples:
start:
PRINT#5, "Press 'y' for YES and 'n' for NO."
GET#5, char
IF char = 121 THEN
PRINT#5, "YES selected"
ELSEIF char = 110 THEN
PRINT#5, "NO selected"
ELSE
PRINT#5, "BAD selection"
GOTO start
ENDIF

name: GTASIZE
description: Returns the size of the global target array, which varies across controller types. The global target array is indexed from zero, so the valid range of indices is 0 to GTASIZE - 1. This array shares elements between the three target types, TARGET, TARGETJ and TARGETE, so an element may be any one of these types depending on whether they were initialised with GTA, GTAJ, or GTAE.

name: >  Greater Than
description: Returns TRUE if <expression-1> is greater than <expression-2>, otherwise returns FALSE . The comparison operators are typically used in conditional statements like IF , REPEAT and WHILE , though can be used anywhere an expression is valid.
examples:
WAIT UNTIL MPOS > 200
VR(0) = x > 0
WAIT UNTIL MPOS > 200

name: >=  Greater Than or Equal
description: Returns TRUE if <expression-1> is greater than or equal to <expression-2>, otherwise returns FALSE . The comparison operators are typically used in conditional statements like IF , REPEAT and WHILE , though can be used anywhere an expression is valid.
examples: IF target >= 120 THEN MOVEABS(0)

name: HALT
description: Halts execution of all running programs. You can use HALT in a program. HALT does not stop any motion. Currently executing, or buffered moves will continue unless they are terminated with a CANCEL or RAPIDSTOP command.
examples:
>>HALT%[Process 20:Line 2] (31) - Program is stopped
%[Process 21:Line 1] (31) - Program is stopped
>>

name: HEX
description: HEX returns the hexadecimal value for the decimal number supplied as a STRING which can be assigned to a STRING variable or be PRINT ed.
params:
A hexadecimal 
STRING
 of the
  number
examples:
>>PRINT HEX(AXISSTATUS)
10
>>
DIM value AS STRING
value = value + HEX(number)

>>PRINT HEX(AXISSTATUS)
10
>>

DIM value AS STRING
value = value + HEX(number)

name: HLM_COMMAND
description: The HLM_COMMAND command performs a specific Host Link command operation to one or to all Host Link Slaves on the selected port. Program execution will be paused until the response string has been received or the timeout time has elapsed. The timeout time is specified by using the HLM_TIMEOUT parameter. The status of the transfer can be monitored with the HLM_STATUS parameter.
params: The the Host Link operation to perform:

This performs the Host Link PC MODEL READ (MM) command
  to read the CPU Unit model code. The result is written to the MC Unit
  variable specified by mc_area and mc_offset.

This performs the Host Link TEST (TS) command to check
  correct communication by sending string "MCxxx TEST STRING" and
  checking the echoed string. Check the HLM_STATUS parameter for the result.

This performs the Host Link ABORT (XZ) command to abort
  the Host Link command that is currently being processed. The ABORT command
  does not receive a response.

This performs the Host Link INITIALIZE (**) command to
  initialise the transmission control procedure of all Slave Units.

This performs the Host Link STATUS WRITE (SC) command
  to change the operating mode of the CPU Unit.

The specified serial port (see specific controller
  specification for numbers).

The Slave node number to send the Host Link command to.
  Range: [0, 31].
The specified CPU Unit operating mode.

The MC Unit's memory selection to write the received
  data to.

The address of the specified MC Unit memory area to
  read from.
examples:
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_MREAD, 1, 12, MC_VR, 233)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_TEST, 2, 23)
PRINT HLM_STATUS PORT(2)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_INIT, 2)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_ABORT, 2, 4)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_STWR, 2, 0, 2)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_MREAD, 1, 12, MC_VR, 233)

HLM_COMMAND(HLM_TEST, 2, 23)
PRINT HLM_STATUS PORT(2)

HLM_COMMAND(HLM_INIT, 2)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_ABORT, 2, 4)
HLM_COMMAND(HLM_STWR, 2, 0, 2)

name: HLM_READ
syntax: HLM_READ(port, node, pc_area, pc_offset, length, mc_area, mc_offset)
description: The HLM_READ command reads data from a Host Link Slave by sending a Host Link command string containing the specified node of the Slave to the serial port. The received response data will be written to either VR or Table variables. Each word of data will be transferred to one variable. The maximum data length is 30 words (single frame transfer). Program execution will be paused until the response string has been received or the timeout time has elapsed. The timeout time is specified by using the HLM_TIMEOUT parameter. The status of the transfer can be monitored with the HLM_STATUS parameter.
params:
The specified serial port. (See specific controller
  specification for numbers)

The Slave node number to send the Host Link command to.
  Range: [0, 31].
The PC memory selection for the Host Link command.

The address of the specified PC memory area to read
  from. Range: [0, 9999].

The number of words of data to be transferred. Range:
  [1, 30].

The MC Unit's memory selection to write the received
  data to.

The address of the specified MC Unit memory area to
  write to.

name: HLM_STATUS
description: Returns the status of the Host Link serial communications.

name: HLM_TIMEOUT
description: Sets the timeout value for Hostlink communications.
examples: HLM_TIMEOUT = 600

name: HLM_WRITE
syntax: HLM_WRITE(port, node, pc_area, pc_offset, length, mc_area, mc_offset)
description: The HLM_WRITE command writes data from the MC Unit to a Host Link Slave by sending a Host Link command string containing the specified node of the Slave to the serial port. The received response data will be written from either VR or Table variables. Each variable will define on word of data which will be transferred. The maximum data length is 29 words (single frame transfer). Program execution will be paused until the response string has been received or the timeout time has elapsed. The timeout time is specified by using the HLM_TIMEOUT parameter. The status of the transfer can be monitored with the HLM_STATUS parameter.
params:
The specified serial port (see specific controller
  specification for numbers).

The Slave node number to send the Host Link command to.
  Range: [0, 31].
The PC memory selection for the Host Link command.

The address of the specified PC memory area to write
  to. Range: [0, 9999].

The number of words of data to be transferred. Range:
  [1, 30].
The MC Unit's memory selection to read the data from.

The address of the specified MC Unit memory area to
  read from.
examples: HLM_WRITE(1, 28, PLC_EM, 50, 25, MC_VR, 200)

name: HLS_MODEL
description: Defines the model number returned to a Hostlink Master.

name: HMI_CONNECTIONS
description: Return the connection strings for all currently connected clients.

name: HMI_GET_PAGE
description: Return the currently selected page on the given HMI Client. If the IP address is not specified then the current page for the lowest active session will be returned.

name: HMI_GET_STATUS
description: Return the status of the given HMI Client. If the IP address is not specified then the current page for the lowest active session will be returned.

name: HMI_PROC
description: Sets the process number on which the HMI Server protocol will be initiated. This value must be set before the first HMI Client connection occurs. The default value at power up is -1, which will automatically select the process number according to the normal RUN command rules. If this value is to be set, then it is recommended that it be set in the special MC_CONFIG program to insure that the value is valid before any HMI Client can connect to the Motion Coordinator.

name: HMI_SERVER
description: This command allows the Trio HMI Server to be controlled, configured, and interrogated from a TrioBASIC program. If there are no parameters then the function is 0, and the parameter is 0.
params: Read the HMI Client error data
Read the HMI Client version information
examples:
'Check for error
IF HMI_SERVER(1, 0) THEN
PRINT "HMI Client reports error"
PRINT "HMI Error="; HMI_SERVER(1,
0)
PRINT "HMI
Description="; HMI_SERVER(1, 1)
PRINT "MC Program="; HMI_SERVER(1, 2)
PRINT "MC Process="; HMI_SERVER(1, 3)
ENDIF

name: HMI_SET_PAGE
description: Request that the HMI Client change to the given page. If the IP address is not specified the request will be sent to all currently connected clients. This command will wait for all pending HMI Client requests to complete before submitting the new request, but it will not wait for the HMI Client to complete the request. This means the controller will continue to run the software without waiting for the requested page to show on the HMI Client.

name: # Hash
description: The # symbol is used to specify a communications channel to be used for serial input/output commands.
params:
Used for 
Motion Perfect internal operations

Used for 
Motion Perfect internal operations

Channel configured using the 
OPEN

  command for protocol usage

Channel configured using the 
OPEN

  command for file and string variable access
examples:
PRINT #1, "Printing data to RS232 Channel"
PRINT #5, "Printing data to Motion Perfect Terminal
5"
WHILE KEY #6
GET #63, VR(123)
WEND

PRINT #1, "Printing data to RS232 Channel"
PRINT #5, "Printing data to Motion Perfect Terminal
5"

WHILE KEY #6
GET #63, VR(123)
WEND

name: IDLE
description: IDLE returns whether the axis is stationary and has no moves loaded on it, a move being loaded when it is either in the motion move buffer (MTYPE), the next move buffer (NTYPE) or the process move buffer (PMOVE), and additionally when there is an additional movement (MOVEADD) active. Note that if the axis has an additional axis (ADDAX) assigned to it, that this is not taken account of, neither in terms of being ‘loaded’ nor in terms of having its velocity added to this axis. The state of IDLE refers only to the Demanded Position of the axis, so when IDLE, while the axis has completed the moves velocity profiling, the servo system may still be settling into position. Note that pausing a move by any means, like feed-hold (FHOLD_IN), does not cause an axis to be considered idle, as it still has a move or moves loaded on it. The most common usage of IDLE is to see if a move has completed using a WAIT statement, but it can be used anywhere a value is expected. When WAIT IDLE is used immediately after a move is loaded, since the move will be in PMOVE, IDLE will immediately be FALSE and the WAIT IDLE will wait until the move completes.
examples:
MOVE(100)


WAIT IDLE


PRINT "Move Done"
OP(move_finished, ON)
IF IDLE AXIS(1) THEN
BASE(1)
MOVE(100)
MOVE(50)
MOVE(-150)
ENDIF
REGIST(20)
MOVELINK(100, 120, 20, 20, 3)
WAIT UNTIL MARK OR IDLE
IF MARK THEN
captured_position = REG_POS
missed_mark = 0
ELSE
PRINT #5, "Failed to find MARK"
missed_mark = missed_mark + 1
IF missed_mark > 10 THEN
GOSUB flash_amber_lamp
ENDIF
ENDIF
BASE(4, 5, 6, 7, 8)
MERGE = ON
INTERP_FACTOR AXIS(7) = OFF 'Calculate vector speed for
XYZ only
INTERP_FACTOR AXIS(8) = OFF 'A and B axes simply follow in
sync.
MOVE(x1, y1, z1, a1, b1)
MOVE(x2, y2, z2, a2, b2)
MOVE(x3, y3, z3, a3, b3)
MOVE(x4, y4, z4, a4, b4)
MOVE(x5, y5, z5, a5, b5)
MOVE(x6, y6, z6, a6, b6)
WAIT IDLE

MOVE(100)


WAIT IDLE


PRINT "Move Done"
OP(move_finished, ON)

IF IDLE AXIS(1) THEN
BASE(1)
MOVE(100)
MOVE(50)
MOVE(-150)
ENDIF

REGIST(20)
MOVELINK(100, 120, 20, 20, 3)
WAIT UNTIL MARK OR IDLE
IF MARK THEN
captured_position = REG_POS
missed_mark = 0
ELSE
PRINT #5, "Failed to find MARK"
missed_mark = missed_mark + 1
IF missed_mark > 10 THEN
GOSUB flash_amber_lamp
ENDIF
ENDIF

BASE(4, 5, 6, 7, 8)
MERGE = ON
INTERP_FACTOR AXIS(7) = OFF 'Calculate vector speed for
XYZ only
INTERP_FACTOR AXIS(8) = OFF 'A and B axes simply follow in
sync.
MOVE(x1, y1, z1, a1, b1)
MOVE(x2, y2, z2, a2, b2)
MOVE(x3, y3, z3, a3, b3)
MOVE(x4, y4, z4, a4, b4)
MOVE(x5, y5, z5, a5, b5)
MOVE(x6, y6, z6, a6, b6)
WAIT IDLE

name: IEEE_IN
syntax: IEEE_IN(byte0, byte1, byte2, byte3[, byte4, byte5, byte6, byte7])
description: The IEEE_IN function returns the floating-point number represented by the given byte values in IEEE 754 format, typically received over a communications link such as Modbus. An IEEE 754 single-precision value is composed of 4 bytes, and a double-precision value is composed of 8 bytes.
params:
The bytes that represent an IEEE 754 floating point
  value, 4 values for 32-bit float and 8 values for a 64-bit float.
examples: VR(200) = IEEE_IN(VR(0), VR(1), VR(2), VR(3))

name: IEEE_OUT
description: The IEEE_OUT function returns a single byte extracted from the floating-point value encoded in IEEE 754 format, typically for transmission over a communication bus system. The function is called multiple times to extract each byte in turn, 4 times for IEEE 754 single precision, and 8 times for IEEE 754 double precision.
params: Any TrioBASIC floating-point variable or parameter.
The byte number (0 - 7) to be extracted.

Number of bits in the floating-point value (32 or 64),
  default 32.
examples:
a = MPOS AXIS(2)
byte0 = IEEE_OUT(a, 0)
byte1 = IEEE_OUT(a, 1)
byte2 = IEEE_OUT(a, 2)
byte3 = IEEE_OUT(a, 3)
a = MPOS AXIS(2)
byte0 = IEEE_OUT(a, 0, 64)
byte1 = IEEE_OUT(a, 1, 64)
byte2 = IEEE_OUT(a, 2, 64)
byte3 = IEEE_OUT(a, 3, 64)
byte4 = IEEE_OUT(a, 4, 64)
byte5 = IEEE_OUT(a, 5, 64)
byte6 = IEEE_OUT(a, 6, 64)
byte7 = IEEE_OUT(a, 7, 64)

a = MPOS AXIS(2)
byte0 = IEEE_OUT(a, 0)
byte1 = IEEE_OUT(a, 1)
byte2 = IEEE_OUT(a, 2)
byte3 = IEEE_OUT(a, 3)

a = MPOS AXIS(2)
byte0 = IEEE_OUT(a, 0, 64)
byte1 = IEEE_OUT(a, 1, 64)
byte2 = IEEE_OUT(a, 2, 64)
byte3 = IEEE_OUT(a, 3, 64)
byte4 = IEEE_OUT(a, 4, 64)
byte5 = IEEE_OUT(a, 5, 64)
byte6 = IEEE_OUT(a, 6, 64)
byte7 = IEEE_OUT(a, 7, 64)

name: IF..THEN..ELSEIF..ELSE..ENDIF
syntax:
IF condition THEN


  commands


ELSEIF expression THEN


  commands


ELSE


  commands


ENDIF
params: TrioBASIC statements that you wish to execute
examples:
IF count >= batch_size THEN
PRINT #3, CURSOR(20); "   BATCH COMPLETE   ";
GOSUB index 'Index conveyor to clear batch
count = 0
ENDIF
IF WDOG = ON THEN
OP(warning, ON)
ELSE
OP(warning, OFF)
ENDIF
IF operating_state = 0 THEN
PRINT#5, "Machine Running"
ELSEIF operating_state = 1 THEN
PRINT#5, "Machine Idle"
ELSEIF operating_state = 2 THEN
PRINT#5, "Machine Jammed"
ELSE
PRINT#5, "Machine in unknown state"
ENDIF

IF count >= batch_size THEN
PRINT #3, CURSOR(20); "   BATCH COMPLETE   ";
GOSUB index 'Index conveyor to clear batch
count = 0
ENDIF

IF WDOG = ON THEN
OP(warning, ON)
ELSE
OP(warning, OFF)
ENDIF

IF operating_state = 0 THEN
PRINT#5, "Machine Running"
ELSEIF operating_state = 1 THEN
PRINT#5, "Machine Idle"
ELSEIF operating_state = 2 THEN
PRINT#5, "Machine Jammed"
ELSE
PRINT#5, "Machine in unknown state"
ENDIF

name: IN
description: IN is used to read the state of the Digital Inputs. Digital I/O normally refers to the 24 V inputs and outputs attached to the Motion Coordinator. I/O points that do not have hardware attached can be used as "Virtual" outputs and inputs that can be set with OP and read back with IN. If called with no parameters, IN returns the binary sum of the first 32 inputs. If called with one parameter, it returns the state (1 or 0) of that specific input channel. If called with 2 parameters IN returns the binary sum of the group of inputs. For the 2 parameter case, the inputs should be less than 32 apart. IN is equivalent to IN(0, 31)
params: The state of the selected input or range of inputs
Returns the binary sum of the first 32 inputs

Input number to return the value of/start of input
  group
examples:
WAIT UNTIL IN(4) = ON
GOSUB place
WHILE TRUE
MOVEABS(IN(4, 7) * 1.5467)
WAIT IDLE
WEND
If (IN AND 12) <> 0 THEN GOTO start
'(Bit 2 = 4 and Bit 3 = 8) so the mask is 12

WAIT UNTIL IN(4) = ON
GOSUB place

WHILE TRUE
MOVEABS(IN(4, 7) * 1.5467)
WAIT IDLE
WEND

If (IN AND 12) <> 0 THEN GOTO start
'(Bit 2 = 4 and Bit 3 = 8) so the mask is 12

name: INCLUDE
description: Allows the inclusion of TrioBASIC commands and constructs (except ON BASICERROR). This is typically used to share data initialisation between programs, but you can imagine an INCLUDE as a copy and paste from the include into the program at the point of the INCLUDE statement, but with the benefit that any changes made to the included file are shared between all programs that include it. Whenever an included program is modified, all programs that depend on it are re-compiled as well, avoiding inconsistencies. Note that prior to firmware version 2.0269: • Nested includes were not allowed (now they are allowed to be up to 5 deep) • Includes had to be the first statement (barring comments) in a program (now they may be placed anywhere) • Only variable definitions and conditional logic were allowed, and it could not be used as a general subroutine with any commands within it (it may now contain any statements) Circular dependency is not permitted. So, A cannot include itself; if A includes B, B cannot include A; if A includes B and B Includes C, C cannot include either B or A, etc.
params: The name of the program to be included
examples:
'Include data definitions
INCLUDE "DATA_DEFS"
'Motion commands using included data
FORWARD AXIS(drive_axis)
CONNECT(1, drive_axis) AXIS(linked_axis)
drive_axis = 4
linked_axis = 1

name: INDEVICE
description: This parameter specifies the default active input device. Specifying an INDEVICE for a process allows the channel number for a program to set for all subsequent GET , KEY , INPUT and LINPUT statements. This command is process specific so other processes will use the default channel. This command is available for backward compatibility, it is currently recommended to use #channel, instead.
examples:
INDEVICE = 5
'Get character on channel 5:
IF KEY THEN
GET k
ENDIF

name: INITIALISE
description: This system command sets all Axis Parameters to their default values. The parameters are also reset each time the controller is powered up, or when an EX (software reset) command is performed. INITIALISE may reset a parameter relating to a digital drive communication or encoder causing the connection to be lost.
examples:
>>INITIALISE
>>

name: INPUT
description: Waits for an ASCII string to be received on the current input device, terminated with a carriage return <CR>. If the string is valid its numeric value is assigned to the specified variable. If an invalid string is entered it is ignored, an error message displayed and input repeated. Multiple values may be requested on one line, the values are separated by commas, or by carriage returns <CR>. Poll KEY to check to if a character has been received before performing an INPUT.
params: See # for the full channel list (default 0 if omitted)

The variable to store the received character, this may be
  local variable, 
VR
 or 
TABLE
examples:
INPUT num
PRINT "BATCH COUNT = "; num[0]
On terminal:
123 <CR>
BATCH COUNT = 123
PRINT "ENTER LENGTH AND WIDTH?";
INPUT VR(11), VR(12)
ENTER LENGTH AND WIDTH ? 1200,
1500 <CR>

INPUT num
PRINT "BATCH COUNT = "; num[0]
On terminal:
123 <CR>
BATCH COUNT = 123

PRINT "ENTER LENGTH AND WIDTH?";
INPUT VR(11), VR(12)
ENTER LENGTH AND WIDTH ? 1200,
1500 <CR>

name: INPUTS0 / INPUTS1
description: The INPUTS0/INPUTS1 parameters holds the state of the Input channels as a system parameter. Reading the inputs using these system parameters is not normally required. The IN (x, y) command should be used instead. They are made available in this format to make the input channels accessible to the SCOPE command which can only store parameters.

name: INSTR
description: Searches the input string looking for the search string and returns the (zero based) index of the first occurrence of the string or -1 if the string is not found.
params: Optional integer offset to start searching at

Optional wildcard character to use within the search
  string expressed as a single character string or as a numerical ASCII value
examples:
DIM str1 AS STRING(32)
str1 = "TRIO MOTION TECHNOLOGY"
PRINT INSTR(str1, "MOTION") 'value = 5
PRINT INSTR(6, str1, "MOTION") 'value = -1
PRINT INSTR("Value = 123.45E10", "###.##E##",
"#") 'Value = 8
PRINT INSTR("this is my string", "is *y",
42) 'Value = 5
PRINT INSTR(3, str1, "IO") 'Value = 8

name: INT
description: The INT function returns the integer part of a number by truncating the fractional part towards zero. To convert a floating-point value to the nearest whole number, use the ROUND function, but note that this returns a floating-point value, so use INT to cast the result of ROUND to integer, if required
params: The integer part of the expression

name: INTEGER_READ
description: INTEGER_READ performs a low-level read of a 64-bit source, splitting it into two 32-bit values, where each value represents the bit pattern of the source’s least significant and most significant 32-bit words. The source may be integer or floating-point, as it is the bit pattern (i.e. internal representation) that is read, not its value or a cast of its value. This can be used to read the position from high resolution encoders
params:
64-bit value that will be read, can be 
VR
,
  
TABLE
, or system variable.

The variable to store the least significant (rightmost)
  32 bits, this may be local variable, 
VR
 or 
TABLE

The variable to store the most significant (leftmost)
  32 bits, this may be local variable, 
VR
 or 
TABLE
examples:
VR(1) = 0.9999
INTEGER_READ(VR(1), VR(4), VR(5))
PRINT VR(1); "    ";
PRINT "MSW "; HEX(VR(5)), "LSW ";
HEX(VR(4))
0.9999    MSW
48E8A71E     LSW 3FEFFF2E
INTEGER_READ(ENCODER AXIS(2), VR(4), VR(5))
PRINT "Encoder Value "; VR(5)[0]

name: INTEGER_WRITE
description: INTEGER_WRITE performs a low-level write to a 64-bit destination by combining the bit patterns of two 32-bit values into a 64-bit bit pattern and writing this into the destination. The destination may be integer or floating-point, as it is the bit pattern (i.e. internal representation) that is written, not its value or a cast of its value.
params:
64-bit value that will be written, can be 
VR
,
  
TABLE
, or system variable.

Least significant (rightmost) 32 bits, can be any valid
  TrioBASIC expression.

Most significant (leftmost) 32 bits, can be any valid
  TrioBASIC expression.
examples:
INTEGER_WRITE(VR(1), $1234, $5678)
INTEGER_READ(VR(1), VR(4), VR(5))
PRINT HEX(VR(4)), HEX(VR(5))
INTEGER_WRITE(VR(1), $48E8A71E, $3FEFFF2E)
PRINT VR(1)

name: INTERNALIO_BASE
description: The majority of Motion Coordinators have internal I/O addressed at IN(0) to IN(15) and OP(8) to OP(15). If the Motion Coordinator has more than 16 internal I/O points, the extra I/O are addressed from IN/OP(16) and above. These addresses are usually allocated to CAN I/O. Therefore, to allow application programs to have common functionality between the standard 16 I/O points and Motion Coordinators with more than 16 I/O, the parameter INTERNALIO_BASE can be set to split the internal I/O at the 15/16 boundary. INTERNALIO_BASE sets the start address for the internal I/O point number 16. The internal I/O is then split into 2 groups, 0 to 15 and INTERNALIO_BASE to INTERNALIO_BASE + number of extra internal I/O points. It is common to set the INTERNALIO_BASE to the next value above the last CAN I/O point. This command only has a function on Motion Coordinators with more than 16 internal I/O points.
examples: INTERNALIO_BASE = 32
INTERNALIO_BASE = 32

name: INTERP_FACTOR
description: This parameter excludes the axis from the interpolated motion calculations so that it will become a following axis. This means that you can create an interpolated XY move with Z completing its movement at the same time. The interpolated speed is calculated using any axes that have INTERP_FACTOR of 1, so at least one axis must be enabled and have a distance in the motion command otherwise the calculated speed will be zero and the command will complete immediately with no movement. The following two graphs illustrate how the SPEED is resolved along the path when both axes have an INTERP_FACTOR of 1, and how SPEED is resolved along the X axis when the Y axis has an INTERP_FACTOR of 0. INTERP_FACTOR only operates with MOVE , MOVEABS and MHELICAL (on the 3 rd axis) and their SP versions. All other motion commands require interpolated axes and so ignore this parameter.
examples:
INTERP_FACTOR AXIS(2) = 0
'Perform movement
BASE(0, 1, 2)
MOVEABS(x_offset, y_offset, z_offset)
WAIT IDLE
INTERP_FACTOR AXIS(2) = 1

name: INVERT_IN
description: The INVERT_IN command allows digital input channels to be individually inverted in software. This is important as these input channels can be assigned to activate functions such as feed-hold. When called with one parameter, the current inversion state of the specified input number is returned (either ON or OFF). When called with two parameters, the inversion state is set to that specified in the second parameter. The two-parameter form can also be used as a function, but it always returns TRUE (-1).
params: the input is inverted in software
examples:
INVERT_IN(7, ON)
FWD_JOG = 7

name: INVERT_STEP
description: INVERT_STEP is used to switch a hardware inverter into the stepper pulse output circuit. This can be necessary for connecting to some stepper drives. The electronic logic inside the Motion Coordinator stepper pulse generation assumes that the FALLING edge of the step output is the active edge which results in motor movement, and this is suitable for most stepper drives. INVERT_STEP should be set with WDOG = OFF. If the setting is incorrect, a stepper motor may lose position by one step when changing direction.
examples:
BASE(2)
INVERT_STEP = ON

name: IN_DEBOUNCE
description: The inputs go to the set state after the voltage has been either 24 or 0 for the IN_DEBOUNCE time in servo cycles. The selected debounce time is applied to all inputs when read in a program using the Motion iX IN function or the equivalent IN binding in the IEC 61131-3 language. Registration input functionality is not affected by the IN_DEBOUNCE value.
params:
The number of servo cycles for which the input must
  remain in a steady state for before the ON/OFF value is passed to a program.
examples:
IN_DEBOUNCE = 10
IN_DEBOUNCE = 12 'SERVO_PERIOD is 1000 so we expect 12 ms
WA(200)
TICKS = 0
OP(8, ON)
WAIT UNTIL IN(0) = ON
t = -TICKS
PRINT "HI: "; t[0]
WA(200)





TICKS = 0
OP(8, OFF)
WAIT UNTIL IN(0) = OFF
t = -TICKS
PRINT "LO: "; t[0]
IN_DEBOUNCE = 10

IN_DEBOUNCE = 12 'SERVO_PERIOD is 1000 so we expect 12 ms
WA(200)
TICKS = 0
OP(8, ON)
WAIT UNTIL IN(0) = ON
t = -TICKS
PRINT "HI: "; t[0]
WA(200)





TICKS = 0
OP(8, OFF)
WAIT UNTIL IN(0) = OFF
t = -TICKS
PRINT "LO: "; t[0]

name: IN_FILTER
description: IN_FILTER is used configure digital filtering on digital Inputs. Digital I/O refers to the 24 V inputs and outputs attached to the Motion Coordinator. Some controllers allow for a filter to be configured on these inputs. Filtering is only supported on MotionPLC controllers and MotionPLC Slices currently.
params: Input to return the value of/start of input group

Filtering range. The input state (ON/OFF) is registered
  only after it has remained stable for the duration of the filter time.
examples:
IN_FILTER(0, 5)
filter_8ms = 7


IN_FILTER(0, 4, filter_8ms)
IN_FILTER(0, 7, 0)
IN_FILTER(0, 5)

filter_8ms = 7


IN_FILTER(0, 4, filter_8ms)
IN_FILTER(0, 7, 0)

name: IN_POS
description: This axis parameter signals that the axis move has finished and that the axis is in position within defined limits of following error and speed. The IN_POS feature simplifies the checking required at end of moves to ensure a motor has reached position. Using IN_POS requires parameters IN_POS_DIST and IN_POS_SPEED to be set up first. The IN_POS_DIST and IN_POS_SPEED default to zero. Reaching zero position and speed error may take considerable time or not happen, depending on the servo configuration.
examples:
BASE(0) 


SERVO = ON 


UNITS = 100000 


SPEED = 5 


ACCEL = 50


DECEL = 50 





IN_POS_DIST = 0.002 


IN_POS_SPEED = 0.1 


REPEAT 


  TRIGGER 


  MOVE(1) 


  WAIT IN_POS 


  WA(50) 


UNTIL FALSE

name: IN_POS_DIST
description: IN_POS_DIST is a distance, scaled using the axis UNITS, indicating the required position error before IN_POS signals that the axis move has finished and that the axis is in position. IN_POS_DIST defaults to zero. Reaching zero position and speed error may take considerable time or not happen if this parameter is not set.
examples:
BASE(0) 


SERVO=ON 


UNITS=100000 


SPEED=5 


ACCEL=50


DECEL=50 





IN_POS_DIST=0.002 


IN_POS_SPEED=0.1 


REPEAT 


TRIGGER 


  MOVE(1) 


  WAIT IN_POS 


  WA(50) 


UNTIL FALSE

name: IN_POS_SPEED
description: This axis parameter is a speed, scaled using axis UNITS, indicating the required minimum measured speed before IN_POS signals that the axis move has finished and that the axis is in position. The IN_POS_SPEED defaults to zero. Reaching zero position and speed error may take considerable time or not happen if this parameter is not set.
examples:
BASE(0) 


SERVO=ON 


UNITS=100000 


SPEED=5 


ACCEL=50


DECEL=50 





IN_POS_DIST=0.002 


IN_POS_SPEED=0.1 


REPEAT 


TRIGGER 


  MOVE(1) 


  WAIT IN_POS 


  WA(50) 


UNTIL FALSE

name: IOMAP
description: Lists the current digital I/O map, analogue I/O map or full I/O map.

name: IOSIZE
description: Returns the total number of digital I/O channels, both real and virtual, which varies across controller types. The digital I/O is numbered from zero, so the valid range is 0 to IOSIZE - 1.
examples:
>>OP(IOSIZE – 1, ON)
>>

name: IO_STATUS
description: This command reads the status of a remote I/O device on EtherCAT. Status bit representation depends on the device implementation.
params: The slot which the EtherCAT I/O module is connected

Network address of the I/O device from which the status
  is read

Index of the 
VR
 where the status
  is stored
Index of the status being read (default 0)

name: IO_STATUSMASK
syntax:
value = IO_STATUSMASK(slot, address, read_write, vr_index or
mask value [, status index])
description: This command reads or writes the status mask of a remote Ethercat I/O device. With a status mask system, errors triggered by an IO_STATUS of a device can be masked out thus preventing a SYSTEM_ERROR . If the same bit is set in IO_STATUS and IO_STATUSMASK on the same device, a system error is triggered. Status bit representation depends on the device implementation.
params: The slot which the Ethercat I/O module is connected

Network address of the I/O device from which the status
  is read.

name: IP_ADDRESS
description: IP_ADDRESS is used to set the Ethernet IPv4 address of the main Ethernet port of the Motion Coordinator. This parameter uses the standard dot-decimal notation to define the 4 separate octets of the IP address that define the IP address’s 32-bit value. The value is held in flash EPROM and can be set in the MC_CONFIG script.
examples:
IP_ADDRESS = 192.168.0.250
'MC_CONFIG script file
IP_ADDRESS = 192.168.2.100

'MC_CONFIG script file
IP_ADDRESS = 192.168.2.100

name: IP_BIND_ADDRESS
description: IP_BIND_ADDRESS is used to set the Ethernet IPv4 UDP and TCPSERVER bind address for client connections. By default, when a TrioBASIC client connection is opened with the OPEN command, the local socket is bound to the source IP address, 0.0.0.0. This is a synonym for all current local IP addresses. When a UDP or TCPSERVER client is created, the OPEN statement only specifies one port, which is used to create both the source and destination sockets. If IP_BIND_ADDRESS is 0.0.0.0, then we cannot talk to applications that are running on the same device. To avoid this situation, we set IP_BIND_ADDRESS to a specific local IP address, for example 127.0.0.1. The default value is 0.0.0.0. Note that this parameter is applicable to PC-MCAT only.
examples: IP_BIND_ADDRESS = 192.168.0.250
IP_BIND_ADDRESS = 192.168.0.250

name: IP_GATEWAY
description: IP_GATEWAY is used to set the Ethernet network gateway address of the main Ethernet port of the Motion Coordinator. The Gateway is the IPv4 address of the internet access router on the factory network. It is only required if the Motion Coordinator is to be accessed via the internet. This parameter uses the standard dot (.) notation to define the 4 separate octets of the IP gateway address. The value is held in flash EPROM and can be set in the MC_CONFIG script.
examples:
IP_GATEWAY = 192.168.0.254
'MC_CONFIG script file
IP_GATEWAY = 192.168.0.254

'MC_CONFIG script file
IP_GATEWAY = 192.168.0.254

name: IP_MAC
description: IP_MAC returns the configured MAC address of the main Ethernet port of the Motion Coordinator. The MAC address is set once at manufacture and is unique to that controller. The value is held in flash EPROM and is normally read-only. If write access is available on older versions of firmware, do not change the MAC address under any circumstances without first consulting Trio.

name: IP_MEMORY_CONFIG
description: The MC464 Ethernet port has memory allocated to buffer the incoming and outgoing data telegrams. Each buffer page uses 1600 bytes of memory. If some ports are turned off using IP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG, then IP_MEMORY_CONFIG may be used to re-allocate the unused memory and give a larger buffer size to the incoming and outgoing data. By default, there are 2 x 1600 bytes allocated to Tx and 2 x 1600 allocated to Rx. The value of IP_MEMORY_CONFIG is $22 (or 2 + 32 in decimal), and in most networks this buffer size is enough to handle all the network traffic.
examples:
'Disable Ethernet IP and text file loader ports
IP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG = $37
'Allocate the freed memory space to Rx net-buffer
IP_MEMORY_CONFIG = $29

name: IP_NETMASK
description: IP_NETMASK is used to set the Ethernet IPv4 subnet mask of the main Ethernet port of the Motion Coordinator. This parameter uses the standard dot (.) notation to define the 4 separate octets of the IP subnet mask. The value is held in flash EPROM and can be set in the MC_CONFIG script.
examples:
IP_NETMASK = 255.255.255.0
'MC_CONFIG script file
IP_NETMASK = 255.255.255.0

'MC_CONFIG script file
IP_NETMASK = 255.255.255.0

name: IP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG
description: MC464: This Motion Coordinator is limited to 7 communication ports on Ethernet, IP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG allows the user to select which ports are in use and therefore reduce the load on the communications processor. By default, all ports except the transparent protocol text file loader port are enabled. It is recommended to use the MC4xx protocol which is enabled by default. All other Motion Coordinators in the MC4xx, MC5xx and MC6xx ranges; this parameter takes a value that is the number of Ethernet packets that are read at a time. The default value is 0, which allows the firmware to automatically decide the data size that is appropriate to efficient operation.
examples:
IP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG = 1 + 2 + 4 + 8 + 16 + 32 + 64
'or
IP_PROTOCOL_CONFIG = $7F

name: IP_PROTOCOL_CTRL
description: This parameter allows the user to disable the operation of one or more of the communication ports on Ethernet. If a bit is at 0, the port is enabled. If the bit is a 1, then the port is disabled and will not respond when a client tries to open it. It can be used to temporarily disable a port while a BASIC program configures the protocol settings on power up. The port will then be enabled in the program after the setup. By default all ports are enabled.

name: IP_TCP_TIMEOUT
description: IP_TCP_TIMEOUT defines the duration in milliseconds for which the TCP connections (Ethernet/IP, ModbusTCP, HMI, Token and Telnet) will stay open without any activity. When this is exceeded, the TCP connection will be closed by the controller. The default is 3600 seconds. The parameter must be in the MC_CONFIG to be effective.

name: IP_TCP_TX_THRESHOLD
description: IP_TCP_TX_THRESHOLD defines the number of bytes in the TCP socket transmit buffer which will trigger a telegram transmit. The default is 32. This value applies to all the TCP protocols.
examples: IP_TCP_TX_THRESHOLD = 16

name: IP_TCP_TX_TIMEOUT
description: IP_TCP_TX_TIMEOUT defines the period in milliseconds at which a TCP telegram will be transmitted after receiving the first byte if the number of bytes threshold is not reached. The default is 20 ms. This value applies to all the TCP protocols.
examples: IP_TCP_TX_TIMEOUT = 1000

name: IS_ENCRYPTION_KEY
description: The System Function, IS_ENCRYPTION_KEY returns either TRUE or FALSE to indicate that a USER or OEM key have been set in the controller. The type parameter selects which key type is being requested. Motion Perfect has tools for managing the Encryption Key.
examples:
' Is the USER encryption key set?
IF IS_ENCRYPTION_KEY(2)=FALSE THEN
PRINT "No USER Encryption Key set in this
controller"
ENDIF

' Is the USER encryption key set?
IF IS_ENCRYPTION_KEY(2)=FALSE THEN
PRINT "No USER Encryption Key set in this
controller"
ENDIF

name: IS_FILE
description: IS_FILE returns the state of whether a specific file exists or not within the project directory of the controller ( DIR ). The name of the file could be a string literal, a variable of string type and VRSTRING could also be used. IS_FILE and IS_PROG have the same function.
params:
TRUE (-1) if the file exists, 


  FALSE (0) if the file does not exists.
examples:
file_status = IS_FILE "MAIN_TASK"
DIM textfile_name AS STRING(12)
textfile_name = "textfile_no1"
IF IS_FILE textfile_name THEN
DEL textfile_name
ENDIF
file_status = IS_FILE "MAIN_TASK"

DIM textfile_name AS STRING(12)
textfile_name = "textfile_no1"
IF IS_FILE textfile_name THEN
DEL textfile_name
ENDIF

name: IS_INF
description: Check the value of a stored variable. Tests to see if the value is a floating-point infinite number and therefore likely to cause an expression to generate a BASIC Error.
examples:
IF IS_INF(TABLE(3000)) THEN
PRINT "TABLE(3000) is Infinity"
ENDIF
FOR i = 0 TO 4095
IF IS_INF(VR(i)) THEN
VR(i) = 0
ENDIF
NEXT i

IF IS_INF(TABLE(3000)) THEN
PRINT "TABLE(3000) is Infinity"
ENDIF

FOR i = 0 TO 4095
IF IS_INF(VR(i)) THEN
VR(i) = 0
ENDIF
NEXT i

name: IS_NAN
description: Check an expression to test if it is a NaN (not a number, a bit pattern that does not represent a valid floating-point value). If a battery backed memory loses its battery power, the bit pattern will be random and the NaN will cause a BASIC error. NaN values can also be set from an external source like a PC or PLC setting values over a fieldbus.
examples:
FOR i = 0 TO 65535
IF IS_NAN(VR(i)) THEN
VR(i) = 0
ENDIF
NEXT i

FOR i = 0 TO 65535
IF IS_NAN(VR(i)) THEN
VR(i) = 0
ENDIF
NEXT i

name: IS_OPEN
description: IS_OPEN returns the state of the selected channel. The channel may be a communications port, a file or a temporary buffer. IS_OPEN state TRUE does not imply that the Ethernet connection is valid and open, it only refers to the channel in the controller firmware so as to find out if a channel number is already in use.
params:
TRUE (-1) if channel is open, 


  FALSE (0) if the channel is closed.
The channel number using the # channel notation
examples:
DIM comm_status AS BOOLEAN
comm_status = IS_OPEN #20
IF FALSE = IS_OPEN #21 THEN
OPEN #21 AS "TCP:192.168.0.230(1000)" FOR READ_WRITE
WA(20)
ENDIF

DIM comm_status AS BOOLEAN
comm_status = IS_OPEN #20

IF FALSE = IS_OPEN #21 THEN
OPEN #21 AS "TCP:192.168.0.230(1000)" FOR READ_WRITE
WA(20)
ENDIF

name: IS_PROG

The file/program exists and it is open for reading.
The file/program does exist and it is open for writing.

The file/program does exist and it is open for both
  reading and writing.
Optional path name for internal memory.
Path name for optional SD card.

Path is RAM or not applied; filename with no extension.


  

Path is SD; the full filename with extension.  e.g.
  file1.txt, program1.bas etc.
