NEXT a





SPEED=10


MERGE=ON


LIMIT_BUFFERED=16


ACC(SPEED*20)


TRIGGER
MOVE_PB_CONT=1





MOVE_PB=10


MOVE_PB.12=ON


MOVE_PB_IDLE=10


MOVE_PB_IDLE.12=OFF
MOVE(20)


WAIT IDLE


WA(2000)


MOVE(20)


WA(2000)


SPEED=0


MOVE(20)


WAIT IDLE


WA(2000)


MOVE(10)

FOR a=0 TO 1


    UNITS AXIS(a)=10


NEXT a





SPEED=10


MERGE=ON


LIMIT_BUFFERED=16


ACC(SPEED*20)


TRIGGER
MOVE_PB_CONT=1





MOVE_PB=10


MOVE_PB.12=ON


MOVE_PB_IDLE=10
MOVE_PB_IDLE.12=OFF
MOVE(20)


WAIT IDLE


WA(2000)


MOVE(20)


WAIT IDLE


WA(2000)


MOVE(10)

FOR a=0 TO 1


    UNITS AXIS(a)=10


NEXT a





SPEED=10


MERGE=ON


LIMIT_BUFFERED=16


ACC(SPEED*20)


TRIGGER
MOVE_PB_CONT=1





MOVE_PB=10


MOVE_PB.12=ON


MOVE_PB_IDLE=10


MOVE_PB_IDLE.12=OFF
MOVE(20)


WAIT IDLE


WA(2000)


MOVE(20)


WA(2000)


SPEED=0


MOVE(20)


WAIT IDLE


WA(2000)


MOVE(10)

name: MPE
description: Sets the type of channel handshaking to be performed on the command line. This is normally only used by the Motion Perfect program, but can be used for user applications with the PCMotion ActiveX control in asynchronous mode. There is only one Command Line process. If Trio PCMotion connects and uses the Motion Perfect command line port, then Motion Perfect will not be able to connect at the same time.
params:
No protocol.  Motion Perfect is in Direct Mode or not
  connected.

Motion Perfect version 3 connected in either tool or
  synchronised mode.
Motion Perfect version 4 connected in tool mode.
Motion Perfect version 4 connected in synchronised mode.
examples:
IF MPE = 0 THEN
' OK to reset the controller
EX
ELSE
PRINT #5,"Cannot reset when Motion Perfect is
connected"
ENDIF

name: MPE_MODE
description: Read the connection mode in current use on the command line process. This is normally only used by Motion Perfect, but can be used for user applications.
params:
No protocol.  Motion Perfect is in Direct Mode or not
  connected.

Motion Perfect version 3 connected in either tool or
  synchronised mode.
Motion Perfect version 4 connected in tool mode.
Motion Perfect version 4 connected in synchronised mode.
examples:
IF MPE_MODE = 0 THEN
' OK to reset the controller
EX
ELSE
PRINT #5,"Cannot reset when Motion Perfect is connected"
ENDIF

name: MPOS
description: This parameter is the position of the axis as measured by the encoder or resolver. Unless using an absolute encoder MPOS is reset to 0 on power up or software reset. The value is adjusted using the DEFPOS command or OFFPOS axis parameter to shift the datum position or when the REP_DIST is in operation. The position is reported in user UNITS .
examples:
WAIT UNTIL MPOS >= 1250
SPEED = 2.5

name: MPOS_DELAY
description: Reserved Keyword

name: MPOS_PRE_COMP
description: Reserved Keyword

name: MSPEED
description: MSPEED can be used to represent the speed measured as it represents the change in measured position in user UNITS (per second) in the last servo period. This value represents a snapshot of the speed and significant fluctuations can occur, particularly at low speeds. MPSEEDF can be used to obtain a filtered version of MSPEED.

name: MSPEEDF
description: MSPEEDF is the value of MSPEED after a low pass filter has been applied. The filter helps to remove jitter and noise from the value which could otherwise produce misleading results when MSPEED is read in a program. MSPEEDF represents the average speed measured as a change in measured position in user UNITS (per second).
examples:
BASE(3)
MSPEED_FILTER = 0.5 'Initialise the filter coefficient
WHILE TRUE
IF MSPEEDF > 5 THEN
GOSUB start_main
ENDIF
...
WEND

name: MSPEED_FILTER
description: MPSEED_FILTER is the filter coefficient value for use by MSPEEDF . MSPEED_FILTER can take any value between 0 and 1.0. Lower values mean longer time constant and therefore greater filtering effect. When set to 1, the MSPEEDF value will be the same as MSPEED . The filter equation approximates to the following and is processed once per SERVO_PERIOD . MSPEEDF = (1 - MSPEED_FILTER) * MSPEEDF + MSPEED_FILTER * MSPEED
examples:
BASE(3)
MSPEED_FILTER = 0.5 'Initialise the filter coefficient
WHILE TRUE
IF MSPEEDF > 5 THEN
GOSUB start_main
ENDIF
...
WEND

name: MSPHERICAL
syntax: MSPHERICAL({parameters}, mode[, gtpi[, rotau[, rotav[, rotaw]]]])
description: Moves the three axis group defined in BASE along a spherical path with a vector speed determined by the SPEED set in the first axis of the BASE array. There are 2 modes of operation with the option of finishing the move at an endpoint different to the start or returning to the start point to complete a circle. The path of the movement in 3D space can be defined either by specifying a point somewhere along the path, or by specifying the centre of the sphere.
params: Specify end point and mid-point on curve.
Specify end point and centre of sphere.

Two mid-point are specified, and the curve completes a
  full circle.

Mid-point on curve and centre of sphere are specified
  and the curve completes a full circle.

0 = MOVE; 1 = MOVETURN


  

Replace MOVE with MOVETURN

0 = MOVETURN mode 5; 1 = MOVETURN mode 6


  

Only applicable if bit 11 set

If this optional parameter is non-zero, modes 0 and 1
  will perform a move taking the opposite way around a 360-degree circle to the
  same endpoint.

If this optional parameter is non-zero, a 4
th
  axis will perform linear interpolation at the same time as the spherical
  move. The axis is the next in the BASE sequence. The move distance does not
  affect the path length or time taken for the movement. The path length is
  calculated just from the spherical distance.

If this optional parameter is non-zero, a 5
th
  axis will perform linear interpolation at the same time as the spherical
  move.

If this optional parameter is non-zero, a 6
th
  axis will perform linear interpolation at the same time as the spherical
  move.
examples:
radius = 30 'sphere radius


angle = 0   'radians





startx = 0


starty = 0


startz = 0





endx = startx + radius * COS(angle)


endy = starty + radius * SIN(angle)


endz = startz + radius





midx = (endx - startx) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midy = (endy - starty) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midz = (endz - startz) * SIN(PI / 4)
BASE(3,4,5)
MSPHERICAL(endx, endy, endz, midx, midy, midz, 0)
radius = 30    'sphere radius


angle = PI/4   'radians 





startx = 0


starty = 0


startz = 0





endx = startx + radius * COS(angle)


endy = starty + radius * SIN(angle)


endz = startz + radius





midx = (endx - startx) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midy = (endy - starty) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midz = (endz - startz) * SIN(PI / 4)
BASE(0, 1, 2)
MSPHERICAL(endx, endy, endz, midx, midy, midz, 0)

radius = 30 'sphere radius


angle = 0   'radians





startx = 0


starty = 0


startz = 0





endx = startx + radius * COS(angle)


endy = starty + radius * SIN(angle)


endz = startz + radius





midx = (endx - startx) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midy = (endy - starty) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midz = (endz - startz) * SIN(PI / 4)
BASE(3,4,5)
MSPHERICAL(endx, endy, endz, midx, midy, midz, 0)

radius = 30    'sphere radius


angle = PI/4   'radians 





startx = 0


starty = 0


startz = 0





endx = startx + radius * COS(angle)


endy = starty + radius * SIN(angle)


endz = startz + radius





midx = (endx - startx) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midy = (endy - starty) * (1 - COS(PI / 4))


midz = (endz - startz) * SIN(PI / 4)
BASE(0, 1, 2)
MSPHERICAL(endx, endy, endz, midx, midy, midz, 0)

name: MSPHERICALSP
syntax: MSPHERICAL({parameters}, mode[, gtpi[, rotau[, rotav[, rotaw]]]])
description: Performs a spherical move the same as MSPHERICAL and additionally allows vector speed to be changed when using multiple moves in the look ahead buffer when MERGE = ON , using additional parameters FORCE_SPEED , ENDMOVE_SPEED and STARTMOVE_SPEED
examples:
BASE(3, 4, 5)
FORCE_SPEED = 30
ENDMOVE_SPEED = 30
MSPHERICALSP(30, 0, 30, 8.7868, 0, 21.2132, 0)

name: MS_BUS
description: The command MS_BUS is used to perform advanced operations on the MS-Bus network. In normal use the MS-Bus network will start automatically without the need for any commands in a startup program. MS_BUS command functions may be useful when debugging and setting up a MS-Bus system.
params: Set to MS-Bus port slot number

name: MS_BUS_CONFIG
description: This 2D array can be used to configure slices during initialisation of the MS-Bus network. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the type of slice setting, the second dimension is the position of the slice within that master network.
params: Type of register to configure on power on

Configure digital output state if a slice goes into an
  error state
Configure digital input filter for a slice
Configure Analogue input range for a slice

Address determined by the position of the node on the MS-Bus
  network.  The first node is address 0.

name: MS_BUS_IO
description: The command MS_BUS_IO is used to setup slices on a MS-Bus network. The MS_BUS_IO command returns TRUE (-1) if successful and FALSE (0) if the command execution was in error. Functions which return a value must either put the value in a VR or print it to the current output terminal.
params: Set default output mode when slice is in error
Set to MS-Bus port slot number

name: MTYPE
description: This parameter holds the type of move currently being executed. This parameter may be interrogated to determine whether a move has finished or if a transition from one move type to another has taken place. A non-idle move type does not necessarily mean that the axis is moving. It may be at zero speed part way along a move or interpolating with another axis without moving itself. It takes a servo period before a motion command is loaded into the buffer, so checking MTYPE immediately after a motion command will probably fail. You should use WAIT LOADED or WAIT IDLE to check that a command is loaded or complete
examples:
IF MTYPE AXIS(2) = 2 THEN


  OP(1, ON)
ELSE
OP(1, OFF)
ENDIF

name: *  Multiply
description: Multiplies expression1 by expression2

name: NEG_OFFSET
description: For Piezo Motor Control. This sets an offset to the DAC output when the position loop is demanding a negative voltage output. NEG_OFFSET is applied after DAC_SCALE so is always a value appropriate to the D to A converter resolution. The negative offset must be a negative value.
examples:
NEG_OFFSET = -328
POS_OFFSET = 450
NEG_OFFSET = -395

name: NEW
description: Deletes a program or table from the controller memory. If you are deleting a program from within a TrioBASIC program it is recommended to use the DEL command as makes easier to read code. When deleting the table all the values are set to 0 Do not delete programs when connected to Motion Perfect as it will cause a controller mismatch and you will be disconnected.
examples:
>>NEW "NAMEDPROGRAM"
OK
>>
>>NEW "TABLE"
OK
>>

>>NEW "NAMEDPROGRAM"
OK
>>

>>NEW "TABLE"
OK
>>

name: NIN
description: This parameter returns the number of inputs fitted to the system. The value is set by the firmware taking into consideration the total I/O detected, including module I/O, CAN I/O, Fieldbus I/O and CANopen I/O.

name: NIO
description: This parameter returns the number of inputs/outputs fitted to the system. The value is set by the firmware taking into consideration the total I/O detected; including module I/O, CAN I/O, Fieldbus I/O and CANopen I/O. Inputs/Outputs outside of NIO can be used as virtual

name: NODE_AIO
description: This 2D array can be used to set the start address of any EtherCAT node analogue I/O channels. This can be used to define a user specific I/O map to fix I/O points from different sources in place. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network.
examples:
NODE_AIO(0, 5) = 0
NODE_AIO(0, 5) = 40

name: NODE_AXIS
description: This 2D array can be used to over-ride the drive addressing of any EtherCAT node axis. This can be used to define a user specific axis map to fix axes from different sources in place. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network. An error is raised if the axis requested is already in use when the EtherCAT protocol is started.

name: NODE_AXIS_COUNT
description: This 2D array can be used to set the number of axes that are located at a single EtherCAT node. This can be used to define a user specific axis map when using multi-axis drives. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network.

name: NODE_AXIS_MAP
description: This 3D array can be used to over-ride the drive to controller axis mapping for multi-axis drives of any EtherCAT sub-device. This can be used to define a user specific axis map to fix axes from different sources in place. The array is 3-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network, the third dimension is the axis index in the drive starting from 0. An error is raised if the axis requested is already in use when the EtherCAT protocol is started.

name: NODE_INDEX
description: This 2D array can be used to set the pointer to a block of VRs used by the EtherCAT node. It can be used to define a user specific Input Output map from different data sources including Boolean and Integer data within the EtherCAT node. There is one VR mapped per PDO object, starting with the values from slave to master, (e.g. slave actual values, DIN, status word, actual position, etc.) then the values from master to slave (e.g. slave target values, DOUT, control word, target position, etc.) The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network.
params:
The slot number of the EtherCAT port. (0 on MC4N, MC6N
  and Flex-6 Nano)

Address determined by the position of the node on the
  EtherCAT network. The first node is address 0.

EtherCAT cyclic data is mapped to a block of VRs
  starting at this VR index. (MC664, MC6N, Flex-6 Nano)

EtherCAT cyclic data is mapped to a block of VRs
  starting at this VR index. (MC4N)
examples:
NODE_INDEX(0, 0) = 0


NODE_INDEX(0, 1) = 20

name: NODE_IO
description: This 2D array can be used to set the start address of any EtherCAT node I/O channels. This can be used to define a user specific I/O map to fix I/O points from different sources in place. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network. The default I/O allocation order is: Controller I/O, Module I/O, CAN I/O, EtherCAT I/O (both drive I/O and independent EtherCAT I/O) When using multiple I/O types, it is best to configure the I/O order with the I/O base modifier parameters. See below.
params:
The slot number of the EtherCAT port.  (0 on MC4N, MC6N
  and Flex-6 Nano)

Address determined by the position of the node on the
  EtherCAT network.  The first node is address 0.

EtherCAT I/O for the defined Node starts at this I/O
  point address
examples:
MODULEIO_BASE = 0: NODE_IO(0, 5) = 0: CANIO_BASE = 0
MODULEIO_BASE = 0: NODE_IO(0, 5) = 72: CANIO_BASE = 0
MODULEIO_BASE = 144: NODE_IO(0, 5) = 0: CANIO_BASE = 0

name: NODE_PROFILE
description: This 2D array is used to set the EtherCAT profile within the internal database to use the selected profile. Each profile gives extra functionality and is vendor and product code specific. Consult the extra technical notes made available for your connected slave device. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within that master network.
params:
The slot number of the EtherCAT port. (0 on MC4N, MC6N
  and Flex-6 Nano)

Address determined by the position of the node on the
  EtherCAT network.  The first node is address 0.

name: NODE_SYNC_OFFSET
description: This 2D array can be used to set the EtherCAT Sync0 offset time for any node on the network. This can be used to define a specific offset that applies to the defined node, leaving all other nodes at the default Sync0 offset value. The default Sync0 offset may also be defined using ETHERCAT function $91. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the position of the node within the master network.
examples:
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 2) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 3) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 4) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 5) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 0) = 0
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 1) = 0
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 2) = 0
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 3) = -70000

NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 2) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 3) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 4) = 200000
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 5) = 200000

NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 0) = 0
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 1) = 0
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 2) = 0
NODE_SYNC_OFFSET(0, 3) = -70000

name: NOP
description: This parameter returns the number of outputs fitted to the system. The value is set by the firmware taking into consideration the total I/O detected, including module I/O, CAN I/O, Fieldbus I/O and CANopen I/O.

name: NOT
description: The NOT operator inverts all the bits of the supplied integer value. When supplied with a floating-point value, this is first truncated to an integer value. Be wary when using NOT with bit values 0 and 1, as while NOT 0 will work as expected (returning TRUE (because NOT 0 is $FFFFFFFF (-1 in decimal)), NOT 1 will not return the expected 0 (FALSE), instead returning -2 (which is not FALSE) , because NOT 1 is $FFFFFFFE in hexadecimal (-2 in decimal).
examples:
>>PRINT 7 AND NOT 1.5
6
IF NOT CAN(0, 9, 13, 1, 8, $6060, 0, $02) THEN
PRINT #user, "Failed to set velocity mode"
STOP
ENDIF

>>PRINT 7 AND NOT 1.5
6

IF NOT CAN(0, 9, 13, 1, 8, $6060, 0, $02) THEN
PRINT #user, "Failed to set velocity mode"
STOP
ENDIF

name: NTYPE
description: This parameter holds the type of the first buffered move. The NTYPE buffer can be cleared using CANCEL (1)
examples:
IF NTYPE = 0 THEN
MOVE( TABLE(count))
count = count + 1
ENDIF
IF LOADED THEN
MOVE( TABLE(count))
count = count + 1
ENDIF
>>?NTYPE
2
>>?MTYPE
1
>>?PMOVE PROC(14)


0
>>

IF NTYPE = 0 THEN
MOVE( TABLE(count))
count = count + 1
ENDIF
IF LOADED THEN
MOVE( TABLE(count))
count = count + 1
ENDIF

>>?NTYPE
2
>>?MTYPE
1
>>?PMOVE PROC(14)


0
>>

name: NW_NODE_AIO
description: This 2D array can be used to set the start address of any EtherCAT node analogue I/O channels. This can be used to define a user specific I/O map to fix I/O points from different sources in place. The array is 2-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the Configured Station Alias address of the node within that master network. When using multiple I/O types, it is best to configure the I/O order with the I/O base modifier parameters. See NODE_IO.
examples:
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 1) = 10
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 2) = 12
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 3) = 14
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 4) = 16
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 5) = 18
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 6) = 20
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 7) = 22
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 8) = 24
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 9) = 26
NW_NODE_AIO(0, 10) = 28

name: NW_NODE_AXIS_MAP
description: This 3D array can be used to over-ride the drive to controller axis mapping for multi axis drives of any EtherCAT subdevice. This can be used to define a user specific axis map to fix axes from different sources in place. The array is 3-dimensional, the first dimension is the master slot identifier, the second dimension is the address of the device. This is usually set on the device using front panel switches or written into EEPROM using MotionPerfect. The third dimension is the axis index in the drive starting from 0. An error is raised if the axis requested is already in use when the EtherCAT protocol is started.

name: NW_NODE_INDEX
description: This 2D array can be used to set the pointer to a block of VRs used by the EtherCAT node. It can be used to define a user specific Input Output map from different data sources including Boolean and Integer data within the EtherCAT node. There is one VR mapped per PDO object, starting with the values from slave to master, ( eg slave actual values, DIN, status word, actual position etc.) then the values from master to slave ( eg slave target values, DOUT, control word, target position etc.)
params:
The slot number of the EtherCAT port.  (0 on MC4N, MC6N
  and Flex-6 Nano)
The configured alias address of the slave device.

EtherCAT cyclic data is mapped to a block of VRs
  starting at this VR index. (MC664, MC6N, Flex-6 Nano)

EtherCAT cyclic data is mapped to a block of VRs
  starting at this VR index. (MC4N)
examples: NW_NODE_INDEX(0, 4000) = 1000

name: NW_NODE_IO
description: This 2D array can be used to set the start address of any EtherCAT node I/O channels. This can be used to define a user specific I/O map to fix I/O points from different sources in place. The default I/O allocation order is; Controller I/O, Module I/O, CAN I/O, EtherCAT I/O (both drive I/O and independent EtherCAT I/O) When using multiple I/O types, it is best to configure the I/O order with the I/O base modifier parameters. See NODE_IO.
params:
The slot number of the EtherCAT port.  (0 on MC4N, MC6N
  and Flex-6 Nano)
The configured alias address of the slave device.

EtherCAT I/O for the defined Node starts at this I/O
  point address
examples:
NW_NODE_IO(0, 1) = 32
NW_NODE_IO(0, 2) = 48
NW_NODE_IO(0, 3) = 64
NW_NODE_IO(0, 4) = 80
NW_NODE_IO(0, 5) = 96

name: NW_NODE_PROFILE
description: This is an array system parameter which can be used in MC_CONFIG. The parameter is used to define which profile number should be mapped to a particular EtherCAT slave. The EtherCAT protocol startup will fail if the selected profile is not available or is out of range. The NW_NODE_PROFILE profile ID values are initialised to zero, because any profile ID value will only be used if a slave device has been setup with the same non-zero configured station alias. Zero valued station alias addresses are not used by the EtherCAT protocol. If set to non-zero, the use of NODE_PROFILE for the same slave device will be used in preference to a NW_NODE_PROFILE.
params:
The slot number of the EtherCAT port. (0 on MC4N, MC6N
  and Flex-6 Nano)
examples:
NW_NODE_PROFILE(0, 12) = 2
EtherCAT State: Init (0)
EtherCAT State: Init (0)
EtherCAT State: Pre-Operational (0)
EtherCAT State: Safe-Operational (0)
EtherCAT State: Operational (0)
EtherCAT Configuration (0):
P600: 0 : 0 : 2000
P366 Coupler: 1 : 0 : 2001
P375 3 Axes Stepper Output: 2 : 12 : 12 (11:2:0,12:2:0,13:2:0)
? nw_node_profile(0,12)
2

name: NW_TIMEOUT
description: NW_TIMEOUT is a slot based system parameter used to define the EtherCAT network timeout parameters during network startup. A timeout value can be set for each phase of the startup sequence or for the overall EtherCAT startup.
params: The slot index for the EtherCAT port.
Identifies which timeout parameter to configure.
examples: NW_TIMEOUT(0, 2) = 4000

name: N_ANA_IN
description: This parameter returns the number of analogue input channels available to the Motion Coordinator. This includes all built in and external inputs.
examples:
>>PRINT N_ANA_IN
10
>>

name: N_ANA_OUT
description: This parameter returns the number of analogue output channels available to the controller.
examples:
>>PRINT N_ANA_OUT
12
>>

name: <>  Not Equal
description: Returns TRUE if <expression-1> is not equal to <expression-2>, otherwise returns FALSE .
examples: IF MTYPE <> 0 THEN GOSUB scoop

name: OFF
description: OFF returns the value 0
examples:
IF IN(56) = OFF THEN GOSUB tiger
WDOG = OFF
IF IN(56) = OFF THEN GOSUB tiger
WDOG = OFF

name: OFFPOS
description: The OFFPOS parameter allows the axis position value to be offset by any amount without affecting the motion that is in progress. OFFPOS can therefore be used to effectively datum a system at full speed. Values loaded into the OFFPOS axis parameter are reset to 0 by the system software after the axis position is changed. OFFPOS can be read to detect when a DEFPOS command has completed.
examples:
>>PRINT DPOS
300.0000
>>OFFPOS = 125
>>PRINT DPOS
425.0000
>>
OFFPOS = -DPOS 'This is equivalent to DEFPOS(0)
BASE(conv)
REGIST(3)
WAIT UNTIL MARK
OFFPOS = -REG_POS 'Leading edge of box is now zero

>>PRINT DPOS
300.0000
>>OFFPOS = 125
>>PRINT DPOS
425.0000
>>
OFFPOS = -DPOS 'This is equivalent to DEFPOS(0)

BASE(conv)
REGIST(3)
WAIT UNTIL MARK
OFFPOS = -REG_POS 'Leading edge of box is now zero

name: ON
description: ON returns the value 1.
examples: OP(lever, ON)

name: ON.. GOSUB/ GOTO
syntax:
ON expression GOxxx label[, label2[, ...]]


...


label1:


commands


RETURN


...


label2:


commands


RETURN


Where GOxxx can be 
GOSUB
 or 
GOTO
description: The expression is evaluated and then the integer part is used to select a label from the list. If the expression has the value 1 then the first label is used, 2 then the second label is used, and so on. Once a label is selected it is used with either GOSUB or GOTO If the value of the expression is less than 1 or greater than the number of labels the command is stepped through with no action. Once the label is selected a GOSUB is performed.
params:
Any valid TrioBASIC expression, should return a value 1
  or greater
TrioBASIC statements that you wish to execute
A valid label that occurs in the program.
examples:
REPEAT
GET #3, char
UNTIL char >= 1 AND char <= 3
ON char GOSUB mover, stopper, change
ON (IN(4, 6) + 1) GOTO prog0, prog1, prog2, prog3, prog 'Select
program
GOTO continue 'Skip progs if unknown input selected
prog0:
RUN "tuning", 2
GOTO continue
prog1:
RUN "cutting", 2
GOTO continue
prog2:
RUN "packing", 2
GOTO continue
prog3:
RUN "moving", 2
GOTO continue
Prog4:
RUN "lifting", 2
GOTO continue
continue:
...
DIM a AS INTEGER





a = 2





ON a GOSUB one, two, three


PRINT "Done"





STOP 'Prevents running into subroutines when machine stopped.





one:


PRINT "1"


RETURN





two:


PRINT "2"


RETURN





three:


PRINT "3"


RETURN
ON BASICERROR GOTO error_routine
'(rest of program)
'Run time error handling
error_routine:
IF RUN_ERROR <> 31 THEN
'If there is a run time error then stop the machine as
per a program stop
RAPIDSTOP(2)
'Print the run error if the program is not stopping
normally
PRINT "RUN_ERROR = "; RUN_ERROR[0],
"ERROR_LINE = "; ERROR_LINE[0]
ENDIF
STOP

REPEAT
GET #3, char
UNTIL char >= 1 AND char <= 3
ON char GOSUB mover, stopper, change

ON (IN(4, 6) + 1) GOTO prog0, prog1, prog2, prog3, prog 'Select
program
GOTO continue 'Skip progs if unknown input selected
prog0:
RUN "tuning", 2
GOTO continue
prog1:
RUN "cutting", 2
GOTO continue
prog2:
RUN "packing", 2
GOTO continue
prog3:
RUN "moving", 2
GOTO continue
Prog4:
RUN "lifting", 2
GOTO continue
continue:
...

DIM a AS INTEGER





a = 2





ON a GOSUB one, two, three


PRINT "Done"





STOP 'Prevents running into subroutines when machine stopped.





one:


PRINT "1"


RETURN





two:


PRINT "2"


RETURN





three:


PRINT "3"


RETURN

ON BASICERROR GOTO error_routine
'(rest of program)
'Run time error handling
error_routine:
IF RUN_ERROR <> 31 THEN
'If there is a run time error then stop the machine as
per a program stop
RAPIDSTOP(2)
'Print the run error if the program is not stopping
normally
PRINT "RUN_ERROR = "; RUN_ERROR[0],
"ERROR_LINE = "; ERROR_LINE[0]
ENDIF
STOP

name: OP
description: Sets output(s) and allows the state of the first 32 outputs to be read back. Digital I/O normally refers to the 24 V inputs and outputs attached to the Motion Coordinator. I/O points that do not have hardware attached can be used as "Virtual" outputs and inputs that can be set with OP and read back with IN. There are four modes of operation for the OP command, using up to three parameters: Read Base Block Write Base Block Set Single Output Write Block
examples:
OP(44, 1)
OP(44, ON)
OP(18 << 8)
read_output:
VR(0) = OP
'Clear 0-7 and 16-32
VR(0) = VR(0) AND $0000FF00
'Set $1A42 in outputs 16-32,
'8-15 will remain in their original state
VR(0) = VR(0) OR $1A420000
OP(VR(0))
OP(10, 13, $F)

read_output:
VR(0) = OP
'Clear 0-7 and 16-32
VR(0) = VR(0) AND $0000FF00
'Set $1A42 in outputs 16-32,
'8-15 will remain in their original state
VR(0) = VR(0) OR $1A420000
OP(VR(0))
OP(10, 13, $F)

name: OPEN
description: OPEN provides access to a file on the controller. The text file can be initialised as a file that Motion Perfect can synchronise with, a temporary file, a file on the SD card or as a FIFO buffer. All files are in the controller file directory however only a text file can be viewed or edited in Motion Perfect. Once the file has been opened then it can be manipulated by the standard TrioBASIC channel commands. If the file is opened with read access then any TrioBASIC GET type commands such as GET , INPUT , LINPUT and KEY can be used on the channel. If the file is opened with write access, then the PRINT type commands can be used on the channel. The channel should be closed using TrioBASIC command CLOSE when you have finished with it.
params:
The 
TrioBASIC error
 that
  occurred during the file open operation. If there is no error, then value is
  0.


  

If you are opening TCP or UDP, the value returned is
  True (-1) when successful, otherwise False (0).

The TrioBASIC 
#
 channel to be
  associated with the file. The channel range depends on the type of file:


  

40 to 48 for files and string variables. 


  

20 to 29 for TCP and UDP multi-client connections on
  ARM base controllers. (Firmware version 2.0256 onwards).

A file stored in the controller’s file system subject
  to the OUTPUT(mode) specified (see below)
A file stored in the controller’s RAM (volatile)

A file stored on the controller’s SD-card
  (non-volatile)

A file stored on a USB flash memory stick, plugged into
  one of the controller’s USB sockets (Flex-7 only, non-volatile)
The IP address and port number of a TCP connection
The IP address and port number of a UDP connection
The port number of the TCP server

For files and string variables (channels 40 to 48),
  this is the name of the file to be opened, for example, “data_points”,
  “SD:process_log”, “RAM:temp_data”, or “STRING:my_var”.


  

For TCP and DGRAM connections (channels 20 to 29), the
  IP address is in dot notation and port number is enclosed in brackets, for
  example, “TCP:192.168.8.152(3000)”, or “DGRAM:192.168.0.250(1000)”.


  

For TCP servers (channels 20 to 29), only the port
  number is required, enclosed in brackets, for example, “TCPSERVER:(790)”.
The operations permitted on the file.

The file will be opened for reading. When the end of
  the file is reached 
KEY
 will return 
FALSE
, and the 
GET
 and 
INPUT
 functions will fail.

The file will be opened for writing. If the file does
  not exist, then it will be created. If the file already exists, then it will
  be cleared.

Opens a non-volatile text file that 
Motion
  Perfect can read, edit, and save into the project. The file is synchronised
  with the PC directory as the rest of the Motion Perfect files.

Opens a non-volatile text file that is only accessible
  by the controller. The file is not synchronised, but it can be potentially
  added to the project with Motion Perfect.

Opens a temporary file (RAM) that is only accessible by
  the controller.

Opens a non-volatile text file only accessible by the
  controller.
The channel will be opened for read/write operations.

The file will be opened for writing. The file must
  exist. All data is written to the end of the file.

The file will be opened for reading and will be managed
  as a circular buffer. This is only valid for files stored in internal RAM.


  

mode defaults to zero. If mode is 0 then the KEY
  command will return the number of characters in the FIFO. If mode is 1 then
  the 
KEY
 command will return the number of lines in the
  FIFO.


  

terminator defaults to 13 (carriage return). If mode is
  1 and this character is added to the FIFO then the line count will be
  incremented by 1. If mode is 1 and this character is removed from the FIFO
  then the line count is decremented by 1.

The file will be opened for writing and will be managed
  as a circular buffer. This is only valid for files in internal RAM. If the
  file does not exist it will be created (size) bytes long (free memory
  permitting). Note that the minimum size created will be 256 bytes.


  

If the file does exist then it must be of type FIFO, the
  size parameter is ignored, and the contents are cleared.


  

If mode bit 0 is set, then the FIFO will be opened in
  line mode. If mode bit 1 is set, then the FIFO will be opened in non-blocking
  mode, which will allow the FIFO to overwrite. If the FIFO is in non-blocking
  and line modes, then when a line overflows it will clear the next line, up to
  the terminator character.


  

If the FIFO is in line mode, then terminator contains
  the end of line characters.

Open a file in RAM for output with an explicit program
  type.

0 => Trio BASIC


  

 1 => Trio BASIC Encrypted


  

 2 => G CODE


  

 3 => TEXT


  

 4 => IEC


  

 5 => FIFO


  

 6 => TEMP


  

 7 => PROJECT


  

 8 => BINARY


  

 9 => MC_CONFIG


  

10 => IEC Library


  

11 => HMI


  

12 => Trio BASIC Library


  

13 => Trio BASIC Library Encrypted


  

14 => HMI Library


  

15 => Volatile text (Not preserved across controller
  restarts)


  

16 => ROBOT


  

17 => ROBOT Trio BASIC


  

18 => TEMP TEXT
examples:
IF (OPEN #40 AS "SD:product_log.txt" FOR
OUTPUT(0)) = 0 THEN
PRINT #40, DATE$ 'Print the date
PRINT #40, products_complete[0]; " products
completed"
PRINT #40, product_failures[0]; " products
failed"
CLOSE #40
ELSE
PRINT "Error opening product log"
ENDIF
OPEN #41 AS "gcodeprogram" FOR OUTPUT(1)
WHILE file_downloading
IF KEY #1
GET #1, char
PRINT #41, char;
ENDIF
Length = length + 1
WEND
CLOSE #41
OPEN #41 AS "gcodeprogram" FOR INPUT
OPEN #42 AS "gcodefifo" FOR FIFO_WRITE(length)
WHILE KEY #41
GET #41, char
PRINT #42, char;
WEND
CLOSE #42
CLOSE #41
OPEN #20 AS "DGRAM:192.168.0.250(1000)" FOR READ_WRITE
CHANNEL_READ(20, strInput)
CLOSE #20
OPEN #21 AS "TCP:192.168.8.152(3000)" FOR READ_WRITE
WA(20)
PRINT #21, "LDA20,300"; CHR($0D);
WA(20)
TICKS = 500
WHILE TICKS > 0 AND KEY #21
GET #21, k
PRINT #5, CHR(k); 'Print answer to MPv3 terminal #5
WEND
PRINT #5, ""
WA(50)
CLOSE #21
DIM time_to_leave AS INTEGER
DIM server_message AS STRING(512)
DIM tcpserver_channel AS INTEGER
DIM tcp_port AS INTEGER
DIM tcpserver_id AS STRING(50)
DIM local AS STRING(20)
tcpserver_channel = 25
tcp_port = 790
time_to_leave = 0
tcpserver_id = "TCPSERVER:(" + STR(tcp_port, 0)
+ ")"
OPEN #tcpserver_channel AS tcpserver_id FOR READ_WRITE
PRINT #5, "Awaiting connection for " +
tcpserver_id
REPEAT
IF KEY #tcpserver_channel THEN
CHANNEL_READ(tcpserver_channel , server_message,
CHR(10))
'We can also write back to the client. Here we send
back what we received
local = STR(VR(0), 0)
CHANNEL_WRITE(tcpserver_channel, " " + TIME$
+ " " + STR(VR(0), 0) + " " + STR(ENCODER AXIS(0)))
PRINT #5, "Received: " + server_message
IF LEFT(server_message, 4) = "exit" THEN
PRINT #0, "EXITING " + tcpserver_id
CLOSE #tcpserver_channel
time_to_leave = 1
ENDIF
ENDIF
UNTIL time_to_leave > 0
'CHANNEL_WRITE(tcpserver_channel, " " + TIME$ +
" " + CHR(VR(0)) + " " + CHR(VR(1)) )'CHR(DRIVE_CURRENT
AXIS(2)
DIM time_to_leave AS INTEGER
DIM server_message AS STRING(512)
DIM tcpserver_channel AS INTEGER
DIM tcp_port AS INTEGER
DIM tcpserver_id AS STRING(50)
DIM tcpserver_ip_address AS STRING(15)
tcpserver_channel = 25
tcp_port = 790
tcpserver_ip_address = "192.168.0.250"
tcpserver_id = "TCP:" + tcpserver_ip_address +
"(" + STR(tcp_port, 0) + ")"
OPEN #tcpserver_channel AS tcpserver_id FOR READ_WRITE
PRINT #5, "Connecting to server:  " +
tcpserver_id
WA(20)
PRINT #tcpserver_channel, "Hello, server!"; CHR($0D);
'$0d = Carriage return
REPEAT
time_to_leave = VR(0) 'Evaluate if it's time to leave
WHILE KEY #tcpserver_channel
GET #tcpserver_channel, k
PRINT #5, CHR(k); 'Print answer from serve to
terminal #5
WEND
IF VR(0) = 1 THEN
PRINT #tcpserver_channel, "exit"; CHR($0D);
'$0d = Carriage return
ENDIF
UNTIL time_to_leave > 0
CLOSE #tcpserver_channel
PRINT #5, "Communication is closed."

name: OPEN_WIN
description: This parameter defines the start position of the first registration window which will be used for registration marks if windowing is specified by the REGIST command.
examples:
OPEN_WIN = 170.00
CLOSE_WIN = 230.0
REGIST(256 + 3)
WAIT UNTIL MARK

name: OPEN_WINB
description: This parameter defines the start position of the second registration window which will be used for registration marks if windowing is specified by the REGIST command (mode 20 only) .
examples:
OPEN_WINB = -40
CLOSE_WINB = 40

name: OR
description: This performs an OR function between corresponding bits of the integer part of two valid TrioBASIC expressions. The OR function between two bits is defined as follows: OR 0 1 0 0 1 1 1 1
examples:
WAIT UNTIL IN(2)=ON OR MOTION_ERROR
result=10 OR (2.1*9)
result=10 OR 18
01010
OR   
10010
11010
WAIT UNTIL IN(2)=ON OR MOTION_ERROR

name: OUTDEVICE
description: The value of this parameter determines the default output device for the process it is issued within, allowing the channel number to be set for all statements that generate output, like PRINT , AXISLIST , DIR , etc. This command is process specific so other processes will use the default channel. This command is available for backward compatibility, it is currently recommended to use #channel, instead. However, it remains useful for commands that do not accept a channel number, like DIR.

name: OUTLIMIT
description: The output limit restricts the DAC output to a lower value than the maximum. This can be used to limit the analogue outputs or demand value to a digital drive. OUTLIMIT will always limit the DAC output if you are using a servo control or just manually setting DAC. As it is applied to the output of the closed loop algorithm it is not applied to position-based axis.
examples:
OUTLIMIT AXIS(0) = 1023
OUTLIMIT AXIS(3) = 300000
OUTLIMIT AXIS(4) = 300000
OUTLIMIT AXIS(5) = 300000
OUTLIMIT AXIS(0) = 1023

OUTLIMIT AXIS(3) = 300000
OUTLIMIT AXIS(4) = 300000
OUTLIMIT AXIS(5) = 300000

name: OUTPUT
description: See OPEN for details.

name: OV_GAIN
description: The Output Velocity (OV) gain is a gain constant which is multiplied by the change in measured position. The result is summed with all the other gain terms and applied to the servo DAC. Adding NEGATIVE output velocity gain to a system is mechanically equivalent to adding damping. It is likely to produce a smoother response and allow the use of a higher proportional gain than could otherwise be used, but at the expense of higher following errors. High values may lead to oscillation and produce high following errors. For an output velocity term Kov and change in position DPm, the contribution to the output signal is: O ov = K Ov × δP m

name: PCMCAT_PROC
description: When the PC-MCAT API opens a connection to the Motion Coordinator, the PCMCAT_PROGRAM is started on the highest available process. PCMCAT_PROC can be set to specify a different process for the PCMCAT_PROGRAM. If the defined process is in use then the next lower available process will be used. This programs handles operations that cannot be handled through direct access to shared memory. A FIFO is initialised in shared memory when the API opens a connection to handle these operations. The PCMCAT_PROGRAM would manage this FIFO acting as a bridge between the shared memory and the Motion Coordinator. PCMCAT_PROC is stored in Flash EPROM and can also be set in the MC_CONFIG script file. This command only works on the PC-MCAT and PC-MCAT S products.
examples:
>>PCMCAT_PROC = 19
>>
'MC_CONFIG script file
PCMCAT_PROC = -1  'Start on default process on connection

>>PCMCAT_PROC = 19
>>

'MC_CONFIG script file
PCMCAT_PROC = -1  'Start on default process on connection

name: PEEK
description: The PEEK command returns value of a memory location of the controller AND ed with an optional mask value. PEEK is only normally used for de-bugging purposes and should only be used under the instruction of Trio Motion Technology
params: The value returned from the memory location

A value so you can filter particular bits of the
  address

name: PI
description: The number π is the constant equal to the ratio of a circle’s circumference to its diameter, which is approximately 3.14159.
examples:
circum = 100
PRINT "Radius = "; circum / (2 * PI)
'Motor has 8192 counts per turn.
UNITS = 8192 / (2 * PI)

circum = 100
PRINT "Radius = "; circum / (2 * PI)

'Motor has 8192 counts per turn.
UNITS = 8192 / (2 * PI)

name: PICKLINK
syntax:
PICKLINK(
mode, x, y, z, withdraw, link
axis, link distance, overlap control A, overlap control B , u, v, w ,withdraw
control, xy control, approach control
)
description: PICKLINK is a move type aimed at making it much simpler to program fast pick and place operations. Associated move type MOVEPICK is a profiled move equivalent to PICKLINK. PICKLINK is an absolute move to a position in 3..6 axes, linked to a distance on the controlling link axis. The motion includes an incremental “withdraw” amount and special controls for the move speed profile to allow fast pick and place moves. Multi-axis interpolation uses the UNITS, SPEED, ACCEL and DECEL of the BASE axis.
params:
Specify the number of axes 0 = xyz, 1 = xyzu, 2 = xyzuv,
  3 = xyzuvw

Incremental withdraw distance from the start position
  on z axis
Axis to link motion profile to
0..1 Adjust the speed for withdraw section (optional)
0..1 Adjust the speed for xy section (optional)
0..1 Adjust the speed for approach section (optional)

name: PLC_AR
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_CONFIG
description: The PLC_CONFIG parameter controls optional features and modes in the IEC61131-3 runtime environment. When a bit is set in the PLC_CONFIG, the corresponding mode of operation will be applied to all PLC tasks running in the Motion Coordinator.
examples:
PLC_CONFIG = 1
PLC_CONFIG.1 = 1
PLC_CONFIG = 1
PLC_CONFIG.1 = 1

name: PLC_DM
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_EM
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_ERROR
description: PLC_ERROR shows a bit pattern to indicate which processes in the multitasking system, which are running IEC61131-3 PLC tasks, have raised a run-time error flag. There is one bit per PLC task running in the Motion Coordinator.
examples:
>>?HEX(PLC_ERROR)
100000
>>

name: PLC_HR
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_IR
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_LR
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_OVERFLOW
description: PLC_OVERLOW can be used to check that PLC tasks are not exceeding the PLC scan time that has been set for the task. There is one bit per PLC task running in the Motion Coordinator.
examples:
>>?HEX(PLC_OVERFLOW)
20
>>

name: PLC_REFRESH
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLC_RUN
description: PLC_RUN shows a bit pattern to indicate which processes in the multitasking system are running IEC61131-3 PLC tasks. There is one bit per PLC task running in the Motion Coordinator.
examples:
>>?HEX(PLC_RUN)
4c
>>

name: PLC_TC
description: Used in the Hostlink command structure to set up and control the Hostlink serial communication link.

name: PLM_OFFSET
description: This axis parameter is used exclusively for the SLM interface module and only in PLM (position mode). The parameter allows for an offset between the absolute position within one turn held by the SLM/PLM motor encoder and the zero position in the controller. It is not normally required to set this parameter as it is configured during the initialisation of the PLM.

name: PLOT_CALC
description: Reserved keyword

name: PMOVE
description: Returns the state of the process move buffer. When a process encounters a motion command, it loads the motion’s requirements into its “process move buffer”. This can hold one motion command for any group of axes, and when the load into the process move buffer is complete the PMOVE parameter becomes TRUE. When the next servo period occurs, if the axis move buffer for all the axes involved in the move has room, this move will be loaded into it. When this second transfer is complete the PMOVE parameter becomes FALSE. Motion can only be loaded into the process move buffer when PMOVE is FALSE, so execution of a motion command when PMOVE is TRUE will cause this command to stall until the process move buffer becomes free. Each process has its own PMOVE parameter.

name: POKE
description: The POKE command allows a value to be entered into a memory location of the controller. The POKE command can prevent normal operation of the controller and should only be used if instructed by Trio Motion Technology.
params:
A value so you can filter particular bits of the
  address

name: PORT
description: Assigns ONE command, function or port parameter operation to a particular communication PORT.

name: POSI_SEQ_DELAY
description: This command controls the number of servo cycles delay applied to the demand position (DPOS). This feature is enabled through POSI_SEQ_MODE.

name: POSI_SEQ_MODE
description: The command POSI_SEQ_MODE controls the delay filter to apply the demand position (DPOS). The delay filter allows the final axis output position (DPOS) to be fed through a delay buffer. The delay is defined as a number of servo cycles, specified using the command POSI_SEQ_DELAY. The filter is controlled using POSI_SEQ_MODE. There are 3 modes of operation: 0: No delay (filter disabled) 1: Delay only. The latest value is pushed to delay buffer and popped later when the number of specified delay cycles has elapsed. 2: 2-point filter delay. The average of latest value pushed to delay buffer and current value popped from delay buffer. Internally, the delay buffer must be fully populated before delayed values become active and overwrite demand position, until this happens the demand position will not be delayed. Value: Default value is 0.

name: POS_DELAY
description: To compensate for the delay in sending data across the digital drive network a delay factor can be specified that adds a position correction to the HW_PSWITCH trigger position, proportional to the current measured speed. As an Axis Parameter, the syntax can take either BASE or the AXIS(n) modifier. Requires special hardware functions in the Motion Coordinator FPGA.
params:
Delay time to be applied to the HW_PSWITCH position
  value
examples:
POS_DELAY AXIS(2)=2.5
POS_DELAY AXIS(3)=2.5

POS_DELAY AXIS(2)=2.5
POS_DELAY AXIS(3)=2.5

name: POS_OFFSET
description: For Piezo Motor Control. This sets an offset to the DAC output when the position loop is demanding a positive voltage output. POS_OFFSET is applied after DAC_SCALE so is always a value appropriate to the D to A converter resolution.
examples:
POS_OFFSET = 328
POS_OFFSET = 300
NEG_OFFSET = -270
POS_OFFSET = 328

POS_OFFSET = 300
NEG_OFFSET = -270

name: POWER_UP
description: This parameter is included in the token list to allow older programs to compile in the MC4xx/MC5xx/MC6xx Motion Coordinators. It does not have a function because all programs are automatically stored in the Flash EPROM and the Motion Coordinator will always power up from Flash.
examples:
>>?power_up
0

name: PP_STEP
description: PP_STEP is an integer multiplier on the encoder value UNITS and ENCODER_RATIO should be used in preference to PP_STEP

name: PRINT
description: The PRINT command allows the TrioBASIC program to output a series of characters to a channel. A channel may be a serial port or some other type of connection to the Motion Coordinator. Multiple expressions may be printed in a single PRINT statement by separating each expression by a comma or semi-colon. Each expression may be modified using functions like HEX and CHR , and the output format may be specified using a format specifier enclosed within square brackets (see below). In the absence of a format specifier, a value is printed to 4 decimal places. Any value larger than 1e19 and smaller than 1e-18 will be printed using scientific notation.
params:
See 
#
 for the full channel list
  (default 0 or by 
OUTDEVICE
 if omitted)

Any valid TrioBASIC expression, either numeric or
  string, scalar or array, or any of the TARGET types

Punctuates expressions without any separation at all, and
  if used after the final expression it suppresses the output of the carriage
  return and line feed

Punctuates expressions, separating each expression with a tab
  character

Specifies the format that the expression should be output
  with, which may be either one or two numeric values, or a C format string.
  Format specifiers are not allowed with IP, TARGET, TARGETJ or TARGETE expressions.
Number of decimal places to display

The total width to output (negative for left
  justification) and the number of decimal places

Floating-point output in scientific notation in upper case
  (64-bit)
String output (size + character array) – Raw byte output
String output (size + character array) – Unicode output
examples:
DIM string1, string2 AS STRING(20)
string1 = "OPEN"
string2 = "CLOSED"
IF IN(4) = ON THEN
PRINT "Guard status "; string2
ELSE
PRINT "Guard status "; string1
ENDIF

VR(1) = 6
variable = 410.5
PRINT VR(1)[4, 1], variable[2]
6.0     410.50

>>PRINT "POSITION = "; MPOS
POSITION = 123.0000
>>

name: PRMBLK
description: Used by Motion Perfect to access multiple axis parameters in one Ethernet TCP/IP request. The format of PRMBLK is to set up the block description and then to download the values based on a simple numerical block index value. PRMBLK returns TRUE (-1) if successful or FALSE (0) if it fails. This command must not be used while Motion Perfect is connected in Tool Mode or in Synchronised mode. The command also exists in the TrioPC Motion ActiveX and DLL. This reference may be used when developing a PC application that needs more efficient data transfers.
params:
List of parameter names separated by commas.  Maximum
  number of parameters per block is 32.

List of parameter names separated by commas.  Maximum
  number of parameters per block is 32.
examples:
' Set up the axis parameters to be read in one block
PRMBLK(0,3,0,ATYPE,UNITS,P_GAIN,I_GAIN,D_GAIN,OV_GAIN,VFF_GAIN,SPEED,ACCEL)


PRMBLK(1,3,0,DECEL,SRAMP,MERGE,CREEP,VP_SPEED,MSPEED,FWD_IN,REV_IN,FHOLD_IN)


PRMBLK(1,3,0,FS_LIMIT,RS_LIMIT,FE_LIMIT,FE_RANGE,DRIVE_FE_LIMIT,REP_DIST)


PRMBLK(1,3,0,REP_OPTION,DATUM_IN,MPOS,DPOS,ENDMOVE,REMAIN,ENCODER,DAC_OUT)
' Read the parameter block from axes 0, 1, 2, 15, 21, 22
and 23.


PRMBLK(3,3,0..2,15,21..23)
0.00000,1.00000,0.70000,0.00000,0.00000,0.00000,0.00000,13000.00000,999999.00000,9999999.00000,0.00000,0.00000,10000.00000,0.00000,0.00000,-1.00000,-1.00000,-1.00000,400000000000.00000,-400000000000.00000,2000000.00000,1000000.00000,20000.00000,100000.00000,1.00000,-1.00000,0.00000,0.00000,0.00000,0.00000,0.000
00,0.00000<CR>

name: PROC
description: Allows a particular process to be specified when using a Process Parameter, Function or Command.
examples:
RUN "MOTION", 2
'Wait for the program to start running
WAIT UNTIL PROC_STATUS PROC(2) <> 0
'Wait for the program to complete and flash an output
while waiting
REPEAT
OP(10, ON)
WA(100)
OP(10, OFF)
WA(50)
UNTIL PROC_STATUS PROC(2) = 0

name: PROCESS
description: Displays information about the running processes. There are some system processes that you cannot stop.

name: PROCNUMBER
description: Returns the process on which a TrioBASIC program is running. This is normally required when multiple copies of a program are running on different processes.
examples: MOVE(length) AXIS(PROCNUMBER)

name: PROC_LINE
description: Allows the currently executing line number of a process to be obtained.
examples:
>>PRINT PROC_LINE PROC(2)
12
>>

name: PROC_STATUS
description: Returns the status of another process, referenced with the PROC modifier.
examples:
RUN "progname", 12
WAIT UNTIL PROC_STATUS PROC(12) <> 0 'Wait for
program to start
WAIT UNTIL PROC_STATUS PROC(12) = 0 'Wait for program to finish
'Program "progname" has now finished

name: PROFINET
description: The command PROFINET is used to configure the PROFINET communication with Trio controller as the slave. PROFINET stack in TRIO controller’s firmware is not started automatically on power up. To open the communication channel, use function 1 in STARTUP program or any program that is configured to run after power up.
params:
Application Process Identifier, currently only 0
  supported.
Start position (i.e., the base address) in data source
the size and type of data that is sent across the bus
examples:
>>profinet(0, -1, 0, 1, 1, 0, 10, 2, 1, 0, 1) 'Input
module slot 1


>>profinet(0, -1, 0, 2, 1, 0, 11, 2, 1, 0, 1) 'Input module slot 2


>>profinet(0, -1, 0, 3, 1, 0, 100, 2, 1, 1, 1) 'Output module slot 3


>>profinet(0, -1, 0, 4, 1, 0, 101, 2, 1, 1, 1) 'Output module slot 4


>>profinet(0, -1) 'Show module mapping


0 1 (1) : 0 0 10 2 1 0 1


0 2 (1) : 0 0 11 2 1 0 1


0 3 (1) : 0 0 100 2 1 1 1


0 4 (1) : 0 0 101 2 1 1 1
>>profinet(2, -1) 'Stop profinet


>>


>>
>>profinet(1, -1) 'Start profinet


GOAL Start





ProfiNET Stack profiling is ON (this uses the vr's).
PROFINET
(
8
,"trio-192-168-0-240")

>>profinet(2, -1) 'Stop profinet


>>


>>

>>profinet(1, -1) 'Start profinet


GOAL Start





ProfiNET Stack profiling is ON (this uses the vr's).

name: PROGRAM_NAME
description: Returns the name of the process being executed as a string value. This command can be used in conjunction with PROC and PROCNUMBER . See examples below.

FUNCTION printprogname(process_number AS INTEGER) AS
STRING


  DIM progname AS STRING(20)


    


  progname = PROGRAM_NAME PROC(process_number)


    


  RETURN progname


ENDFUNC
PRINT "Running: " + printprogname(PROCNUMBER)
>>?PROGRAM_NAME PROC(18)
DEMO_PROG

FUNCTION printprogname(process_number AS INTEGER) AS
STRING


  DIM progname AS STRING(20)


    


  progname = PROGRAM_NAME PROC(process_number)


    


  RETURN progname


ENDFUNC
PRINT "Running: " + printprogname(PROCNUMBER)

>>?PROGRAM_NAME PROC(18)
DEMO_PROG

name: PROJECT_NAME
description: Returns the name of the current project in controller memory as a string value.

name: PROTOCOL
description: This parameter allows the user to check which protocol is running on the specified PORT . You can write to this parameter however it is advisable to initialise the communication protocol through SETCOM , ANYBUS etc. Do not write a value to PORT (0) as you will disable communications with Motion Perfect.
examples:
IF PROTOCOL PORT(2) <>3 THEN
PRINT#user, “MODBUS has stopped”
ENDIF

name: PSWITCH
syntax:
Cyclic mode


PSWITCH(switch, enable[, axis, output, state, setpos, resetpos])


 


Turn OFF


PSWITCH(switch, OFF[, hold])


 


Sequence mode


PSWITCH(switch, mode, axis, output, state, table_start,
table_end[, pulse_time])
description: The PSWITCH command allows an output to be set when a predefined position is reached, and to be reset when a second position is reached. Normally the setpos must be a lower value than resetpos, however from V2.0313 the setpos can be higher if bit 1 of the enable value is set. There are 64 position switches each of which can be assigned to any axis and to any output, virtual or real. These two positions define a window where a specific output will behave as the state parameter indicates, hence regardless of the direction of the motion, it will always work. From firmware 2.0309 and later, an alternative operation is available that turns the output on and off at selected positions loaded from the TABLE memory. Multiple PSWITCH’s can be assigned to a single output. The actual output is the OR of all position switches on the output OR the OP setting. This means that OP (output, ON ) can override a PSWITCH. When switching the PSWITCH OFF , the output will remain at the current state unless the hold parameter is set to 1. (Hold requires firmware 2.0226 or later)
params: The switch number in the range 0..63
Enable software PSWITCH with Setpos < Resetpos

Axis to link the PSWITCH to, may be any real or virtual
  axis.

Selects the output to set, can be any real or virtual
  output.

turn the output 
ON
 at setpos

turn the output 
OFF
 at setpos

The position at which output is set or reset, in user
  units

The position at which output is set or reset, in user
  units

The PSWITCH output will hold in the same state


  it was when the PSWITCH is set to 
OFF
. (Default)

The PSWITCH output is forced 
OFF

  even if it was 
ON


  when the PSWTICH is set to 
OFF
.
The switch number in the range 0..63

0: Limit setpos to resetpos, 1: Allow setpos to exceed
  resetpos

0: Single Pswitch, 1: Multi Pswitch (Bits 4..6 determine
  multi mode behaviour)

Axis to link the PSWITCH to, may be any real or virtual
  axis.

Selects the output to set, can be any real or virtual
  output.

turn the output 
ON
 at first position

turn the output 
OFF
 at first
  position
TABLE index for start of PSWITCH data
TABLE index for end of PSWITCH data

Time in servo-cycles when pulse output mode bit 4 is set


  

e.g., SERVO_PERIOD is 500 and pulse_time is 10, pulse
  output is 5 ms

name: PWM_CONTROL
description: The PWM control requires special hardware in the FPGA of the Motion Coordinator. Check that this function is supported before using it. The PWM output of any axis can be linked to the speed of moves running on other axes. For example: Axis 4’s PWM output can be linked to the velocity profile of interpolated moves running on axes 0, 1 and 2. - The PWM output mark/space can be written to via application software program in a simple way. - The PWM mark size can be automatically set via an interpolated path speed. - The PWM mark size can be automatically set via an interpolated path speed AND a defined profile along an XY path. - Some implementations also feature a hardware gating function. A 20 MHz clock is used as the base clock for the PWM generation.
params: The PWM output is switched OFF

PWM output is switched on.  The PWM_MARK value comes
  from the PWM_MARK axis parameter.

PWM_CONTROL(2, multiplier, offset, limit value, link
  axis)


  

PWM output is calculated via VP_SPEED (path velocity)
  of an axis and multiplier. It is adjusted on each servo cycle.  


  The value loaded into the PWM_MARK is given by VP_SPEED * multiplier +
  offset.  


  If the calculated value exceeds the “limit value” the limit value is applied.
  The VP_SPEED value is the velocity profile speed for the link axis in user
  units.

PWM_CONTROL(3, GT/LT, ON/OFF, table start, table end)


  

This axis function loads a sequence of positions into
  the gating FIFO. The GT/LT value should be set to 1 for a GT comparison, 0
  for a LT comparison.  


  The ON/OFF bit is toggled as the positions are loaded automatically by the
  function. The FIFO position buffer can be loaded with one or more
  PWM_CONTROL(3,…  functions.  Longer sequences of positions can be loaded as
  the position buffer becomes available.
Turn on PWM gating function if the hardware allows it.
Clear the PWM_ENCODER register to zero.

PWM_CONTROL(8, multiplier, offset, limit_value,
  velocity_link_axis, amplitude_link_axis)     


  

PWM output is calculated via VP_SPEED (path velocity)
  of an axis and multiplier and will be adjusted on each servo cycle. The value
  loaded into the PWM_MARK is given by:


  

VP_SPEED velocity_link_axis * DPOS amplitude_link_axis
  * multiplier + offset.  


  

If this calculated value exceeds the limit_value, the
  limit_value is applied. The VP_SPEED value is the velocity profile speed for
  the velocity link axis.


  

The DPOS of the amplitude_link_axis can be set using a
  CAMBOX profile so that the amplitude varies.


  

This is achieved using 3 stages:


  

1. The CONNPATH(base XY) move type can be used to
  “connect” a virtual axis to an XY path.


  

2. A CAMBOX profile is then linked to the virtual
  axis.    


  

3. The PWM_CONTROL(8, …) function then links to the
  CAMBOX profile

PWM_CONTROL(9, multiplier, duty_cycle, link_axis)   


  

The frequency of the PWM output is calculated via
  VP_SPEED (path velocity of an axis) of the link axis and multiplier and will
  be adjusted on each servo cycle.  


  

The value loaded into the PWM_CYCLE is given by
  20000000 / (VP_SPEED * multiplier). The VP_SPEED value is the velocity
  profile speed for the link axis in user units.  


  

The value loaded into the PWM_MARK is the percentage of
  the PWM_CYCLE specified in duty_cycle.
examples:
BASE(0, 1)
SPEED = 5000
ACC(50000)
MERGE = ON
MOVE(10000, 0)
MOVE(0, -10000)
MOVE(-10000, 0)
MOVE(0, 10000)
'This program sets the PWM output to a minimum of 50 and
maximum of 500. At a speed of 2000 the PWM output will be 250:
BASE(4)
WDOG = OFF
WA(10)
ATYPE = 35
WDOG = ON
PWM_CYCLE = 1000 'Sets 20khz PWM
Link = 0         'Link to axes 0 and 1
Mult = 0.1       'If speed = 2000: pwm = 2000 * 0.1 = 200
PWM_CONTROL(2, mult, 50, 500, link)
start = 1000
TABLE(start, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1000, 1000, 1000, 0, 0)
'Stage 1: Link a virtual axis to the XY path:
CONNPATH(0) AXIS(8)
BASE(4)
WDOG = OFF
WA(10)
ATYPE = 35
WDOG = ON
PWM_CYCLE = 1000 'Sets 20khz PWM
Link = 0         'Link to axes 0 and 1
Mult = 1 / 10000 'If speed = 5000: pwm = 5000 * (1 / 10000)
* DPOS = 500
PWM_CONTROL(8, mult, 50, 500, link, 9)
'Note how the CAMBOX links to a length of the box outline
path of 40000:
TABLE(1000, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1000, 1000, 1000, 0, 0)
BASE(0, 1)
SPEED = 10000
ACC(50000)
MERGE = ON
WHILE TRUE
CAMBOX(1000, 1008, 1, 40000, 8) AXIS(9)
MOVE(10000, 0)
MOVE(0, -10000)
MOVE(-10000, 0)
MOVE(0, 10000)
WAIT IDLE
WA(10)
DEFPOS(0) AXIS(8)
WEND

BASE(0, 1)
SPEED = 5000
ACC(50000)
MERGE = ON
MOVE(10000, 0)
MOVE(0, -10000)
MOVE(-10000, 0)
MOVE(0, 10000)
'This program sets the PWM output to a minimum of 50 and
maximum of 500. At a speed of 2000 the PWM output will be 250:
BASE(4)
WDOG = OFF
WA(10)
ATYPE = 35
WDOG = ON
PWM_CYCLE = 1000 'Sets 20khz PWM
Link = 0         'Link to axes 0 and 1
Mult = 0.1       'If speed = 2000: pwm = 2000 * 0.1 = 200
PWM_CONTROL(2, mult, 50, 500, link)

start = 1000
TABLE(start, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1000, 1000, 1000, 0, 0)
'Stage 1: Link a virtual axis to the XY path:
CONNPATH(0) AXIS(8)
BASE(4)
WDOG = OFF
WA(10)
ATYPE = 35
WDOG = ON
PWM_CYCLE = 1000 'Sets 20khz PWM
Link = 0         'Link to axes 0 and 1
Mult = 1 / 10000 'If speed = 5000: pwm = 5000 * (1 / 10000)
* DPOS = 500
PWM_CONTROL(8, mult, 50, 500, link, 9)
'Note how the CAMBOX links to a length of the box outline
path of 40000:
TABLE(1000, 0, 0, 0, 0, 1000, 1000, 1000, 0, 0)
BASE(0, 1)
SPEED = 10000
ACC(50000)
MERGE = ON
WHILE TRUE
CAMBOX(1000, 1008, 1, 40000, 8) AXIS(9)
MOVE(10000, 0)
MOVE(0, -10000)
MOVE(-10000, 0)
MOVE(0, 10000)
WAIT IDLE
WA(10)
DEFPOS(0) AXIS(8)
WEND

name: PWM_CYCLE
description: Defines the number of internal clocks in the combined mark + space period (Maximum value 65535). A 20MHz clock is used as the base clock for the PWM generation.
params: Number of 20MHz clock pulses for one PWM pulse

name: PWM_MARK
description: Defines the number of ON clocks in the mark, using the internal 20MHz clock (Maximum value 65535). See PWM_CONTROL for mode setting information. A 20MHz clock is used as the base clock for the PWM generation.
params:
Number of internal counts defining the ON part of the
  PWM
examples:
PWM_CYCLE = 1000
PWM_MARK = 250
PWM_CYCLE = 2000
PWM_MARK = 1200

PWM_CYCLE = 1000
PWM_MARK = 250

PWM_CYCLE = 2000
PWM_MARK = 1200

name: P_GAIN
description: The Proportional gain sets the 'stiffness' of the servo response. Values that are too high will produce oscillation. Values that are too low will produce large following errors. For a proportional gain K p and position error E, its contribution to the output signal is: O p = K p × E
examples: P_GAIN AXIS(11)=0.25

name: ^  Power
description: Raises expression1 to the power of expression2.
examples:
x = 2 ^ 6
PRINT x
x = 2 ^ (1 / 3)
PRINT x

x = 2 ^ 6
PRINT x

x = 2 ^ (1 / 3)
PRINT x

name: ' Quote
description: A single quote ' is used to mark the rest of a line as being a comment only with no execution significance. Comments use memory space and so should be concise in very long programs. Comments have no effect on execution speed since they are not present in the compiled code.
examples:
'PROGRAM TO ROTATE WHEEL
turns=10
'turns contains the number of turns required
MOVE(turns)' the movement occurs here

name: RAISE_ANGLE
description: This parameter is used with CORNER_MODE , it defines the maximum change in direction of a 2-axis interpolated move before CORNER_STATE is triggered. When the change in direction is greater than the RAINSE_ANGLE, CORNER_STATE will change so the system can interact with a program. This can be used to change the angle of a cutting knife RAISE_ANGLE does not control the speed so it should be set equal or greater than STOP_ANGLE .

name: RANDOMIZE
description: This command initialises the pseudo-random number generator for the process it is issued within (so issuing this command in one process will not influence the pseudo-random sequence generated in another process). Without parameters, the pseudo-random number generator is primed with a seed that is generated from the constantly varying internal system state. With a parameter, the pseudo-random number generator is primed with the seed specified. Note that use of this command is not normally required, as each time a program is executed, it implicitly calls RANDOMIZE with no parameters to ensure that the sequence is generated from an unknown starting point. However, if you require a known series of pseudo-random values, for example when debugging or testing, then you can use this command with a parameter, usually zero, before you call RND or RNDF.
params:
The integer value that the pseudo-random number generator
  will use to as the base from which to generate values from

name: RAPIDSTOP
description: The RAPIDSTOP command operates like the CANCEL command but does so for all axes using a subset of cancel modes. All axes will decelerate at FASTDEC if specified, otherwise at DECEL .
params:
Cancels axis commands from the 
MTYPE

  buffers (default)

Cancels all active and buffered moves including the 
PMOVE
examples:
CONNECT(1, 0) AXIS(1) 'Axis 1 follows axis 0
BASE(0)
REPAEAT
MOVE(1000) AXIS (0)
MOVE(-100000) AXIS (0)
MOVE(100000) AXIS (0)
UNTIL IN(2) = OFF 'Stop button pressed?
RAPIDSTOP(2)
BASE(0)
REGIST(3)
FORWARD AXIS(1)
MOVE(100000) 'Apply a long move
WAIT UNTIL MARK
RAPIDSTOP
WAIT IDLE 'For MOVEABS to be accurate, the axis must stop
MOVEABS(3000)
BASE(0)
CONNECT(1, 1)
FORWARD AXIS(1)
WAIT UNTIL VPSPEED = SPEED 'Let the axis get to full speed
WA(1000)
RAPIDSTOP
WAIT IDLE AXIS(1) 'Wait for axis 1 to stop
CONNECT(1, 1) 'Re-connect axis 0
REVERSE AXIS(1)
WAIT UNTIL VP_SPEED = SPEED
WA(1000)
RAPIDSTOP
WAIT IDLE AXIS(1)

CONNECT(1, 0) AXIS(1) 'Axis 1 follows axis 0
BASE(0)
REPAEAT
MOVE(1000) AXIS (0)
MOVE(-100000) AXIS (0)
MOVE(100000) AXIS (0)
UNTIL IN(2) = OFF 'Stop button pressed?
RAPIDSTOP(2)

BASE(0)
REGIST(3)
FORWARD AXIS(1)
MOVE(100000) 'Apply a long move
WAIT UNTIL MARK
RAPIDSTOP
WAIT IDLE 'For MOVEABS to be accurate, the axis must stop
MOVEABS(3000)

BASE(0)
CONNECT(1, 1)
FORWARD AXIS(1)
WAIT UNTIL VPSPEED = SPEED 'Let the axis get to full speed
WA(1000)
RAPIDSTOP
WAIT IDLE AXIS(1) 'Wait for axis 1 to stop
CONNECT(1, 1) 'Re-connect axis 0
REVERSE AXIS(1)
WAIT UNTIL VP_SPEED = SPEED
WA(1000)
RAPIDSTOP
WAIT IDLE AXIS(1)

name: READPACKET
description: READPACKET is used to read in data to the VR variables over a serial communications port. The data is transmitted from the PC in binary format with a CRC 16-bit checksum. There are four different data formats, all use the same packet structure: Data CRC Byte 0 Byte 1 Byte 2 … Byte n Byte 0 Byte 1 The 16bit checksum uses the generator polynomial: x 16+x 15+x 2+x 0 or $8005
params:
This value tells the 
Motion Coordinator where to
  start setting the variables in the VR global memory array.
The number of variables to download, maximum 250
The number format for the numbers being downloaded
examples:
READPACKET(1, 100, 10, 2)
FOR value = 0 to 9
'Offset the bytes
VR(value * 4 + 103) = VR(value * 4 + 103) * (2 ^ 32)
VR(value * 4 + 102) = VR(value * 4 + 103) * (2 ^ 16)
VR(value * 4 + 101) = VR(value * 4 + 103) * (2 ^ 8)
VR(value) = (value * 4 + 103) + VR(value * 4 + 102)) + VR(value
* 4 + 101))_
+ VR(value * 4 + 100)
NEXT value

name: READ_BIT
description: READ_BIT can be used to test the value of a single bit within a VR or TABLE element. By default, VR will be used, but the mode parameter can be changed to operate on TABLE data.
params: The bit number to clear, valid range is 0 to 52

The 
VR
/ 
TABLE

  which to operate on
examples:
result = READ_BIT(4, 13)
result = READ_BIT(4, 13, 1)
result = READ_BIT(4, 13)
result = READ_BIT(4, 13, 1)

name: READ_OP
description: Returns the state of digital output logic. If called with one parameter, it returns the state (1 or 0) of that specific output channel. If called with 2 parameters READ_OP returns the binary sum of the group of outputs. READ_OP checks the state of the output logic. The output may be virtual or not powered and you will still see the logic state.
params: The binary pattern of the selected outputs
Output to return the value of/start of output group
examples:
test:
WAIT UNTIL READ_OP(12) = ON
GOSUB place
op_bits = READ_OP(16, 23)
IF op_bits <> 0 THEN
GOSUB check_outputs
ENDIF

test:
WAIT UNTIL READ_OP(12) = ON
GOSUB place

op_bits = READ_OP(16, 23)
IF op_bits <> 0 THEN
GOSUB check_outputs
ENDIF

name: READ_WRITE
description: See OPEN for details.

name: REGIST
description: The REGIST command initiates a capture of an axis position when it sees a registration input or the Z mark on the encoder. Once a registration event is captured MARK is set and the position and speed at the event can be read back. See the Hardware Chapter of the manual to understand which registration mode your hardware supports. Filtering can be applied to the input as well as defining a window of where to capture. Hardware registration captures the encoder count against the registration input in hardware. Time based registration captures the time of the registration event and interpolates the position values being sent back from the drive against it. Although all modes are available for backwards compatibility it is recommended to use modes 20-22 for new applications. Other modes have been provided for compatibility with older products. The REGIST command must be re-issued for each position capture. The captured registration position may be outside REP_DIST . You should always check the captured registration position to ensure it is within your application’s usable range.
params: Cancel all Registration operations on the axis
Single channel hardware registration including Touch Probe
Rising edge on time based registration (use mode 21)
Falling edge on time based registration (use mode 21)

name: REGISTER_KEYWORD
description: This command registers a keyword as a part of the TrioBASIC language. Each keyword must be linked to a BASIC function that holds the implementation of the keyword. It enables users to extend the language using their own keywords. Note that a new keyword cannot be the same as an existing keyword.
params: Minimum number of parameters for BASIC function.
Maximum number of parameters for BASIC function.

name: REGISTER_MOVE
description: This command registers a keyword as a part of the TrioBASIC language. Each move requires two BASIC functions to implement the move, one to load the move and the other to implement motion. It enables users to extend functionality of the controller using their own keywords. Note that a new move name cannot be the same as an existing move name.
params:
Name of your move. This cannot the same as any other
  system keyword
Minimum number of axes for this move
Maximum number of axes for this move
Number of position parameters per axis

name: REGIST_CONTROL
description: Read or set the low-level bit pattern in the registration control register. REGIST_CONTROL is an 8-bit axis parameter that conforms to the standard use of BASE and the AXIS modifier.

name: REGIST_DELAY
description: The value, in milliseconds, of the total system delays between a signal appearing on the registration input and the position being available to the time-based registration algorithm. A digital system will usually transfer the actual position information with a one servo period delay. Therefore, the REGIST_DELAY must be adjusted when the SERVO_PERIOD parameter is not at the default value. In most real-world systems there are delays built into the registration circuit; the external sensor and the input opto-isolator will have some fixed response time. As machine speed increases, the fixed electrical delays will influence the captured registration position. REGIST_DELAY can be adjusted to take account of the total delays due to the servo period and input.
examples:
REGIST_DELAY = -1.01
REGIST_DELAY = -0.515
REGIST_DELAY = -1.135
REGIST_DELAY = -1.01
REGIST_DELAY = -0.515
REGIST_DELAY = -1.135

name: REGIST_SPEED
description: Stores the speed of the axis when a registration mark was seen user units per millisecond. This parameter is used with the first (A) hardware registration channel, or Z mark only. In most real-world systems there are delays built into the registration circuit; the external sensor and the input opto-isolator will have some fixed response time. As machine speed increases, the fixed electrical delays will influence the captured registration position. REGIST_SPEED returns the value of axis speed captured at the same time as REG_POS . The captured speed and position values can be used to calculate a registration position that does not vary with speed because of the fixed delays.
examples:
fixed_delays = 0.020 'Circuit delays in milliseconds
REGIST(20, 0, 0, 0, 0)
WAIT UNTIL MARK
captured_position = REG_POS - (REGIST_SPEED * fixed_delays)

name: REGIST_SPEEDB
description: Stores the speed of the axis when a registration mark was seen user units per millisecond. This parameter is used with the second (B) hardware registration channel, or Z mark only. In most real-world systems there are delays built into the registration circuit; the external sensor and the input opto-isolator will have some fixed response time. As machine speed increases, the fixed electrical delays will influence the captured registration position. REGIST_SPEEDB returns the value of axis speed captured at the same time as REG_POSB . The captured speed and position values can be used to calculate a registration position that does not vary with speed because of the fixed delays.

name: REG_INPUTS
description: Selects which of the hardware registration inputs to use for an axis. When using REGIST modes 3 to 17 the first input is the A channel and the second is the B. It is recommended to use REGIST(20 to 22) for new projects. The following defaults are used: Flexaxis Module / Euro408 MC405 / MC403 / Euro404 Axis 1st input 2nd input Axis 1st input 2nd input 0 0 4 0 0 4 1 1 5 1 1 5 2 2 6 2 2 6 3 3 7 3 * 3 7 4 4 0 4 ** 4 0 5 5 1 * Euro404 and MC405 only 6 6 2 ** MC405 only 7 7 3 MC4N default for built-in encoder: 1 st input, 0. 2 nd input, 4.
examples:
REG_INPUTS AXIS(9) = $72
REG_INPUTS AXIS(0) = $03
REG_INPUTS AXIS(1) = $04
REG_INPUTS AXIS(2) = $05
REG_INPUTS AXIS(3) = $06
REG_INPUTS AXIS(4) = $07
REG_INPUTS AXIS(0) = $40
REG_INPUTS AXIS(1) = $51
REG_INPUTS AXIS(2) = $62
REG_INPUTS AXIS(3) = $73

name: REG_POS
description: Stores the latest position at which a registration mark was seen on each axis in user UNITS . This parameter is used with the first (A) hardware registration channel, or Z mark only.

name: REG_POSB
description: Stores the latest position at which a registration mark was seen on each axis in user units. This parameter is used with the second (B) hardware registration channel, or Z mark only.
examples:
' Registration on rising edge R0 and falling edge R1
REGIST(11)
WAIT UNTIL MARK
position1 = REG_POS
WAIT UNTIL MARKB
position2 = REG_POSB
length = position2 – position1

name: REMAIN_TIME
description: This is the amount of time it will take to finish all the MOVES loaded in the buffer. The value can be used to turn ON/OFF outputs for example used in gluing applications.
examples:
start:


SPEED=
10
ACCEL=
200 
'

second ramp time
