name: STEPINTO
description: Steps one line in a program, but unlike the STEPLINE command, steps into any functions encountered rather than over them. This command is usually issued from the command line, but it can also be used as a statement within a program, in which case it will cause execution to pause when encountered. This command is also used by Motion Perfect to control program stepping. All copies of this named program will step unless the process number is also specified. If the program is not running it will step into either the specified process or the next available process if the process parameter is omitted, pausing execution at the first executable line. If the program name is not supplied, either the SELECT ed program will step (if used from the command line), or the program that paused due to an embedded STEPINTO statement will step.
params: This specifies the program to be stepped.
examples:
>>STEPINTO "conveyor"
OK
%[Process 21:Line 19] – Paused
>>

name: STEPLINE
description: Steps one line in a program, but unlike the STEPINTO command, steps over any functions encountered rather than into them. This command is usually issued from the command line, but it can also be used as a statement within a program, in which case it will cause execution to pause when encountered. This command is also used by Motion Perfect to control program stepping. All copies of the named program will step unless the process number is also specified. If the program is not running it will step into either the specified process or the next available process if the process parameter is omitted, pausing execution at the first executable line. If the program name is not supplied, either the SELECT ed program will step (if used from the command line), or the program that paused due to an embedded STEPLINE statement will step.
params: This specifies the program to be stepped.
examples:
>>STEPLINE "conveyor"
OK
%[Process 21:Line 19] – Paused
>>

name: STEP_RATIO
description: This command sets up an integer ratio for the axis’ demanded positon. Usually this is used on a stepper (pulse+direction) output. It can also be applied to a networked axis in positon mode, such as EtherCAT and RTEX position servo. Every servo-period the change in position is passed through the STEP_RATIO function before it goes to the step pulse or network drive output. The STEP_RATIO function operates before the divide by 16 factor (divide by 256 in Flexslice P375) in the stepper axis. This maintains the good timing resolution of the stepper output circuit. STEP_RATIO does not replace UNITS.
params:
Number of counts to output for the given dpos_count
  value.  Range: 0 to 16777215.

Change in 
DPOS
 value for
  corresponding output count.  Range: 0 to 16777215.
examples:
'Axis 0: 500 counts per mm (31.25 steps per mm)
'Axis 1: 800 counts per mm (50.00 steps per mm)
BASE(0)
STEP_RATIO(500,800)
UNITS = 800
BASE(1)
UNITS = 800
BASE(2)
STEP_RATIO(400, 360)
'Note: this has reduced resolution of the stepper axis
MOVE(360*16) 'move 1 revolution
BASE(0)
STEP_RATIO(1, 1)

'Axis 0: 500 counts per mm (31.25 steps per mm)
'Axis 1: 800 counts per mm (50.00 steps per mm)
BASE(0)
STEP_RATIO(500,800)
UNITS = 800
BASE(1)
UNITS = 800

BASE(2)
STEP_RATIO(400, 360)
'Note: this has reduced resolution of the stepper axis
MOVE(360*16) 'move 1 revolution

BASE(0)
STEP_RATIO(1, 1)

name: STOP
description: Stops one program at its current line. A particular program name may be specified and an optional process number. The process number is required if there is more than one instance of the program running. If no name or process number is specified, then the currently selected program will be stopped.
params: name of program to be stopped.

optional process number to be used when multiple
  instances of the program are running and only one is to be stopped.
examples:
>>STOP axis_init
IF IN(12) = OFF THEN
STOP "hmi_handler"
STOP "motion1"
ENDIF
'Get the process to stop (which is put in VR by an HMI)
proc_a = VR(45)
'Stop the required instance of test_program
STOP "test_program", proc_a
>>STOP axis_init

IF IN(12) = OFF THEN
STOP "hmi_handler"
STOP "motion1"
ENDIF

'Get the process to stop (which is put in VR by an HMI)
proc_a = VR(45)
'Stop the required instance of test_program
STOP "test_program", proc_a

name: STOPPING_DISTANCE
description: This is the distance required for motion to come to rest using the current deceleration parameters.

name: STOP_ANGLE
description: This parameter is used with CORNER_MODE , it defines the maximum change in direction of a 2-axis interpolated move that will be merged at speed. When the change in direction (see CHANGE_DIR_LAST) between two moves is greater than the STOP_ANGLE, the speed is reduced to zero. When a move needs to stop its STARTMOVE_SPEED is set to zero.
examples:
CORNER_MODE.1 = 1
STOP_ANGLE = 25 * PI / 180

name: STORE
description: Used by Motion Perfect to load Firmware to the controller. Removing the controller power during a STORE sequence can lead to the controller having to be returned to Trio for re-initialization.

name: STR
description: Converts a numerical value to a string.
params: Number of decimal places to be used (default=5)
Width of field to be used (default=0, unlimited)
examples:
DIM str1 AS STRING(20)
str1 = STR(VR(100))

DIM str1 AS STRING(20)
str1 = STR(VR(100))

name: STRTOD
description: The STRTOD command reads a sequence of characters and converts them to a numeric value. The conversion stops at the first invalid character found in the input character stream. The characters may be read from the VR array or from a TrioBASIC I/O channel. Values may be integer or floating-point (with and without leading/trailing digits), which may be signed and may include an exponent (with and without sign). The following are examples of valid values: 123 123.456 +12.6 -0.12 .125 12. 12.7E-3 0.1e10 1.6e+3
params:
Integer. If the number is not an integer then 0 is
  returned.

The format is auto-selected to provide the best
  resolution.

name: SYNC
description: The SYNC command is used to synchronise one axis with a moving position on another axis. It does this by linking the DPOS of the slave axis to the MPOS of the master. So both axes must be programed in the same scale (for example mm). This can be used to synchronise a robot to a point on a conveyor. The user can define a time to synchronise and de-synchronise. The synchronising movement on the base axis is the sum of two parts: The conveyor movement from the ‘sync_pos’, this is the movement of the demand point along the conveyor. The movement to ‘pos1’, this is the position in the current USER_FRAME where the sync_pos was captured on the slave axis. When the axis is synchronised it will follow the movements on the ‘sync_axis’. As the SYNC does not fill the MTYPE buffer you can perform movements while synchronised. To synchronise to a new USER_FRAME using SYNC(20) requires the kinematic runtime FEC As SYNC does not get loaded in to the move buffer it is not cancelled by CANCEL or RAPIDSTOP , you have to perform SYNC(4). When a software or hardware limit is reached the SYNC is immediately stopped with no deceleration. Typically you can use the captured position for example REG_POS , or a position from a vision system for the ‘sync_position’. The pos1, pos2 and pos3 are typically the position of the sensor/ vision system in the current USER_FRAME .
params:
1 = Start synchronisation, requires minimum first 5
  parameters

4 = Stop synchronisation, requires minimum first 2
  parameters

10 = Re-synchronise to another axis, requires minimum
  first 5 parameters

20 = Re-synchronise to 
USER_FRAMEB
,
  requires minimum first 5 parameters

Time to complete the synchronisation movement in servo
  period units.
The captured position on the sync_axis.
Absolute position on the first axis on the base array
Absolute position on the second axis on the base array
Absolute position on the third axis on the base array
examples:
'axis(0) - robot axis x
'axis(1) - robot axis y
'axis(2) - robot axis z
'axis(3) - robot wrist rotate
'These are the actual robot axis, FRAME=14 can be applied
to these
'axis(10) - conveyor axis
'axis(11) - conveyor axis
'These are the real conveyors that you wish to link to
'Sensor and conveyor offsets
sen_xpos = 385
conv1_yoff = 200
conv2_yoff = -250
conv2_xoff = 40
place_pos = 0
BASE(0,1)
'Move to home position.
MOVEABS(200,50)
'start conveyors
DEFPOS(0) AXIS(11) ' reset conveyor position for place
FORWARD AXIS(10)
FORWARD AXIS(11)
WAIT IDLE
WHILE(running)
REGIST(20,0,0,0,0) AXIS(10)
WAIT UNTIL MARK AXIS(10)
SYNC(1, 1000, REG_POS, 10, sen_xpos , conv1_yoff)
WAIT UNTIL SYNC_CONTROL AXIS(0)=3
'Now synchronised
GOSUB pick
SYNC(10, 1000, place_pos, 11, conv2_xoff, conv2_yoff)

place_pos = place_pos + 100
WEND

name: SYSTEM_ERROR
description: SYSTEM_ERROR returns an integer value which indicates which active errors have been reported by the firmware. Any bits set in SYSTEM_ERROR will cause the WDOG to be automatically set to OFF, thus halting all motion. If it is necessary to prevent the occurrence of particular errors causing a WDOG OFF condition, then SYSTEM_ERROR_MASK may be set. Masking SYSTEM_ERROR bits must be done with caution as it might create a condition where motors can run out of control.

name: SYSTEM_ERROR_MASK
description: Allows selection of which SYSTEM_ERROR bits will trigger a fault indication and set WDOG to OFF. Masking SYSTEM_ERROR bits must be done with caution as it might create a condition where motors can run out of control.

name: SYSTEM_LOAD
description: SYSTEM_LOAD returns the amount of time that is used by the system and motion software. The value is expressed as a percentage of the current servo period. The remaining time, that is 100 minus SYSTEM_LOAD percent, is therefore available to the application programs. When setting SERVO_PERIOD appropriate to the number of axes running, the value of SYSTEM_LOAD should normally not be more than 55%.
examples:
>>?SYSTEM_LOAD
23.1390
>>

name: SYSTEM_LOAD_AVG
description: SYSTEM_LOAD_AVG returns an average from the previous 256 cycles of the SYSTEM_LOAD value. The value is expressed as a percentage of the current servo period.

name: SYSTEM_LOAD_MAX
description: SYSTEM_LOAD_MAX returns the maximum value of SYSTEM_LOAD since power-up, or since SYSTEM_LOAD_MAX was last set to 0. If SYSTEM_LOAD_MAX is greater than 100 then at some point the firmware system and motion processing has overflowed the servo period. The number of axes should be reduced or the SERVO_PERIOD set to a higher value.

name: SYSTEM_WARNING
description: SYSTEM_WARNING is a bitwise system variable that returns a value which indicates which active warnings have been reported by the firmware.

name: S_REF
description: S_REF is identical to DAC .

name: S_REF_OUT
description: S_REF_OUT is identical to DAC_OUT .

name: -  Subtract
description: Subtracts expression2 from expression1

name: TABLE
syntax:
value = TABLE(address)


array = TABLE(start address, last address)


TABLE(address[, data0..data35])
description: The TABLE command can be used to load and read back the internal TABLE values. As the table can be written to and read from, it may be used to hold information as an alternative to variables. A single global floating-point value can be read from an address or alternatively a range of floating-point values can be read from a range of addresses as an array (1D) of floating-point values that could be copied to another area of VR/TABLE memory or a locally defined array. The table values are floating-point and can therefore be fractional. You can clear the TABLE using NEW “TABLE” Some TABLE elements are automatically backed up depending on the controller type. Use the FLASHTABLE command to force storing TABLE data.
params: returns the value stored at the address

The address of the first point of a write, or the single
  address to read

1D array of values read from a consecutive range of
  TABLE addresses
Range of TABLE addresses to read from as a 1D array
The data written to the address
examples:
TABLE(100, 0, 120, 250, 370, 470, 530)
>>PRINT TABLE(1000)
1234.0000
>>
DIM myints AS INTEGER(5, 20)
myints(2) = TABLE(1000, 1019) 'Row 2 will be populated
PRINT myints
TABLE(1000, TABLE(2000, 2199)) 'Copy points 2000..2199 to
1000
VR(0) = TABLE(100, 199) 'Copy points 100..199 to VR index 0

DIM myints AS INTEGER(5, 20)
myints(2) = TABLE(1000, 1019) 'Row 2 will be populated
PRINT myints

TABLE(1000, TABLE(2000, 2199)) 'Copy points 2000..2199 to
1000
VR(0) = TABLE(100, 199) 'Copy points 100..199 to VR index 0

name: TABLEVALUES
description: Returns a list of table values starting at the table address specified. The output is a comma delimited list of values. TABLEVALUES is provided for Motion Perfect to allow for fast access to banks of TABLE values.
params:
First 
TABLE
 address to be
  returned

Last 
TABLE
 address to be
  returned

0 = Uncompressed comma delimited text (default)


  

1 = Compressed comma delimited text, repeated values
  are compressed using a repeat count before the value (k7,0.0000 representing
  7 successive values of 0.0000). Single values do not have the repeat count;

name: TABLE_POINTER
description: Using the TABLE_POINTER command it is possible to determine which TABLE memory location is currently being used by the CAM or CAMBOX . TABLE_POINTER returns the current table location that the CAM function is using. The returned number contains the table location and divides up the interpolated distance between the current and next TABLE location to indicate exact location. The user can load new CAM data into previously processed TABLE location ready for the next CAM cycle. This is ideal for allowing a technician to finely tune a complex process or changing recipes on the fly whilst running.
examples:
'CAM Pointer demo
'Store the live table points
TABLE(1000, 0, 0.8808, 6.5485, 19.5501, 39.001, 60.999, _
80.4499, 93.4515, 99.1192, 100)
'Store another copy of original points
TABLE(100, 0, 0.8808, 6.5485, 19.5501, 39.001, 60.999, _
80.4499, 93.4515, 99.1192, 100)
'Initialise axes
BASE(0)
WDOG = ON
SERVO = ON
'Set up CAM
CAMBOX(1000, 1009, 10, 100, 1, 4, 0)
'Start Master axis
BASE(1)
SERVO = ON
SPEED = 10
FORWARD
'Read Analogue input and scale CAM based on input
pointer = 0
WHILE TRUE
'Read Analog Input (answer 0-10)
scale = AIN(32) * 0.01
'Detects change in table pointer
IF INT(TABLE_POINTER) <> pointer THEN
pointer = INT(TABLE_POINTER)
IF pointer = 1000 THEN
'First value so update last value
TABLE(1008, TABLE(108) * scale)
ELSEIF pointer = 1001 THEN
'Second value, so must update first and last but 1
value
TABLE(1000, TABLE(100) * scale)
TABLE(1009, TABLE(109) * scale)
ELSE
'Update previous value
TABLE(pointer - 1, TABLE(pointer - 901) * scale)
ENDIF
ENDIF
WEND
STOP

name: TABLE_PROTECT
description: Sets a range of TABLE values to be write protected (i.e. read only). Can be used to define parts of the TABLE where setup data has been stored that must not be changed by any programs, data connections or direct user entry. TABLE_PROTECT is not available for Euro404/408, MC403, MC403-X, MC403-Z, MC404-Z, MC405, MC405-Y and P157.
params:
First 
TABLE
 address to be
  locked

Last 
TABLE
 address to be locked

name: TEXT_FILE_LOADER
description: The TEXT_FILE_LOADER command controls the TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROGRAM on the controller. This function allows the TEXT_FILE_LOADER to be controlled and configured from the BASIC. TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROC can be set to define which process the TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROGRAM runs on. The TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROGRAM is the controller end of the fast file transfer process that communicates with the file loading functionality of PCMotion. If no parameters are used then the function is 0.
examples:
'Wait for a file transfer to start
WAIT UNTIL TEXT_FILE_LOADER(1, 0) = 1
'Process this file
WHILE KEY #fifo_channel
GET #fifo_channel, k
PRINT #echo_channel, CHR(k);
IF k = 13 THEN PRINT #echo_channel, CHR(10);
IF k >= 65 AND k <= 90 THEN 'A to Z
ltflag = 0
spflag = 0
value = 0
GOTO command_pro
ENDIF
WEND
'Set the FIFO as default file location for transparent
protocol
TEXT_FILE_LOADER(2, 1, 1)
'Initialise FIFO
OPEN #fifo_channel AS "TRANSFER_FILE" FOR
FIFO_WRITE(fifo_size)
CLOSE #fifo_channel
'Open FIFO to read
OPEN #fifo_channel AS "TRANSFER_FILE" FOR
FIFO_READ
'Run
WHILE running
'Wait for a file transfer to start
WAIT UNTIL TEXT_FILE_LOADER(1, 0)
WHILE KEY #fifo_channel
GET #fifo_channel, char
PRINT #5, CHR(char)


    WEND
WEND

'Wait for a file transfer to start
WAIT UNTIL TEXT_FILE_LOADER(1, 0) = 1
'Process this file
WHILE KEY #fifo_channel
GET #fifo_channel, k
PRINT #echo_channel, CHR(k);
IF k = 13 THEN PRINT #echo_channel, CHR(10);
IF k >= 65 AND k <= 90 THEN 'A to Z
ltflag = 0
spflag = 0
value = 0
GOTO command_pro
ENDIF
WEND

'Set the FIFO as default file location for transparent
protocol
TEXT_FILE_LOADER(2, 1, 1)
'Initialise FIFO
OPEN #fifo_channel AS "TRANSFER_FILE" FOR
FIFO_WRITE(fifo_size)
CLOSE #fifo_channel
'Open FIFO to read
OPEN #fifo_channel AS "TRANSFER_FILE" FOR
FIFO_READ
'Run
WHILE running
'Wait for a file transfer to start
WAIT UNTIL TEXT_FILE_LOADER(1, 0)
WHILE KEY #fifo_channel
GET #fifo_channel, char
PRINT #5, CHR(char)


    WEND
WEND

name: TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROC
description: When the TrioPC ActiveX starts a text file transfer to the Motion Coordinator, the TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROGRAM is started on the highest available process. TEXT_FILE_LOADER _PROC can be set to specify a different process for the TEXT_FILE_LOADER _PROGRAM. If the defined process is in use, then the next lower available process will be used. TEXT_FILE_LOADER _PROC can be set in the MC_CONFIG script file.
examples:
>> TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROC = 19
>>
'MC_CONFIG script file
TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROC = -1 'Start on default process on connection

>> TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROC = 19
>>

'MC_CONFIG script file
TEXT_FILE_LOADER_PROC = -1 'Start on default process on connection

name: THROW_ERROR
description: This command throws an error with a given error message. It generates a RUN_ERROR number 268. When an error is generated, the given error message is displayed. This command can be used with ‘Open Moves’ to throw an error if a move cannot be loaded. It can also be used in functions or programs to stop a program with a custom error message.
params: Message to display when throwing an error as STRING.

name: TICKS
description: The current count of the process clock ticks is stored in this parameter. The process parameter is a 64-bit counter which is DECREMENTED on each servo cycle. It can therefore be used to measure cycle times, add time delays, etc. The TICKS parameter can be written to and read. As TICKS is a process parameter each process will have its own counter.
examples:
delay:
TICKS = 3000
OP(9, ON)
test:
IF TICKS <= 0 THEN OP(9, OFF) ELSE GOTO
test

name: TIMER
description: The TIMER command allows an output or a selection of outputs to be set or cleared for a predefined period of time. There are 64 timer slots available, each can be assigned to any outputs. The timer can be configured to turn the output ON or OFF.

WHILE MOTION_ERROR

WA(200) 'Waits 200 milliseconds to complete the 5 Hz
period
WEND
WHILE running
REGIST(3)
WAIT UNTIL MARK

WEND

MOVELINK(70, 100, 0, 60, 1)
WAIT LOADED 'Wait until tracking phase starts
TIMER(42, 140, 1, 250) 'Fire output during tracking phase
MOVELINK(-100, 200, 50, 50, 1)
WEND

name: TOKENTABLE
description: When entered in the command line in Direct Mode, the TOKENTABLE will output the complete list of Tokens or “key words” that make up the Trio language. The output format is as plain text with associated numerical and string tags. C144EXECUTE,0,0,1,3,0,0<cre> The command is used by a remote connection such as the TrioPC Motion ActiveX control, DLL etc. to download the token list for use within the remote application software.

C0TOKENTABLE,0,0,0,1,0,0<cbre>

C59FILE,0,0,1,3,0,0<cfre>
C60OPEN,0,0,0,0,0,0<cfre>
.....
Etc.

name: ULPDIFF
description: Returns the difference between two floating-point values in terms of units in the last place (ULP). A floating-point value can be viewed as being an integer (the significand, or mantissa) with a scaling (the exponent), so altering the least significant bit of the significand by one will create a floating-point value that is one unit in the last place (or unit of least precision) different, which is the smallest difference possible. This function provides a measure of how many units in the last place need to be added to one value to get to the other value, which is like a measure of how many values exist between the two supplied values (plus one). This can be useful when comparing floating-point values to determine how close they are to each other (how nearly equal they are). This concept is best illustrated by an example, so if we had a simplified fixed-point number system where the smallest fraction was one-tenth, then the ULP difference between 12.6 and 12.6 is 0, and between 12.6 and 12.7 is 1, and between 12.6 and 13.1 is 5, and so on. The number system used on Trio controllers is more complex than this simplified system, utilising the double precision IEEE 754 standard, which is a floating-point format, so an ULP difference of 1 between two values can be either exceptionally small in absolute terms when the exponent is small, or exceptionally large when the exponent is large, and this is because the ULP is part of the significand and so is scaled by the exponent.
params:
The number of floating-point values between the two
  parameters

name: UNITS
description: UNITS is a conversion factor that allows the user to scale the edges/ stepper pulses to a more convenient scale. The motion commands to set speeds, acceleration and moves use the UNITS scalar to allow values to be entered in more convenient units e.g.: mm for a move or mm/sec for a speed.
examples:
UNITS = 1000 * 4 / 5
UNITS AXIS(1) = 180 * 16
BASE(2)


UNITS = 90654.7200
UNITS AXIS(1) = 180 * 16

name: UNIT_CLEAR
description: Clears all the bits in the UNIT_ERROR system parameter. This command is typically used to reset a SYSTEM_ERROR raised by EtherCAT or to reset module errors from MC664 option modules. Motion Coordinators with an LCD display will show Unn when there is a UNIT_ERROR. UNIT_CLEAR can be used to reset the display.
examples:
UNIT_CLEAR
WA(10)
PRINT UNIT_ERROR[0]
IF SYSTEM_ERROR <> 0 THEN

  UNIT_CLEAR ' attempt to clear the errors

  WA(50)
IF SYSTEM_ERROR <> 0 THEN

    PRINT "Error bits cannot be cleared: ";HEX(SYSTEM_ERROR)

  ENDIF

ENDIF

UNIT_CLEAR
WA(10)
PRINT UNIT_ERROR[0]

IF SYSTEM_ERROR <> 0 THEN

  UNIT_CLEAR ' attempt to clear the errors

  WA(50)
IF SYSTEM_ERROR <> 0 THEN

    PRINT "Error bits cannot be cleared: ";HEX(SYSTEM_ERROR)

  ENDIF

ENDIF

name: UNIT_DISPLAY
description: Returns the pattern of LEDs on a module fitted to the MC664(X).

name: UNIT_ERROR
description: The UNIT_ERROR provides a simple single indicator that at least one module is in error and can indicate multiple modules that have an error. The value returns details which SLOT s are in error.
examples:
IF UNIT_ERROR=2 AND 
SYSTEM_ERROR=1048576 THEN
‘Handle Unit station error for
slot 1
...
ENDIF

name: UNIT_SW_VERSION
description: Returns a value that is the version number of the Software in a module fitted at the specified SLOT. This parameter can be used when connected to a MC664(X) to get the Software version of each module fitted to the Motion Coordinator. For FPGA information use FPGA_VERSION
examples:
>>PRINT UNIT_SW_VERSION SLOT(0)
0

name: UNLOCK
description: Unlocks a Motion Coordinator which has previously been locked using the LOCK command. To unlock the Motion Coordinator, the UNLOCK command should be entered using the same security code number which was used originally to LOCK it. You should use Motion Perfect to LOCK and UNLOCK your controller. If you forget the security code number which was used to lock the Motion Coordinator, it may have to be returned to your supplier to be unlocked.

name: USER_FRAME
description: The USER_FRAME allows the user to program in a different coordinate system. The USER_FRAME can be defined up to a 3-axis translation and rotation from the world coordinate origin. The rotations are applied using the Euler ZYX convention. This means that the z rotation is applied first, then the y is applied on the new coordinate system and finally the x is applied. The coordinate system is defined using the ‘right hand rule’ and the rotation of the origin is defined using the ‘right hand turn’. USER_FRAME requires the kinematic runtime FEC The default coordinate system has the identity 0 and is equal to the world coordinate system, this cannot be modified. If you wish to disable the USER_FRAME select USER_FRAME(0). USER_FRAMEs are applied on the axis FRAME_GROUP . If no FRAME_GROUP is defined then a runtime error will be generated. Movements are loaded with the selected USER_FRAME. This means that you can buffer a sequence of movements on different USER_FRAMES. The active USER_FRAME is the one associated with the movement in the MTYPE . If the FRAME_GROUP is IDLE then the active USER_FRAME is the selected USER_FRAME. The USER_FRAME is applied to all the axes in the FRAME_GROUP . This can be the same group as used by FRAME . The FRAME_GROUP does not have to be 3 axis, however the USER_FRAME will only process position for the axes in the FRAME_GROUP . It can be useful in a 2 axes FRAME_GROUP to perform a USER_FRAME rotation about the third axis. In certain applications involving different USER_FRAMEs, reverting back to USER_FRAME(0) between USER_FRAMEs is necessary. If you wish to check which USER_FRAME, TOOL_OFFSET and VOLUME_LIMIT are active you can print the details using FRAME_GROUP (group).
examples:
FRAME_GROUP(0,0,0,1,2)
USER_FRAME(1,0,0,0,PI/4)
FRAME_GROUP(0,0,0,1,2)
BASE(0,1,2)
DEFPOS(10,20,30)
USER_FRAME(1,10,20,30,PI/2)
USER_FRAME(0)
MOVEABS(100,100,50)
WAIT IDLE
USER_FRAME(1)
FORWARD
FRAME_GROUP(0,0,0,1,2)
BASE(0,1,2)
DEFPOS(10,20,30)
USER_FRAME(1,10,20,30,PI/2)
USER_FRAME(0)
MOVEABS(100,100,50)
WAIT IDLE
USER_FRAME(1)
FORWARD
WAIT UNTIL IN(2) = ON
RAPIDSTOP
USER_FRAME(0)
WAIT UNTIL IN(3)=ON
USER_FRAME(2,0,0,0,PI/4)
USER_FRAME(2)
FORWARD

FRAME_GROUP(0,0,0,1,2)
USER_FRAME(1,0,0,0,PI/4)
FRAME_GROUP(0,0,0,1,2)
BASE(0,1,2)
DEFPOS(10,20,30)
USER_FRAME(1,10,20,30,PI/2)
USER_FRAME(0)
MOVEABS(100,100,50)
WAIT IDLE
USER_FRAME(1)
FORWARD
FRAME_GROUP(0,0,0,1,2)
BASE(0,1,2)
DEFPOS(10,20,30)
USER_FRAME(1,10,20,30,PI/2)
USER_FRAME(0)
MOVEABS(100,100,50)
WAIT IDLE
USER_FRAME(1)
FORWARD
WAIT UNTIL IN(2) = ON
RAPIDSTOP
USER_FRAME(0)
WAIT UNTIL IN(3)=ON
USER_FRAME(2,0,0,0,PI/4)
USER_FRAME(2)
FORWARD

name: USER_FRAMEB
description: USER_FRAMEB is only used with SYNC . It defines the new USER_FRAME to resynchronise to when performing the SYNC (20) operation. When the resynchronisation is complete USER_FRAMEB is the active USER_FRAME . USER_FRAMEB selects one of the defined USER_FRAME s.
examples:
WHILE(running)
USER_FRAMEB(conv1)
REGIST(20,0,0,0,0) AXIS(10)
WAIT UNTIL MARK AXIS(10)
SYNC(1, 1000, REG_POS, 10, sen_xpos , conv1_yoff)
WAIT UNTIL SYNC_CONTROL AXIS(0)=3
'Now synchronised
GOSUB pick
USER_FRAMEB(conv2)
SYNC(20, 1000, place_pos, 11, conv2_xoff,
conv2_yoff)
WAIT UNTIL SYNC_CONTROL AXIS(0)=3
'Now synchronised
GOSUB place
SYNC(4, 500)
place_pos = place_pos + 100
WEND

name: USER_FRAME_TRANS
syntax:
USER_FRAME_TRANS(user_frame_in, user_frame_out, tool_offset_in,
tool_offset_out, table_in,  table_out, [scale])
description: This function enables you to transform a set of positions from one frame to another. This could be used to take a set of positions from a vision system and transform them so that they are a set of positions relative to a conveyor. USER_FRAME_TRANS requires the kinematic runtime FEC It is required to set-up a FRAME_GROUP and USER_FRAME to use this function. If you do not wish to set up a FRAME_GROUP with real axis you can use virtual. The USER_FRAME calculations are performed on raw position data which are integers. The table data is scaled by the scale parameter, for optimal resolution scale should be set to the UNITS of the robot. As all the USER_FRAME transformations use the same coordinate scale it does not matter if the positions are supplied as raw positions or scaled by UNITS .

name: USER_PID
description: The USER_PID is a PID function for general purpose use. The function mirrors the PID found in a typical IEC 61131-3 PLC programming system. The function uses global variables (VR) to pass input, output, and control values to the USER_PID. In Mode 1, the USER_PID runs one cycle each time it is called from a BASIC program. It is designed to be used in a program loop with a consistent, repeatable period. Increasing the value of TS reduces the update frequency of the PID output values. However, unless you add a WA delay in the loop, the controller will still call USER_PID continuously, which can waste CPU resources. For example, if you want a 10 ms update rate, setting TS = 10 or setting TS = 1 with WA(10) will both give almost the same output frequency. But the latter approach makes better use of processor resources. Please check SCHEDULE_TYPE for more details. In Mode 2, the USER_PID is called once only and runs in the Kernel at the SERVO_PERIOD rate. The execution frequency is tied to the servo period, while the update of the output values is governed by the TS input. In both Mode 1 and Mode 2, the TS input determines the scan time. The value of TS must always be greater than the controller’s configured servo period. If TS is set lower than the servo period, the instruction will still execute at the servo period rate.

name: -  Unary minus
description: Negates expression1.

name: VAL
description: Converts a string to a numerical value. If the string is not a numerical value, then VAL returns 0.
examples:
DIM str1 AS STRING(20)
str1 = "-123.456"
VR(100) = VAL(str1)
DIM str2 AS STRING(10)
DIM number AS INTEGER
str2 = "987"
number = INT(VAL(str2)) + 123

DIM str1 AS STRING(20)
str1 = "-123.456"
VR(100) = VAL(str1)

DIM str2 AS STRING(10)
DIM number AS INTEGER
str2 = "987"
number = INT(VAL(str2)) + 123

name: VALIDATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY
description: VALIDATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY is used to check that the controller has the correct user or OEM security code programmed. If the correct security code is not programmed then VALIDATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY will produce a runtime error (parameter out of range) and so stop the program from functioning. Motion Perfect has a tool to generate the validation keys Do not put the user or OEM security code in the program as these must be kept secret.
params:
A string which is a validation keys that has been
  generated by Motion Perfect
examples:
'Validate the user security code


VALIDATE_ENCRYPTION_KEY(2,"1Wgltam0wzrbCVJwUgEnGU")


RUN "MAIN_PROGRAM"

name: VECTOR_BUFFERED
description: This holds the total vector length of the buffered moves. It is effectively the amount the VPU can assume is available for deceleration. It should be executed with respect to the first axis in the group.
examples:
>>BASE(0,1,2)
>>? VECTOR_BUFFERED AXIS(0)
1245.0000
>>

name: VECTOR_MAG
description: The VECTOR_MAG function returns the magnitude of a vector defined by its components, which can number from 1 to 6. The vector’s magnitude is the square root of the sum of the squares of its components, so it is the same as the hypotenuse of a 2-D or 3-D triangle, hence its alternate function name of "HYPOT".
params:
The floating-point value of component ‘n’ of the
  vector.
examples:
>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(PI)
3.1416
>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(3, 4)
5.0000
>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(3, 4, 12)
13.0000
>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6)
9.5394

>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(PI)
3.1416

>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(3, 4)
5.0000

>>PRINT VECTOR_MAG(3, 4, 12)
13.0000

name: VERIFY
description: This parameter is included in the token list to allow older programs to compile in the MC4xx/MC5xx/MC6xx Motion Coordinators. It does not have any function, but the value will store the least significant bit of the value. On MC2xx and before, VERIFY was used to change the encoder port on a pulse count axis to count Quadrature encoder pulses. In the MC4/5/6 ranges, the same action is done by changing the axis type ( ATYPE ).

name: VERSION
description: Returns the version number of the firmware installed on the Motion Coordinator. You can use Motion Perfect to check the firmware version when looking at the controller configuration.

name: VFF_GAIN
description: Servo axes and remote speed (CSV) axes; The velocity feed forward gain is a constant which is multiplied by the change in demand position. Velocity feed forward gain can be used to decreases the following error during constant speed by increasing the output proportionally with the speed. For a velocity feed forward Kvff and change in position ΔΡd, the contribution to the output signal is: 0vff = Kvff x ΔΡd Remote position axes like EtherCAT CSP and RTEX position axes, the VFF_GAIN applies a target position (P t) advance that is proportional to DEMAND_SPEED. P tout = P t + ΔΡd x K vff
examples:
BASE(15)
VFF_GAIN=12

name: VIEW
description: Display source and object code data to the terminal. Optional string parameter can be used to view a specific program otherwise the currently selected program will be used, eg VIEW "STARTUP". The VIEW command will not show the memory contents if the controller is LOCK ed or the project programs are Encrypted.

name: VOLUME_LIMIT
description: VOLUME_LIMIT enables a software limit that restricts the motion into a defined three-dimensional shape. The calculations are performed on DPOS and so it can be used in addition to a FRAME . The limit applies to axes defined in a FRAME_GROUP . VOLUME_LIMIT requires the kinematic runtime FEC If no FRAME_GROUP is defined then a ‘parameter out of range’ run time error will be returned when VOLUME_LIMIT is called. All axes in the FRAME_GROUP must have the same UNITS When the limit is active moves on all axes in the FRAME_GROUP are cancelled and so will stop at FAST_DEC if specified, otherwise at DECEL . Any active SYNC is also stopped. AXISSTATUS bit 15 is also set. This means you should set your VOLUME_LIMIT smaller than the absolute operating limits of the robot.

name: VP_ACCEL
description: Returns the instantaneous acceleration that the velocity profiler is running at, which varies as moves are being profiled. The acceleration is the rate at which speed is increased or decreased.
examples:
MOVE(100)
WA(10)
WAIT UNTIL VP_ACCEL = 0

name: VP_DEMAND_ACCEL
description: Returns the velocity profiler’s demand acceleration.

name: VP_DEMAND_DECEL
description: Returns the velocity profiler’s demand deceleration.

name: VP_DEMAND_JERK
description: Returns the velocity profiler’s demand jerk.

name: VP_DEMAND_SPEED
description: Returns the velocity profiler’s demand speed.

name: VP_ERROR
description: The axis parameter VP_ERROR allows the error state of the VPU to be read. Only the VPU axis will have its VP_ERROR updated. Only valid for VP_MODE 6 – all other modes will only return 0.

name: VP_JERK
description: Returns the instantaneous jerk that the velocity profiler is running at, which varies as moves are being profiled. The jerk is the rate at which acceleration is increased or decreased.
examples:
MOVE(100)
WAIT UNTIL VP_JERK <> 0
WAIT UNTIL VP_JERK = 0

name: VP_MODE
description: Writing to this parameter allows the selection of a velocity profiling mode most suitable for your application. VP_MODE = 0 is the default setting for all Motion Coordinators. All velocity profiling modes allow the SPEED/ACCEL/DECEL (and JERK for modes 5 and 6) motion parameters to be changed while profiling non-SP move types. Velocity profiling modes 5 and 6 allow the SPEED/ACCEL/DECEL/JERK motion parameters to be changed while profiling SP move types. When an SP move becomes active, the motion parameters it was loaded with overwrite the axis parameters. Note that when a move that has had its motion parameters changed completes, the next move will use the motion parameters it was loaded with, so any changes may need to be reapplied for successive moves.

name: VP_MODE_PLC
description: Writing to this parameter allows the selection of the most suitable velocity profiling mode for your PLC (IEC 61131-3) application. The default value is 6 as this includes JERK and other axis parameters suitable for the PLCopen Motion Control (MC) function blocks. VP_MODE_PLC is an MC_CONFIG keyword in all Motion Coordinators that support PLCopen. VP_MODE 5 or 6 is required for PLCopen Motion function blocks with prefix “MC_”
examples: VP_MODE_PLC = 0

name: VP_OPTIONS
description: This parameter controls the behaviour of VP_MODE = 6, with the default value of zero intending to provide functionality suitable for most motion applications. If necessary, the behaviour can be tailored by setting bits that control various aspects, with bits 0 and 1 enabling VP_MODE = 6 to behave more like VPU_MODE = 5, and the remaining bits controlling aspects mainly related to processor usage and accuracy. Note that most applications use VP_MODE = 0 (the default VP_MODE), and so the default value of zero is normally adequate when migrating to VP_MODE = 6, but applications that use VP_MODE = 5 (usually PLC and RPS) may require adjustments to be made. Bit 0 controls whether VP_MODE = 6 has a minimum move buffer depth of 5, which is the default behaviour of VP_MODE = 5. Bit 1 controls whether VP_MODE = 6 binds the forced parameters with the axis parameters, i.e. FORCE_SPEED, FORCE_ACCEL, FORCE_DECEL and FORCE_JERK are bound to SPEED, ACCEL, DECEL and JERK respectively. When this bit is set, the axis parameters are overwritten when a move with forced parameters is loaded, and the axis parameters can subsequently be used to alter its motion, which is the default behaviour for VP_MODE = 5. When this bit is not set, the axis parameters are left untouched when a move that uses forced demands is loaded and the forced demands in place when the move was loaded are used throughout the move, which is the default behaviour of VP_MODE = 0. Note that when bit 1 is set, that the axis demands will reset to the forced demands each time one move ends and another begins, so any changes made to the axis parameters will be lost and therefore require reapplying if required. Bit 2 controls which VP_MODE is used when VP_MODE is set to 5. When unset, VP_MODE = 5 will be used, and when set, VP_MODE = 6 will be used instead. The sole purpose is to allow VP_MODE = 6 to be tested in an environment that specifies the use of VP_MODE = 5. Note that this setting only alters what velocity profiling calculations are performed, so bits 0 and 1 should also be set if full compatibility is required. Bit 3 controls whether the current look-ahead is invalidated when a demand factor, such as SPEED_FACTOR (including LOOKAHEAD_FACTOR), ACCEL_FACTOR or JERK_FACTOR change value. Bit 4 controls whether the blend point is fine-tuned to hit the blend speed exactly on the move junction. The default is to fine-tune once when it is time to start adjusting the velocity profile to hit the blend point, but setting this bit disables this fine-tuning, which is less processor intensive. Bit 5 controls whether fine-tuning is done once, which is the default, or is performed continuously until the blend point is reached. Continuous re-evaluation of the demands to hit the blend point accurately may be slightly more accurate (than doing it only once), but is more processor intensive.

name: VP_POSITION
description: Reserved keyword

name: VP_SPEED
description: Returns the instantaneous speed that the velocity profiler is running at, which varies as moves are being profiled.
examples:
MOVE(100)
WAIT UNTIL SPEED = VP_SPEED

name: VR
syntax:
value = VR(index)


array = VR(first index, last index)


VR(index) = value
description: Recall or assign a single global floating-point value by index, starting from 0. Alternatively a range of floating-point values can be read as an array (1D) of floating-point values that could be copied to another area of VR/TABLE memory or a locally defined array. The number of elements in VR varies across controllers and can be determined using the VRSIZE keyword, so the valid index range is from 0 to VRSIZE - 1. VR can be used as an array or partitioned as several arrays. VR can also be used to hold ASCII representations of STRINGS and can be assigned with a string value. To read the string value back you must use VRSTRING . The numbered variables are globally shared between programs and can be used for communication between programs, so care is needed when multiple programs manipulate the same VR. All VR are stored as 64-bit floating-point values, encoded following the IEEE-754 standard (53 significant bits, 1 sign bit and 11 exponent bits). As such, integers that require more than 53 bits will incur precision loss during the conversion to floating-point.
params: The value written to or read from the VR
The index of a single VR to read or write

1D array of values read from a consecutive range of VR
  indices
Range of VR indices to read from as a 1D array
examples:
val = 15
VR(val) = 1.2555
movep:
MOVEABS(115) 'Move to first put down position:
FOR VR(0) = 101 TO 110
IF VR(VR(0)) = 0 THEN
GOSUB load
ENDIF
MOVE(200)  '200 is spacing between positions
NEXT VR(0)
PRINT "All Positions Are Full"
WAIT UNTIL IN(3) = ON
GOTO movep
load:
'Put load in position and mark array
OP(15, OFF)
VR(VR(0)) = 1
VR(65) = VR(0) * MPOS AXIS(1)
PRINT VR(65)
VR(10) = "Hello World"
PRINT VR(10)       '72 (ASCII for H)
PRINT CHR(VR(10))  'H
PRINT VRSTRING(10) 'Hello World
DIM myints as INTEGER(5,20)
myints(2) = VR(1000,1019) ‘Row 2 will be populated with
VRs
PRINT myints
VR(1000) = VR(2000,2499) ‘Copy VRs 2000..2499 to 1000
TABLE(0,VR(100,199)) ‘Copy VRs 100..199 to TABLE address 0

val = 15
VR(val) = 1.2555

movep:
MOVEABS(115) 'Move to first put down position:
FOR VR(0) = 101 TO 110
IF VR(VR(0)) = 0 THEN
GOSUB load
ENDIF
MOVE(200)  '200 is spacing between positions
NEXT VR(0)
PRINT "All Positions Are Full"
WAIT UNTIL IN(3) = ON
GOTO movep
load:
'Put load in position and mark array
OP(15, OFF)
VR(VR(0)) = 1

VR(65) = VR(0) * MPOS AXIS(1)
PRINT VR(65)

VR(10) = "Hello World"
PRINT VR(10)       '72 (ASCII for H)
PRINT CHR(VR(10))  'H
PRINT VRSTRING(10) 'Hello World

DIM myints as INTEGER(5,20)
myints(2) = VR(1000,1019) ‘Row 2 will be populated with
VRs
PRINT myints

VR(1000) = VR(2000,2499) ‘Copy VRs 2000..2499 to 1000
TABLE(0,VR(100,199)) ‘Copy VRs 100..199 to TABLE address 0

name: VRSIZE
description: Returns the size of the VR array, which varies across controller types. The VR array is indexed from zero, so the valid range of indices is 0 to VRSIZE - 1.
examples:
>>VR(VRSIZE – 1) = 123
>>

name: VRSTRING
description: Combines the contents of an array of VR variables so that they can be printed as a text string or used as part of a STRING variable. All printable characters will be output, and the string will terminate at the first null character found (i.e., VR (n) contains 0).
params: The string value read from VR

Index of first character in the 
VR

  array
examples:
PRINT #5, VRSTRING(100)
string2 = VRSTRING(0)
PRINT #5, VRSTRING(100)
string2 = VRSTRING(0)

name: V_LIMIT
description: This parameter sets a speed limit for an axis such that the vector path speed is reduced as required to ensure that the speed of each axis with a limit set does not exceed its limit. When used with the MOVECIRC and MOVECIRCSP commands, the path speed is constant around the circumference, the rate being determined from the lowest V_LIMIT for each axis in the move. This parameter is supported by VP_MODE = 0 and VP_MODE = 6, but not by VP_MODE = 5.
examples:
DIM x AS INTEGER = 0
DIM y AS INTEGER = 1
V_LIMIT AXIS(x) = 40
V_LIMIT AXIS(y) = 80
BASE(x, y)
SPEED = 200
SERVO = ON
MOVE(0, 500) 'This moves along the y-axis, so moves at 80
mm/s
WA(100)
MOVE(500, 0) 'This moves along the x-axis, so moves at 40
mm/s
WA(10)
MOVE(500, 500) 'This moves at 45 degrees so 
VP_SPEED
 is 56.5685 mm/s

name: WA
description: Holds up program execution for the number of milliseconds specified in the parameter.
params: The number of milliseconds to wait for.
examples:
OP(11, OFF)
WA(2000)
OP(17, ON)

name: WAIT
syntax:
WAIT UNTIL <expression>


WAIT IDLE [AXIS(<axis-number>)]


WAIT IN_POS [AXIS(<axis-number>)]


WAIT LOADED [AXIS(<axis-number>)]
description: Suspends program execution until the specified condition is satisfied. The most generic form of this statement is WAIT UNTIL <expression>, as any valid numeric expression may be used. However, there are some shorthand forms used for common tasks, like WAIT IDLE, WAIT IN_POS and WAIT LOADED. It is very common to use only WAIT IDLE and WAIT LOADED, and in this situation the UNTIL is optional. However, when IDLE and LOADED are part of a more complex expression, the UNTIL is required. Note that the shorthand forms used to behave differently to the generic form using UNTIL, as the shorthand form would put the process to sleep until the condition was met, whereas using WAIT UNTIL would constantly poll the axis, which is less efficient. This is no longer the case, and both variants now put the process to sleep until the axis has become IDLE/IN_POS/LOADED.
examples:
WAIT UNTIL MPOS AXIS(0) > 150


MOVE(100) AXIS(7)
MOVELINK(distance, link_dist, acceldist, deceldist,
linkaxis)
WAIT UNTIL IDLE OR IN(12) = ON
MOVE(100)


WAIT UNTIL IDLE


PRINT "Move Done"
MOVE(100)


WAIT UNTIL IN_POS


PRINT "Move in position"
MOVE(100)


MOVE(350)


WAIT UNTIL LOADED


OP(45, ON)


MOVE(200)


WAIT UNTIL LOADED


OP(45, OFF)
MOVE(100)


WAIT IDLE


PRINT "Move Done"
MOVE(100)


WAIT IN_POS


PRINT "Move in position"
MOVE(100)


MOVE(350)


WAIT LOADED


OP(45, ON)


MOVE(200)


WAIT LOADED


OP(45, OFF)

WAIT UNTIL MPOS AXIS(0) > 150


MOVE(100) AXIS(7)

MOVELINK(distance, link_dist, acceldist, deceldist,
linkaxis)
WAIT UNTIL IDLE OR IN(12) = ON

MOVE(100)


WAIT UNTIL IDLE


PRINT "Move Done"

MOVE(100)


WAIT UNTIL IN_POS


PRINT "Move in position"

MOVE(100)


MOVE(350)


WAIT UNTIL LOADED


OP(45, ON)


MOVE(200)


WAIT UNTIL LOADED


OP(45, OFF)

MOVE(100)


WAIT IDLE


PRINT "Move Done"

MOVE(100)


WAIT IN_POS


PRINT "Move in position"

MOVE(100)


MOVE(350)


WAIT LOADED


OP(45, ON)


MOVE(200)


WAIT LOADED


OP(45, OFF)

name: WDOG
description: Controls the WDOG relay contact used for enabling external drives. The WDOG= ON command MUST be issued in a program prior to executing moves. It may then be switched ON and OFF under program control. If however a following error condition exists on any axis the system software will override the WDOG setting and turn watchdog contact OFF. When WDOG= OFF , the relay is opened, the analogue outputs are set to 0V, the step/direction outputs and any digital axis enable functions are disabled.
examples: WDOG=ON

name: WHILE .. WEND
description: The commands contained in the WHILE..WEND loop are continuously executed while the condition is TRUE . Execution then continues after the WEND. If the condition is FALSE when the WHILE is first executed then the loop will be skipped.
params: TrioBASIC statements that you wish to execute
examples:
WHILE IN(12) = OFF
MOVE(200)
WAIT IDLE
OP(10, OFF)
MOVE(-200)
WAIT IDLE
OP(10, ON)
WEND

name: WORLD_ACCEL
description: Reserved Keyword

name: WORLD_DECEL
description: Reserved Keyword

name: WORLD_DPOS
description: The WORLD_DPOS is the demand position in the FRAME coordinate system. It sits between the DPOS and AXIS_DPOS . With no USER_FRAME or TOOL_OFFSET, WORLD_DPOS is equal to DPOS . With no FRAME , WORLD_DPOS is equal to AXIS_DPOS . For some machinery configurations it can be useful to install a frame transformation which is not 1:1, these are typically machines such as robotic arms or machines with parasitic motions on the axes. In this situation when FRAME is not zero WORLD_DPOS returns the demand position for the programming point of the FRAME . WORLD_DPOS can be scaled by UNITS
examples:
>>PRINT WORLD_DPOS AXIS(10)
5432
>>

name: WORLD_FS_LIMIT
description: An end of travel limit may be set up in software thus allowing the program control of the working range of a robot axis. This parameter holds the absolute position of the world forward travel limit in user WORLD_UNITS . Bit 0 of the ROBOTSTATUS register is set when the axis position is greater than the WORLD_FS_LIMIT. World software limits are only enabled when FRAME <> 0 so that the user can limit the range of motion of the robot. When WORLD_DPOS reaches WORLD_FS_LIMIT the controller will CANCEL all moves on the FRAME_GROUP , the axis will decelerate at FASTDEC if specified, otherwise at DECEL or. Any SYNC is also stopped. As this software limit uses WORLD_DPOS it will require a negative change in WORLD_DPOS to move off the limit. This might not be a negative movement on DPOS due to the selected FRAME transformation.. WORLD_FS_LIMIT is disabled when it has a value greater than REP_DIST or when FRAME = 0.
examples:
WORLD_UNITS = FRAME_SCALE
WORLD_FS_LIMIT = 500
WORLD_RS_LIMIT = -200

name: WORLD_JOGSPEED
description: Reserved Keyword

name: WORLD_RS_LIMIT
description: An end of travel limit may be set up in software thus allowing the program control of the working range of a robot axis. This parameter holds the absolute position of the world forward travel limit in user WORLD_UNITS . Bit 1 of the ROBOTSTATUS register is set when the axis position is greater than the WORLD_RS_LIMIT. World software limits are only enabled when FRAME<>0 so that the user can limit the range of motion of the robot. When WORLD_DPOS reaches WORLD_RS_LIMIT the controller will CANCEL all moves on the FRAME_GROUP , the axis will decelerate at FASTDEC if specified, otherwise at DECEL or. Any SYNC is also stopped. As this software limit uses WORLD_DPOS it will require a negative change in WORLD_DPOS to move off the limit. This might not be a negative movement on DPOS due to the selected FRAME transformation.. WORLD_RS_LIMIT is disabled when it has a value greater than REP_DIST or when FRAME =0.
examples:
WORLD_UNITS = FRAME_SCALE
WORLD_FS_LIMIT = 500
WORLD_RS_LIMIT = -200

name: WORLD_SPEED
description: Reserved Keyword

name: WORLD_UNITS
description: WORLD_UNITS is a conversion factor that allows the user to scale the edges/ stepper pulses to a more convenient scale. WORLD_UNITS is only used when a FRAME is active and only applies to the parameters in the axis coordinate system (after the FRAME ). This includes WORLD_DPOS , WORLD_FS_LIMIT and WORLD_RS_LIMIT .

name: XOR
description: This performs and exclusive or function between corresponding bits of the integer part of two valid TrioBASIC expressions. It may therefore be used as either a bitwise or logical condition. The XOR function between two values is defined as follows: XOR 0 1 0 0 1 1 1 0

name: ZIP_READ
description: This function will read a compressed file into RAM on the Motion Coordinator and then decompress it in blocks. The file must be transferred to the SD card on the Motion Coordinator using the TextFileLoader (executable or ActiveX) with compression enabled and decompression disabled, that way the file will be stored in compressed format. Internally we handle two buffer areas: compressed buffer and decompressed buffer. The compressed buffer is filled from the file, the decompressed buffer is filled from the compressed buffer. The data is transferred between the buffers when required.

name: << Shift left
description: The shift left operator, <<, can be used to logically shift left the bits in an integer variable. The value resulting from expression 1 will be shifted left by the count in expression 2. As the bits are shifted, a 0 will be inserted in the right-most bits of the value.

name: >> Shift Right
description: The shift right operator, >>, can be used to logically shift right the bits in an integer variable. The value resulting from expression 1 will be shifted right by the count in expression 2. As the bits are shifted, a 0 will be inserted in the left-most bits of the value.
examples:
result = AXISSTATUS >> 4
in_fwd_limit = result AND 1
